\frontmatter
\chapter*{Abstract}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Abstract}

A single assumption is granted: it is possible to separate two outcomes. Nothing else. No carrier, no arithmetic, no geometry, no measurement scale. The remainder of this volume traces, in fully mechanised Martin-L√∂f type theory under \AgdaFlag{safe} and \AgdaFlag{without-K}, what is forced by that grant alone and what is annihilated by it.

The entry into the development is itself a uniqueness theorem, not a choice. Any type-theoretic structure that carries two distinguishable, exhaustive points is shown to be boundary-preservingly isomorphic to the canonical two-point base. The two-point base is therefore not an option among many; it is the unique normal form for binary distinction inside the formal setting. The orientation classification that follows determines the only residual ambiguity in the isomorphism‚ÄĒa global swap of the two boundary roles‚ÄĒand exhausts it.

From this entry, the eliminative chain proceeds without addition. The endomorphism space of the base type contains exactly four inhabitants up to pointwise equality. Their composition closure on three vertices fails by absurd-pattern elimination; on more than four vertices it adds structure unsupported by the four cases. The closure that survives is the complete graph on four vertices, $K_4$. Its representation record is inhabited, propositionally unique, and stable under the orbit tests carried out in Part~VI.

The numerical invariants attached to that survivor are determined by reduction. The Euler characteristic evaluates to $2$, the simplex expression $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$ at the forced values $V=4$, $d=3$, $\chi=2$ evaluates to $137$, and the loop numerator $E + d + \chi$ evaluates to $11$ after eight subset candidates are eliminated by absurd patterns. The witness in every case is \texttt{refl}; no parameter is free.

The standard number systems $\mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{R}$ are then constructed inside the same formal environment. Their algebraic laws are proved, not posited. The sequential completeness of the present construction of $\mathbb{R}$‚ÄĒevery Cauchy sequence of reals admits a limit, built from the row-internal stabilisation indices‚ÄĒcloses the analytic layer without invoking quotient types or choice.

The chain returns to its origin. The final theorem of the book establishes that any admissible invariant record on the surviving structure already presupposes the original separation witness. Distinction enters as the minimal grant; it re-emerges as a structural consequence of the closed record. The argument is therefore not an open arrow from premise to conclusion but a closed loop that admits no alternative endpoint.

The complete derivation lives inside twenty-five thousand lines of literate Agda under \AgdaFlag{safe} and \AgdaFlag{without-K}, with no postulates and no library imports. The text and its verifier are the same file.

\section*{Inheritance}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Inheritance}

None of the ideas in this work are new. The derivation is a single thread; what follows names the hands that spun it.

The starting point‚ÄĒthat a distinction, not a thing, is the irreducible atom of form‚ÄĒbelongs to George Spencer-Brown. The conviction that this is not metaphor but structure‚ÄĒthat form reduces to yes-or-no at every point‚ÄĒis the wager this formalization accepts.

That such a claim can be made rigorous at all is owed to the constructivists. Brouwer insisted that existence demands a witness. Heyting gave that demand algebraic form. Curry and Howard revealed that proofs and programs are the same act. Per Martin-L√∂f forged these insights into a type theory strong enough to carry mathematics, and Voevodsky showed what must be given up‚ÄĒaxiom K, uniqueness of identity proofs‚ÄĒif homotopy is to be taken seriously. Every line of this work is executed inside that disciplined formal setting, while the surviving structure is judged by the constraints it satisfies, not by a preference for the setting itself.

The combinatorial backbone has older roots. Euler first counted vertices, edges, and faces as a single invariant. Cayley classified graphs by their symmetries. Birkhoff established when a representation is canonical‚ÄĒwhen structure admits exactly one normal form. These are the tools through which the filtration closes: under the stated constraints, $K_4$, and only $K_4$, survives.

That the spectral analysis of the survivor is determined by these tools is owed to the tradition built by Fourier, Laplace, and Hilbert. In this work, the Laplacian of $K_4$ yields a spectrum---$\{0,4,4,4\}$---that was not chosen but inherited.

What determines which quantities are invariant is the automorphism group, and its theory has its own lineage. Galois revealed that solvability is a property of the group, not the equation. Here, the automorphism group $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$ is the arbiter: only quantities invariant under it factor through the canonical base.

The categorical perspective‚ÄĒthat structure is characterized not by what it \emph{is} but by the maps it \emph{admits}‚ÄĒdescends from Lawvere and the tradition of Tarski, where fixpoints are not found but forced by the architecture of the logic itself.

What is new here is not the question but the execution. The Agda type checker enforces the declared formal constraints without exception, and those constraints are applied to a question these traditions raised from different directions. If the result has weight, it has it because the filter is explicit and mechanically checked.

\mainmatter

\chapter{Introduction}
\label{ch:introduction}

\section{Three Questions, One Filter}
\label{sec:introduction:three-questions-one-filter}

The void is the unmarked state. This observation‚ÄĒthat a system capable of examining itself already distinguishes examination from non-examination‚ÄĒhas appeared independently in logic and in the foundations of mathematics. What has not been carried through in full mechanical detail is the following question: if one grants only the minimal distinction required for a formal system not to collapse into vacuity, which structure remains after the unstable alternatives are eliminated? This book derives that answer.

Two disciplines have arrived at this boundary independently.

Mathematics asks: which structures are consistent?
Logic asks: which statements can be decided at all?

Different languages, different traditions‚ÄĒand yet, when pushed far enough, both converge on the same mechanism: an internal filter that neither chose. Mathematics eliminates structures that contradict themselves. Logic eliminates propositions that cannot be assigned a truth value without inconsistency.

What cannot stand under its own requirements, falls. The stages differ. The verdict is identical.

If the filter is the same everywhere, could the survivor also be the same?

\section{Elimination as Method}
\label{sec:introduction:elimination-as-method}

The standard approach in formal work is constructive: posit axioms, define structures, prove theorems, build upward. The direction is from assumption to consequence.

This work begins from the opposite end‚ÄĒthe void.

The starting point is reduced to the minimum required for the question to be non-vacuous: a distinction must be admissible. Beyond that, no additional structure is granted in advance. A relentless filter is then applied. At every stage, the question is the same: which structures survive their own consistency requirements under these minimal conditions? Those that do not are annihilated. Those that do, persist because the eliminative pressure does not remove them.

This is not a new idea. It is how logic has always worked. What has not been traced in full is the consequence of applying this filter mechanically, from the ground up, without exception. This book traces that consequence.

The filter produces something disconcerting: the more rigorously the initial grant is reduced, the more specific the surviving structure becomes. This is the inverse of what ordinary formal work suggests---fewer axioms should give more models, not fewer. What the derivation shows is that under these minimal conditions the eliminative chain contracts to a single surviving graph, a single invariant record, and a single arithmetic spine.

\section{The Tool: Agda as Executioner}
\label{sec:introduction:agda-as-executioner}

A filter is only as trustworthy as its enforcement. Human reasoning is fallible: it overlooks edge cases, confuses sufficiency with necessity, and mistakes intuition for proof. This is why every step in this work is formalized and verified in Agda, a dependently typed proof assistant.

Agda does not care about elegance or plausibility. It checks one thing: does this term have the type it claims to have? If yes, the step stands. If no, it is rejected. There is no override, no escape hatch.

The compiler flags used in this work‚ÄĒ\AgdaFlag{safe} and \AgdaFlag{without-K}‚ÄĒrestrict the formal environment further. No postulates can be introduced, and no appeal to the uniqueness of identity proofs is permitted. These flags do not create the surviving structure; they ensure that the execution cannot smuggle in extra freedom while the eliminative argument is carried out.

\section{What Happens Here}
\label{sec:introduction:what-happens-here}

The global line of force has a single irreversible spine:

\[
\begin{aligned}
  & D_0
  \longrightarrow
  \text{minimal logical infrastructure}
  \longrightarrow
  \text{classification}
  \longrightarrow
  \text{drift and reachability} \\
  & \longrightarrow
  \text{presentation and fixpoint}
  \longrightarrow
  K_4
  \longrightarrow
  \text{arithmetic and spectral invariants} \\
  & \longrightarrow
  \text{invariant record}
  \longrightarrow
  D_0.
\end{aligned}
\]

The spine does not branch‚ÄĒand it does not dead-end. The book's final theorem closes the loop: any admissible invariant record already presupposes $D_0$. The chain is not an open arrow; it is a closed constraint.

\subsection{The Elimination Protocol}
\label{sec:introduction:elimination-protocol}

The eliminative core of the formalisation can be summarised by the following sequence. At every step, the column on the right names the alternatives that were tested and formally shown to imply a contradiction ($\bot$). Under the minimal conditions fixed above, $K_4$ is the unique structure that remains. No entry in this list is added as an extra postulate; each surviving step is forced by the failure of the listed alternatives.

\small
\setlength{\LTpre}{0pt}
\setlength{\LTpost}{0pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
\begin{longtable}{@{}r l p{5.2cm} p{5.2cm}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{\#} & \textbf{Survivor} & \textbf{Constraint} & \textbf{Eliminated Alternatives} \\
\midrule
\endfirsthead
\toprule
\textbf{\#} & \textbf{Survivor} & \textbf{Constraint} & \textbf{Eliminated Alternatives} \\
\midrule
\endhead
\bottomrule
\endfoot
\bottomrule
\endlastfoot
1 & Distinction ($D_0$)
  & The minimal requirement to separate logical states ($\ell \neq r$). Any formal system unable to distinguish true from false is vacuous.
  & Vacuous carriers, carriers without boundary witnesses, carriers where $\ell = r$. \\
2 & Two-class coverage
  & Every element of the carrier equals $\ell$ or $r$. No unclassified residue is possible.
  & Carriers with hidden elements, partial classifications, carriers with $>2$ equivalence classes. \\
3 & Four endomorphisms
  & Coverage forces the boundary-output table $2 \times 2 = 4$. The four resulting cases are then proved mutually distinct.
  & $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$ (which fail to host exactly 4 distinct, symmetric maps). \\
4 & Drift acyclicity
  & Applying classification to classification produces a level tower. The reachability relation on that tower is proved well-founded.
  & Cyclic level structures, infinite descending chains, non-well-founded hierarchies. \\
5 & Presentation fixpoint
  & Every reachability witness reduces to a canonical finite form. The observable that measures proofs factors through a unique base.
  & Non-canonical representations, observables that depend on proof-internal choices. \\
6 & $K_4$
  & The complete graph on four vertices is the unique fixpoint of the filter. At this point the absolute statement is justified: no proper subgraph survives all five constraints above.
  & Every proper subgraph of $K_4$, every supergraph, every non-complete topology on 4 vertices. \\
7 & Derived arithmetic
  & $K_4$ formally induces counting (naturals), orientation (integers), spectral ratios (rationals), and limit closure (reals).
  & Number systems not grounded in $K_4$ structure, algebraic laws contradicting spectral data. \\
8 & Invariant record
  & The combinatorial invariants of $K_4$ ($V=4$, $E=6$, $d=3$, $\chi=2$, $\mathrm{Spec}=\{0,4,4,4\}$) evaluate to exact integers without free parameters.
  & Any alternative assignment requiring a different graph or a tunable parameter. \\
\end{longtable}
\normalsize

Uniqueness is not merely asserted; it is proven. Part~IV establishes $K_4$ as the sole fixpoint, and Chapter~\ref{ch:k4-representation-theory} proves that any two admissible invariant records over this structure must be propositionally equal.

\section{Structure of the Text}

The argument unfolds in six parts. The first four narrow the field until $K_4$ remains. The fifth develops the arithmetic attached to that survivor. The sixth gathers the resulting invariants into a final record.

\textbf{Part I (Foundation)} sets out the logical apparatus used throughout: equality, coproducts, negation, decidability, and the natural numbers. Nothing needed by the later uniqueness arguments is left in the background, so those arguments rest on explicit definitions rather than tacit convention.

\textbf{Part II (Drift and Reachability)} studies what happens when classification is iterated. The unclassified residue of each stage compels passage to the next, and the result is a well-founded tower whose shape can be analysed precisely.

\textbf{Part III (Heteromorphisms and Presentation)} turns from endomorphisms to maps between distinct carriers. Its presentation theorems reduce reachability witnesses to canonical representatives and thereby supply the uniqueness statements on which the later invariant record depends.

\textbf{Part IV (Observables and Fixpoint)} asks which quantities may be read off from proofs without depending on accidents of presentation. The answer leads to a canonical observable base and to the proof that $K_4$ alone survives the full sequence of constraints.

\textbf{Part V (Derived Arithmetic)} develops the number systems that emerge from the structure of $K_4$. Counting yields naturals, orientation yields integers, spectral ratios yield rationals, and completion yields reals; the familiar algebraic laws are then proved within the formal development.

\textbf{Part VI (Derived Constants of $K_4$)} assembles the graph's combinatorial data into a verified computation record and closes the loop by showing that every admissible measurement structure already presupposes the initial distinction $D_0$.

Every claim in the book is machine-checked. Because the manuscript is written in literate Agda, the exposition and the verified development remain aligned line by line.

\section{Methodological Defense against Numerology}
\label{sec:introduction:not-numerology}

Highly specific integers in a mathematical derivation invite an obvious suspicion: perhaps the construction was tuned so that a chosen number would appear.

That suspicion would be justified if the formal system admitted free parameters, adjustable axioms, or arbitrary normalisations. The present derivation does not. At no point does the Agda formalisation introduce a coefficient or convention whose sole role is to steer the computation toward a preferred value.

The elimination protocol (¬ß\ref{sec:introduction:elimination-protocol}) makes this rigidity precise. Each stage removes alternatives by explicit proof. There is therefore no second graph satisfying the same constraints, no alternative spectral package, and no competing arithmetic evaluation produced by a different internal choice.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Distinction.} For the formal work not to collapse into vacuity, some distinction must be admissible. Denying that possibility leaves no carrier, no types, and no terms on which the eliminative argument could act.

\item \textbf{Two-class coverage.} The alternative---a carrier with elements outside $\{\ell, r\}$---is contradicted by the coverage proof. The type checker enforces this.

\item \textbf{Four endomorphisms.} The count $2 \times 2 = 4$ is arithmetic forced by the carrier size. The claim that five or three endomorphisms could survive is refuted by exhaustive case analysis in the code.

\item \textbf{$K_4$ over any smaller graph.} $K_3$ fails because four mutually distinct vertices cannot close on three nodes. $K_2$ and $K_1$ fail earlier. The proofs are in Chapter~\ref{ch:elimination-classification}.

\item \textbf{Drift and reachability.} Once classification is applied to classification itself, passage to a level tower is forced. Acyclicity then appears as a theorem of that tower, not as an added design principle.

\item \textbf{Presentation fixpoint.} The collapse of observables to a unique canonical form is proven. The alternative---multiple incompatible canonical forms---would violate the uniqueness of the factoring proven in Part~IV.

\item \textbf{Arithmetic.} Integers, rationals, and reals are derived from $K_4$ structure. The axioms of each number system are theorems, not postulates.

\item \textbf{$\mathcal{C} = 137$.} The values $V=4$, $E=6$, $d=3$, $\chi=2$ are determined by the elimination chain and not adjustable. The form of the expression $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$ is fixed by the kind classification of \hyperref[ch:invariant-kinds]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:invariant-kinds}}: $V$ and $\chi$ are factors of disjoint kinds sharing one underlying carrier and combine multiplicatively; $d^2$ is a factor of a kind disjoint from both and combines additively. Evaluating the expression on the forced values yields $4^3 \cdot 2 + 3^2 = 128 + 9 = 137$, witnessed by \texttt{refl}. The aggregation is not introduced as a definition independent of the structure: \S\ref{sec:simplex-eval-status} states the closure of the four layers --- values, form, meaning, exhaustion --- in full.
\end{enumerate}

Numerology, by contrast, has a signature: the construction is chosen \emph{after} the target is known, and the degrees of freedom are large enough that hitting the target is unsurprising. The test is simple. If the values $V$, $E$, $d$, $\chi$ could be adjusted to hit a different target, the result would be numerological. They cannot: each of the four is forced by the elimination chain and machine-checked. If the form combining them could be exchanged for another, the result would be numerological. It cannot: the kind classification eliminates every alternative form of binary connector between these four invariants.

The claim here is therefore the rigid one. The four invariants are forced; the form of their evaluating expression is forced; the integer $137$ is the value of the unique well-typed scalar invariant of the closed record. Inside the monomial pool of degree $\leq 4$ generated by the same invariants the integer $137$ admits a single pair-sum decomposition, and that decomposition is exactly the kind-respecting one. \S\ref{sec:simplex-eval-status} states this in full.

\section{Two Volumes, One Boundary}
\label{sec:introduction:two-paths}

A companion volume, \textit{Form}, does not re-derive $K_4$. It imports the present development by the literal line \texttt{open import Void} and treats the exported invariant ledger as settled mathematical input. The asymmetry is deliberate: this volume proves; the companion volume identifies.

The division of labour is strict. \textit{Void} carries the eliminative work: distinction, classification, reachability, presentation collapse, arithmetic, the invariant record, and the closure back to distinction. \textit{Form} begins only after this chain has closed. It reads the forced quantities $(V,E,d,\chi,\kappa)$, the value $\mathcal{C} = 137$, the loop numerator $11$, and the remaining exported constants as candidates for physical observables, and it states openly what would falsify each identification.

The two books therefore do not offer two independent formal derivations of the same object. They offer one formal derivation and one interpretive projection. That asymmetry is not a weakness but a methodological guardrail. If physical vocabulary entered the eliminative chain itself, the derivation would invite the numerology objection this book is designed to block. By forcing the mathematical survivor in \textit{Void} first and permitting physical names only in \textit{Form}, the boundary remains visible.

The coupling between the two volumes is mechanical. If \textit{Void} changes, \textit{Form} must be rechecked against the new exported interface; if an expected theorem or invariant disappears, the import layer breaks. Yet the logical direction is one-way: \textit{Form} depends on \textit{Void}, while \textit{Void} stands on its own.

The reader who has both volumes can therefore read them in sequence: theorem first, interpretation second. The reader who has only this one loses nothing essential, because the eliminative derivation is self-contained.

\section{What This Book Does Not Contain}
\label{sec:introduction:physics-free}

This volume contains no physics. It names no particle, invokes no gauge group, fits no coupling constant, and introduces no empirical unit of measurement---metre, second, kilogram, or electronvolt. The number $137$ appears here as a theorem of $K_4$ combinatorics, not as an approximation to an observed constant. The loop numerator $11$ arises from a $\gcd$ filter on $\{E, d, \chi\}$-subset sums, not from any physical model.

This separation is not a matter of taste. If the derivation of $K_4$ were mixed from the outset with interpretive labels---if ``proton'' appeared where ``degree-shifted monomial'' is all the proof requires, or ``electroweak'' where ``$\kappa^2$-scaled'' is exact---then the reader could reasonably suspect that the formal chain had been steered toward familiar targets. Its credibility depends on avoiding that mixture.

Every quantity computed in this volume is an invariant of $K_4$ under $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$. Whether any of these invariants correspond to measured quantities is a separate hypothesis, addressed only in the companion volume \textit{Form}.

The distinction between mathematical theorem and physical interpretation is therefore maintained throughout. Everything derived in this volume is a formal topological or combinatorial invariant of $K_4$, and nothing more is claimed here.

\part{Foundation}

The formal development begins by fixing the minimal logical infrastructure on which the later combinatorial and arithmetic arguments depend. Equality, pairing, negation, and the natural numbers are often treated as background. Here they are introduced explicitly so that later uniqueness claims rest on a fully visible formal basis.

This choice is methodological rather than polemical. Before any graph-theoretic or spectral argument can be evaluated, the ambient logic has to be specified carefully enough that no hidden axiom or informal convention can influence the outcome.

\chapter{Minimal Logical Infrastructure}
\label{ch:foundation-necessity}

The first task is to specify the formal environment itself. In Agda, constructive work depends not only on the definitions one writes but also on the background principles permitted by the language.

The flag \AgdaFlag{safe} excludes postulates and ensures that no external axiom is introduced silently. The flag \AgdaFlag{without-K} avoids the additional identification of all equality proofs and keeps the treatment of identity compatible with standard homotopy-theoretic practice. Universe levels provide the usual stratification that prevents impredicative self-reference.

None of this is peculiar to the present text. These are standard structural restrictions, recorded here because the later arguments use them repeatedly and because the opening code block is easiest to read once its ambient assumptions have been stated explicitly.

\begin{code}[hide]
{-# OPTIONS --safe --without-K #-}
\end{code}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Strict Environment Constraint: No axioms, no postulates, no Axiom K.
module Void where

-- ¬ß Stratification primitives prevent self-reference paradoxes.
open import Agda.Primitive using (Level; lzero; lsuc; _‚äĒ_; SetŌČ)
\end{code}

\section{Propositional Equality}
\label{sec:foundation:propositional-equality}

A formal development needs an internal notion of equality before it can state substitution, transport, or congruence. In the Martin-L√∂f type theory used here for the formal execution, this role is played by the identity type.

The definition used here is the standard one: an inductive family generated by reflexivity. From that single constructor one obtains symmetry, transitivity, congruence, and substitution by straightforward pattern matching on \texttt{refl}. No additional extensional principle is introduced in this development.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Propositional equality: the identity type with unique constructor refl.
infix 4 _‚Č°_

data _‚Č°_ {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} (x : A) : A ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď where
  refl : x ‚Č° x
{-# BUILTIN EQUALITY _‚Č°_ #-}

-- ¬ß Symmetry is forced by elimination on refl.
sym : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí y ‚Č° x
sym refl = refl

-- ¬ß Transitivity is forced by elimination on the first proof.
trans : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y z : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí y ‚Č° z ‚Üí x ‚Č° z
trans refl q = q

-- ¬ß Congruence: equality is invariant under function application.
cong : {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ} {B : Set ‚Ąď‚āā} (f : A ‚Üí B) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí f x ‚Č° f y
cong f refl = refl

-- ¬ß Substitution: transport along an equality proof.
subst : {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ} (P : A ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď‚āā) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č° y ‚Üí P x ‚Üí P y
subst P refl px = px
\end{code}

The preceding definitions also yield a small uniqueness lemma. If $p : x \equiv y$ and $q : y \equiv x$ compose with $p$ to the trivial loop, then $q$ is propositionally equal to $\mathrm{sym}\,p$. This is simply the reflexivity computation written as a separate statement, but it will be convenient later when inverse paths have to be identified canonically.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Symmetry is the unique inverse closing the trivial loop.
sym-unique :
  {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y : A}
  (p : x ‚Č° y) (q : y ‚Č° x) ‚Üí
  trans p q ‚Č° refl ‚Üí
  q ‚Č° sym p
sym-unique refl q eq = eq
\end{code}

\section{Universe Lifting}
\label{sec:foundation:universe-lifting}

Universe stratification prevents self-reference, but it also creates a practical issue: data living at one level cannot simply be reused at the next. The standard remedy is \texttt{Lift}, which places a value in a higher universe without changing its content.

The accompanying lemmas record the two properties that matter later. First, the embedding is faithful: equality between lifted values reflects equality between the originals. Second, \texttt{Lift} satisfies the expected universal property, so any wrapper with exact round-trip behaviour is canonically isomorphic to it under that requirement.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Lift embeds a type into the next universe level.
data Lift {‚Ąď : Level} (A : Set ‚Ąď) : Set (lsuc ‚Ąď) where
  lift : A ‚Üí Lift A

-- ¬ß Lower extracts the embedded value.
lower : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} ‚Üí Lift A ‚Üí A
lower (lift x) = x

-- ¬ß Lift is injective by construction.
lift-injective : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} {x y : A} ‚Üí lift x ‚Č° lift y ‚Üí x ‚Č° y
lift-injective refl = refl

-- ¬ß A type isomorphism: mutually inverse maps with both round-trips trivial.
record SetIso {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} (A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ) (B : Set ‚Ąď‚āā) : Set (‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚äĒ ‚Ąď‚āā) where
  field
    to      : A ‚Üí B
    from    : B ‚Üí A
    to-from : (b : B) ‚Üí to (from b) ‚Č° b
    from-to : (a : A) ‚Üí from (to a) ‚Č° a
open SetIso public

-- ¬ß Universal property of Lift: every faithful wrapper is canonically isomorphic.
Lift-universal :
  {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď}
  (W : Set (lsuc ‚Ąď))
  (wrap : A ‚Üí W) (unwrap : W ‚Üí A) ‚Üí
  ((a : A) ‚Üí unwrap (wrap a) ‚Č° a) ‚Üí
  ((w : W) ‚Üí wrap (unwrap w) ‚Č° w) ‚Üí
  SetIso W (Lift A)
Lift-universal {‚Ąď} {A} W wrap unwrap unwrap-wrap wrap-unwrap = record
  { to      = őĽ w ‚Üí lift (unwrap w)
  ; from    = őĽ { (lift a) ‚Üí wrap a }
  ; to-from = őĽ { (lift a) ‚Üí cong lift (unwrap-wrap a) }
  ; from-to = őĽ w ‚Üí wrap-unwrap w
  }
\end{code}

\section{Universe-Polymorphic Equality}
\label{sec:foundation:universe-polymorphic-equality}

Some later statements quantify over all universe levels and therefore live in $\mathit{Set}_\omega$. For that reason the text also introduces an identity type at the top universe.

The definition mirrors ordinary propositional equality exactly: there is a single constructor \texttt{reflŌČ}, and the standard derived operations are obtained in the same way. The lemma below records its universal role among reflexive relations at $\mathit{Set}_\omega$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Universe-polymorphic equality at SetŌČ.
infix 4 _‚Č°ŌČ_

data _‚Č°ŌČ_ {A : SetŌČ} (x : A) : A ‚Üí SetŌČ where
  reflŌČ : x ‚Č°ŌČ x

-- ¬ß Symmetry at SetŌČ.
symŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí y ‚Č°ŌČ x
symŌČ reflŌČ = reflŌČ

-- ¬ß Transitivity at SetŌČ.
transŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} {x y z : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí y ‚Č°ŌČ z ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ z
transŌČ reflŌČ q = q

-- ¬ß Congruence at SetŌČ.
congŌČ : {A B : SetŌČ} (f : A ‚Üí B) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí f x ‚Č°ŌČ f y
congŌČ f reflŌČ = reflŌČ

-- ¬ß Substitution at SetŌČ.
substŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} (P : A ‚Üí SetŌČ) {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí P x ‚Üí P y
substŌČ P reflŌČ px = px

-- ¬ß ‚Č°ŌČ is initial among reflexive relations at SetŌČ.
‚Č°ŌČ-initial :
  (R : {A : SetŌČ} ‚Üí A ‚Üí A ‚Üí SetŌČ) ‚Üí
  ({A : SetŌČ} (x : A) ‚Üí R x x) ‚Üí
  {A : SetŌČ} {x y : A} ‚Üí x ‚Č°ŌČ y ‚Üí R x y
‚Č°ŌČ-initial R r reflŌČ = r _
\end{code}

\section{Products and Dependent Pairs}
\label{sec:foundation:products-dependent-pairs}

So far, every structure holds a single piece of information. But the moment a statement like ``there exists an element with a certain property'' must be expressed, two things must be held at once: the element and the proof of its property. Without a way to bundle these together, existential statements are inexpressible inside this constructive setting. The system can talk about individual objects but cannot witness claims about them.

The product type (\texttt{A √ó B}) is the simplest response: it holds two independent values side by side. But the more consequential structure is the dependent pair (\texttt{ő£ A B}), where the type of the second component depends on the value of the first. This is what makes it possible to say ``there exists an $a$ in $A$ such that $B(a)$ holds''‚ÄĒthe $a$ and its proof travel together as a single witness. Without dependent pairs, the development would have universal statements but no internal form of existential witness.

The formal apparatus is correspondingly lean. The record \texttt{\_√ó\_}
handles the non-dependent case of two pieces of data carried together,
while \texttt{ő£} expresses the genuinely existential case in which the
second component depends on the first. From this point on, existential
claims in the book are not verbal gestures but inhabitants of
\texttt{ő£}-types, with ordinary products as the constant special case.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Non-dependent product of two types.
infixr 3 _√ó_

record _√ó_ (A B : Set) : Set where
  constructor _,_
  field
    fst : A
    snd : B

open _√ó_ public

-- ¬ß Dependent pair: the type of witnesses.
record ő£ (A : Set) (B : A ‚Üí Set) : Set where
  constructor _,_
  field
    fst : A
    snd : B fst

open ő£ public
\end{code}

\section{Absurdity and Negation}
\label{sec:foundation:absurdity-negation}

If the filter described in the introduction is to have any teeth, it must be possible to express that something \textit{cannot} exist. A system that can only affirm‚ÄĒnever deny‚ÄĒhas no elimination power. Under such a regime, incompatible candidates would remain indistinguishable from admissible ones.

The empty type (\texttt{‚ä•}) is the formal expression of impossibility: a type with no constructors, and therefore no inhabitants. If one were to hold a value of type \texttt{‚ä•}, a contradiction would already have been reached, and from that contradiction anything follows. This is \texttt{‚ä•-elim}, the principle of \textit{ex falso quodlibet}: from the impossible, derive whatever you wish.

Negation falls out immediately. To say ``$A$ is false'' is to say ``$A$ leads to a contradiction''‚ÄĒformally, $\neg A$ is the function type $A \to \bot$. No separate negation primitive is added. Double negation and inequality are then derived forms, not new axioms.

The code reflects exactly this economy. The type \texttt{‚ä•} has no
constructors, \texttt{‚ä•-elim} is its eliminator, and negation is defined
directly as the function type $A \to \bot$. Refutation is therefore not
an external meta-principle but part of the same internal calculus as
construction.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The empty type: no constructors, no inhabitants.
data ‚ä• : Set where

-- ¬ß Ex falso: the empty type eliminates into any type.
‚ä•-elim : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} ‚Üí ‚ä• ‚Üí A
‚ä•-elim ()

-- ¬ß Ex falso at the top universe.
‚ä•-elimŌČ : {A : SetŌČ} ‚Üí ‚ä• ‚Üí A
‚ä•-elimŌČ ()

-- ¬ß Negation: the unique map into absurdity.
¬¨_ : {‚Ąď : Level} ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď
¬¨ A = A ‚Üí ‚ä•

-- ¬ß Double negation.
¬¨¬¨_ : {‚Ąď : Level} ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď
¬¨¬¨ A = ¬¨ (¬¨ A)

-- ¬ß Inequality: negation of propositional equality.
infix 2 _‚Č†_
_‚Č†_ : {‚Ąď : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď} ‚Üí A ‚Üí A ‚Üí Set ‚Ąď
x ‚Č† y = ¬¨ (x ‚Č° y)
\end{code}

\section{Disjoint Union}
\label{sec:foundation:disjoint-union}

Pairs express ``both A and B.'' But logic also requires ``either A or B''‚ÄĒand it requires knowing \textit{which one}. An untagged alternative‚ÄĒwhere the two cases are merged without a label‚ÄĒdestroys the ability to distinguish them. Case analysis becomes impossible.

The disjoint union (\texttt{A ‚äé B}) solves this by tagging each side: \texttt{inj‚āĀ} marks a witness from the left, \texttt{inj‚āā} from the right. Pattern matching can then determine which case holds. The $\mathit{Set}_\omega$ variant (\texttt{\_‚äéŌČ\_}) extends this to the top universe level, for the same reason equality was extended to all levels: the later arguments quantify at that height and need the same tagged alternatives there.

Again the formal mechanism is minimal. The datatype \texttt{\_‚äé\_}
stores the two tagged alternatives, and \texttt{\_‚äéŌČ\_} repeats the
same construction at the top universe. Every later binary split in the
book, including the coverage data of \texttt{Distinction}, is simply an
instance of pattern matching on this tagged sum.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Disjoint union (coproduct) of two types.
infixr 2 _‚äé_

data _‚äé_ {‚Ąď : Level} (A B : Set ‚Ąď) : Set ‚Ąď where
  inj‚āĀ : A ‚Üí A ‚äé B
  inj‚āā : B ‚Üí A ‚äé B

-- ¬ß Disjoint union at SetŌČ.
infixr 2 _‚äéŌČ_

data _‚äéŌČ_ (A B : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  inj‚āĀŌČ : A ‚Üí A ‚äéŌČ B
  inj‚āāŌČ : B ‚Üí A ‚äéŌČ B
\end{code}

\section{Ontological Primitives}
\label{sec:foundation:ontological-primitives}

Before distinction itself is defined, three auxiliary predicates fix the local vocabulary. A contractible type has a center to which every inhabitant is equal, and therefore carries no internal separation. A type with a distinct pair contains two provably different elements. Non-triviality is simply the negation of contractibility.

These notions provide the vocabulary for the next stage of the argument. They make precise what it means for a carrier to admit a genuine boundary and prepare the non-contractibility results used shortly afterward.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Contractibility: a single center absorbs all inhabitants.
isContr : Set ‚Üí Set
isContr A = ő£ A (őĽ c ‚Üí (x : A) ‚Üí c ‚Č° x)

-- ¬ß A type has a distinct pair if two inhabitants are provably unequal.
HasDistinctPair : Set ‚Üí Set
HasDistinctPair A = ő£ A (őĽ a ‚Üí ő£ A (őĽ b ‚Üí a ‚Č† b))

-- ¬ß Non-triviality: the type is not contractible.
NonTrivial : Set ‚Üí Set
NonTrivial A = ¬¨ (isContr A)
\end{code}

\section{The Distinction Record}
\label{sec:foundation:distinction-record}

Formally, a distinction consists of a carrier, two designated elements of that carrier, a proof that these elements are different, and a cover asserting that every element of the carrier coincides with one of them. This is precisely the data required for the two-case eliminator developed in the next chapter; anything less would leave either separation or coverage unavailable.

The cover is not an ornamental extra. Without it one would have two marked points but no uniform way to classify arbitrary elements of the carrier, and the later elimination arguments would lose their basic case split. The counterexample below makes that dependence explicit rather than rhetorical.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The first distinction: carrier, boundary points, separation, and coverage.
record Distinction : Set1 where
  field
    S     : Set
    ‚Ąď     : S
    r     : S
    ‚Ąď‚Č†r   : ‚Ąď ‚Č† r
    cover : (x : S) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° ‚Ąď) ‚äé (x ‚Č° r)

open Distinction public
\end{code}

\subsection{Necessity of Coverage}
\label{sec:foundation:cover-necessary}

The separation proof and the cover serve different purposes. It is natural to ask whether the cover might be recovered from the two designated boundary points alone. The answer is negative under the displayed data. On a carrier with more than two distinguishable elements, an eliminator based only on the boundary witnesses fails, because an off-boundary element need not satisfy a predicate determined at those two points.

The four-element vertex type $K_4\mathrm{Vertex}$, introduced later in Chapter~\ref{ch:k4-representation-theory}, provides a concrete witness to this failure. Lemma~\ref{lem:cover-necessary} records the argument formally. The cover is therefore part of the definition for operational reasons: it is what makes global two-case elimination possible.

\chapter{Non-Vacuity}
\label{ch:non-vacuity}

The record \texttt{Distinction} has now been defined, but a definition alone does not produce an inhabitant. If \texttt{Distinction} were empty, then every universal statement about distinctions would hold vacuously, and the subsequent development would lose substantive force. The purpose of this chapter is to block that collapse in the weakest form needed at the outset.

Under \AgdaFlag{safe}, no inhabitant of \texttt{Distinction} is simply postulated. The statement used here is the double-negation form $\neg\neg\,\mathit{Distinction}$. It does not yet supply a witness, but it excludes stable denial and is exactly the weaker non-vacuity guard needed before the later constructive witness appears.

Stronger results appear later. Chapter~\ref{ch:two-normal-form} constructs \texttt{Two-distinction} without premises, and Chapter~\ref{ch:scale-closure} provides an independent route through measurement. By contrast, the stronger claim that every inhabited type already contains a distinction is false; the unit type $\top$ is the standard counterexample. The present chapter therefore uses the weaker formulation because it is both correct and sufficient for what follows.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Non-vacuity demands that Distinction cannot be refuted.
record NonVacuityLaw : Set1 where
  field
    nonvacuity : ¬¨¬¨ Distinction

open NonVacuityLaw public
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.0: Distinction Is Irrefutable}
\label{law:nonvacuity:irrefutable}

The first law simply restates the field of \texttt{NonVacuityLaw}. Its role is foundational: without it, every later theorem quantified over distinctions would hold for purely vacuous reasons.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.0: Distinction is irrefutable.
law0-0-nonvacuity : (nv : NonVacuityLaw) ‚Üí ¬¨¬¨ Distinction
law0-0-nonvacuity = nonvacuity
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.1: Non-Vacuity Is Non-Eliminability}
\label{law:nonvacuity:non-eliminability}

This is the same content in a slightly sharper form. The negation of \texttt{Distinction} is itself unstable, even though no explicit witness has yet been constructed.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.1: NonVacuityLaw is non-eliminability of Distinction.
law0-1-nonvacuity-is-non-eliminability :
  NonVacuityLaw ‚Üí ¬¨ (¬¨ Distinction)
law0-1-nonvacuity-is-non-eliminability = nonvacuity
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.2: Distinction Carrier Is Inhabited}
\label{law:nonvacuity:carrier-inhabited}

Once a distinction record is given, its carrier is inhabited, witnessed immediately by the left boundary point $\ell$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.2: Distinction carrier is inhabited.
law0-2-inhabited : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d
law0-2-inhabited d = ‚Ąď d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.3: Distinction Carrier Has A Distinct Pair}
\label{law:nonvacuity:distinct-pair}

The record also provides a distinct pair directly, namely the two boundary points together with their inequality proof.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.3: Distinction carrier has a distinct pair.
law0-3-has-distinct-pair : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí HasDistinctPair (S d)
law0-3-has-distinct-pair d = (‚Ąď d , (r d , ‚Ąď‚Č†r d))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.4: Distinction Carrier Is Non-Contractible}
\label{law:nonvacuity:non-contractible}

The carrier of a distinction cannot be contractible. Under the record fields, a contraction center would identify $\ell$ and $r$, contradicting the separation proof $\ell \neq r$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.4: Distinction carrier is non-contractible.
law0-4-not-contractible : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí NonTrivial (S d)
law0-4-not-contractible d (c , collapse) =
  ‚Ąď‚Č†r d (trans (sym (collapse (‚Ąď d))) (collapse (r d)))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.5: Distinct Pair Forces Non-Contractibility}
\label{law:nonvacuity:distinct-pair-forces-noncontractible}

The previous argument does not depend on the full distinction record. Any type equipped with a distinct pair is non-contractible for the same reason: a contraction center would force the two elements to be equal.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.5: Distinct pair forces non-contractibility.
law0-5-distinct-pair-forces-nontrivial :
  {A : Set} ‚Üí HasDistinctPair A ‚Üí NonTrivial A
law0-5-distinct-pair-forces-nontrivial (a , (b , a‚Č†b)) (c , collapse) =
  a‚Č†b (trans (sym (collapse a)) (collapse b))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.6: Distinction Is Reconstructible From Its Data}
\label{law:nonvacuity:reconstruction}

The distinction record contains no hidden structure beyond its displayed fields. Given a carrier, two elements, a proof that they differ, and a cover, one can reconstruct a \texttt{Distinction} directly.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.6: Distinction is reconstructible from its data.
fromDistinctCovered :
  {S‚āÄ : Set} (a b : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí
  a ‚Č† b ‚Üí
  ((x : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° a) ‚äé (x ‚Č° b)) ‚Üí
  Distinction
fromDistinctCovered {S‚āÄ} a b a‚Č†b cov = record
  { S = S‚āÄ ; ‚Ąď = a ; r = b ; ‚Ąď‚Č†r = a‚Č†b ; cover = cov }

law0-6-reconstruction :
  {S‚āÄ : Set} (a b : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí
  (a‚Č†b : a ‚Č† b) ‚Üí
  (cov : (x : S‚āÄ) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° a) ‚äé (x ‚Č° b)) ‚Üí
  Distinction
law0-6-reconstruction = fromDistinctCovered
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 0.7: Reconstruction Is Exact}
\label{law:nonvacuity:reconstruction-exact}

This reconstruction is exact in the strongest available sense. If one extracts the fields of a distinction and feeds them back into the constructor, the result is definitionally equal to the original term, so the proof is simply \texttt{refl}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 0.7: Reconstruction is exact by definitional equality.
law0-7-reconstruction-exact :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  fromDistinctCovered (‚Ąď d) (r d) (‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (cover d) ‚Č° d
law0-7-reconstruction-exact d = refl
\end{code}

The record may therefore be unpacked and reassembled without loss. Later arguments use this observation when moving between the record itself and its component data.

\chapter{Elimination and Classification}
\label{ch:elimination-classification}

Once a distinction is fixed, the associated endomorphism space is severely constrained. Because the cover identifies every element of the carrier with either $\ell$ or $r$, an endomorphism is determined entirely by its values on those two points. The boundary-output table therefore has at most four entries.

This chapter makes that observation precise and uses it to derive the four-function configuration that later reappears as $K_4$.

\section{Two-Class Coverage}
\label{sec:classification:two-class-coverage}

\subsection{Law 1.1: Carrier Coverage Is Two-Class}
\label{law:classification:two-class-coverage}

The first classification lemma is just the cover field restated for later use. Its importance is practical rather than subtle: every subsequent case distinction on the carrier depends on the fact that the cover reduces all elements to these two classes.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.1: Carrier coverage is two-class.
law1-1-cover : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (x : S d) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° ‚Ąď d) ‚äé (x ‚Č° r d)
law1-1-cover = cover
\end{code}

Two-class coverage is the first structural restriction on the carrier. Figure~\ref{fig:first-distinction} records this dichotomy as a two-node distinction diagram.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=3.2cm]
  \node[circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=10mm, fill=fdLight] (l) {$\ell$};
  \node[circle, draw=fdRed, thick, minimum size=10mm, fill=fdLight, right of=l] (r) {$r$};
  \draw[->, thick] (l) -- node[above] {distinction} (r);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{First distinction: two boundary witnesses forced by two-class coverage.}
\label{fig:first-distinction}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Law 1.2: Elimination Is Forced By Coverage}
\label{law:classification:elimination-from-coverage}

Coverage yields a canonical eliminator for predicates on the carrier. If a property $P$ is known at the two boundary points $\ell$ and $r$, then it is known everywhere, because the cover reduces every input to one of those two cases. The uniqueness lemma below records the corresponding rigidity: any eliminator satisfying the same boundary equations agrees with the canonical one everywhere.

Formally, the point is straightforward. Any eliminator that agrees with
\texttt{Distinction-elim} on the two boundary witnesses must inspect
$\mathrm{cover}\,d\,x$, and this case split leaves only the same two
branches. The boundary equations then force the values to coincide, so
the alleged alternative reduces to pointwise equality with the canonical
eliminator. The dependent eliminator inherits the same rigidity through
transport.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The canonical eliminator for a distinction carrier.
Distinction-elim :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  {P : S d ‚Üí Set} ‚Üí
  P (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
  P (r d) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  P x
Distinction-elim d {P} p‚Ąď pr x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ x‚Č°‚Ąď = subst P (sym x‚Č°‚Ąď) p‚Ąď
... | inj‚āā x‚Č°r = subst P (sym x‚Č°r) pr
\end{code}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.2: Elimination is forced by coverage.
law1-2-elim :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  {P : S d ‚Üí Set} ‚Üí
  P (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
  P (r d) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  P x
law1-2-elim = Distinction-elim

-- ¬ß The non-dependent eliminator is pointwise unique on its boundary equations.
Distinction-elim-unique :
  (d : Distinction) {B : Set}
  (e‚āĀ e‚āā : B ‚Üí B ‚Üí S d ‚Üí B) ‚Üí
  ((b‚Ąď br : B) ‚Üí e‚āĀ b‚Ąď br (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° b‚Ąď) ‚Üí
  ((b‚Ąď br : B) ‚Üí e‚āĀ b‚Ąď br (r d) ‚Č° br) ‚Üí
  ((b‚Ąď br : B) ‚Üí e‚āā b‚Ąď br (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° b‚Ąď) ‚Üí
  ((b‚Ąď br : B) ‚Üí e‚āā b‚Ąď br (r d) ‚Č° br) ‚Üí
  (b‚Ąď br : B) (x : S d) ‚Üí e‚āĀ b‚Ąď br x ‚Č° e‚āā b‚Ąď br x
Distinction-elim-unique d e‚āĀ e‚āā e‚āĀ‚Ąď e‚āĀr e‚āā‚Ąď e‚āār b‚Ąď br x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ p =
        trans (cong (e‚āĀ b‚Ąď br) p)
       (trans (e‚āĀ‚Ąď b‚Ąď br)
       (trans (sym (e‚āā‚Ąď b‚Ąď br))
              (cong (e‚āā b‚Ąď br) (sym p))))
... | inj‚āā p =
        trans (cong (e‚āĀ b‚Ąď br) p)
       (trans (e‚āĀr b‚Ąď br)
       (trans (sym (e‚āār b‚Ąď br))
              (cong (e‚āā b‚Ąď br) (sym p))))
\end{code}

\section{Duality}
\label{sec:classification:duality}

Once the carrier has been reduced to the two boundary classes, there is a canonical nontrivial self-map obtained by exchanging them. This map, called \emph{duality}, sends $\ell$ to $r$ and $r$ to $\ell$. Later results close the classification absolutely: up to pointwise equality, it is the only non-identity automorphism of a distinction.

Its basic property is involutivity. Because every element of the carrier is identified by the cover with one of the two boundary points, applying the swap twice returns the original element after a short case analysis.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The dual map: swap boundary cases.
Distinction-dual : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d ‚Üí S d
Distinction-dual d x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ _ = r d
... | inj‚āā _ = ‚Ąď d

-- ¬ß Duality is involutive by exhaustive case analysis.
Distinction-dual-involutive :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d x) ‚Č° x
Distinction-dual-involutive d =
  Distinction-elim d proof-‚Ąď proof-r
  where
    proof-‚Ąď : Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d (‚Ąď d)) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d
    proof-‚Ąď with cover d (‚Ąď d)
    ... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)
    ... | inj‚āĀ _ with cover d (r d)
    ... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))
    ... | inj‚āā _   = refl

    proof-r : Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d (r d)) ‚Č° r d
    proof-r with cover d (r d)
    ... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))
    ... | inj‚āā _ with cover d (‚Ąď d)
    ... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)
    ... | inj‚āĀ _   = refl
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.3: Duality Is An Involution}
\label{law:classification:duality-involution}

The previous construction is summarized in theorem form here: duality is an involution.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.3: Duality is an involution.
law1-3-dual-involutive :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (x : S d) ‚Üí
  Distinction-dual d (Distinction-dual d x) ‚Č° x
law1-3-dual-involutive = Distinction-dual-involutive
\end{code}

\section{Pointwise Equality}
\label{sec:classification:pointwise-equality}

To compare endomorphisms, the natural relation is pointwise equality. Two functions are identified when they agree at every argument. On a distinction this relation is especially effective, because the cover reduces every input to one of the two boundary cases.

The universal property below states this explicitly. Any relation on endomorphisms that already determines agreement on the two boundary points refines to pointwise equality on the whole carrier under the cover.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Pointwise equality of functions.
infix 4 _‚Čó_
_‚Čó_ : {‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚Ąď‚āā : Level} {A : Set ‚Ąď‚āĀ} {B : Set ‚Ąď‚āā} ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí Set (‚Ąď‚āĀ ‚äĒ ‚Ąď‚āā)
_‚Čó_ {A = A} f g = (x : A) ‚Üí f x ‚Č° g x

-- ¬ß The identity function.
id : {A : Set} ‚Üí A ‚Üí A
id x = x

-- ¬ß Pointwise equality is initial among boundary-respecting comparisons of endomorphisms.
‚Čó-universal :
  (d : Distinction)
  (R : (S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí (S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí Set) ‚Üí
  ({f g : S d ‚Üí S d} ‚Üí R f g ‚Üí f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° g (‚Ąď d)) ‚Üí
  ({f g : S d ‚Üí S d} ‚Üí R f g ‚Üí f (r d) ‚Č° g (r d)) ‚Üí
  {f g : S d ‚Üí S d} ‚Üí R f g ‚Üí f ‚Čó g
‚Čó-universal d R agree-‚Ąď agree-r {f} {g} rfg x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ p = trans (cong f p) (trans (agree-‚Ąď rfg) (cong g (sym p)))
... | inj‚āā p = trans (cong f p) (trans (agree-r rfg) (cong g (sym p)))
\end{code}

\section{Four-Case Endomorphism Classification}
\label{sec:classification:four-case-endomorphism-classification}

An endomorphism $f : S \to S$ is determined by the pair of values $f(\ell)$ and $f(r)$. Since each of these values must be either $\ell$ or $r$, exactly four candidate combinations arise:

\begin{itemize}
\item $f(\ell) = \ell$ and $f(r) = \ell$: the constant-left map.
\item $f(\ell) = r$ and $f(r) = r$: the constant-right map.
\item $f(\ell) = \ell$ and $f(r) = r$: the identity.
\item $f(\ell) = r$ and $f(r) = \ell$: the dual.
\end{itemize}

The soundness proof below turns these four candidates into an exhaustive classification of the endomorphism space. The datatype \texttt{EndoCase} is introduced simply to name them once and for all.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The four-case classification of endomorphisms.
data EndoCase : Set where
  case-constL : EndoCase
  case-constR : EndoCase
  case-id     : EndoCase
  case-dual   : EndoCase
\end{code}

Figure~\ref{fig:k3-failure} illustrates the basic obstruction behind the later appearance of $K_4$: a three-vertex closure cannot accommodate four distinct endomorphism cases without omission.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (a) at (90:1.8) {$c_L$};
  \node (b) at (210:1.8) {$c_R$};
  \node (c) at (330:1.8) {$id$};
  \node[draw=fdRed, text=fdRed, right=2.8cm of c] (d) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- cycle;
  \draw[dashed, thick, fdRed, ->] (d) -- ($(b)!0.5!(c)$);
  \node[rectangle, rounded corners=1mm, draw=fdRed, text=fdRed, fill=white, below=0.2cm of d] {unstable on $K_3$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The failure of $K_3$: the four mathematically distinct endomorphism cases cannot close over three vertices. One function is structurally forced into non-closure.}
\label{fig:k3-failure}
\end{figure}

\section{The \texorpdfstring{$K_4$}{K4} Classification Module}
\label{sec:classification:k4-module}

The four cases are only names until they are related back to actual endomorphisms of the carrier. The \texttt{K‚āĄ} module packages the corresponding constructions: the endomorphisms themselves, pointwise equality, the four representatives, and the maps that interpret and classify them. The parameter $d$ keeps the whole discussion relative to a fixed distinction.

\paragraph{Step 1: Endo type and pointwise equivalence.}
The endomorphism type and its equivalence relation are fixed first.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß K‚āĄ endomorphism algebra: classify, interpret, and verify.
module K‚āĄ (d : Distinction) where
  Endo : Set
  Endo = S d ‚Üí S d

  ‚Čó-refl : {f : Endo} ‚Üí f ‚Čó f
  ‚Čó-refl x = refl

  ‚Čó-sym : {f g : Endo} ‚Üí f ‚Čó g ‚Üí g ‚Čó f
  ‚Čó-sym p x = sym (p x)

  ‚Čó-trans : {f g h : Endo} ‚Üí f ‚Čó g ‚Üí g ‚Čó h ‚Üí f ‚Čó h
  ‚Čó-trans p q x = trans (p x) (q x)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: The four canonical endomorphisms.}
The four representative endomorphisms are then defined. The only slightly nontrivial one is the dual map, whose behaviour on the boundary points is verified by case analysis on \texttt{cover}. Their canonical status is established by the classification laws that follow, not assumed at the moment of definition.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Constant-left endomorphism.
  constL : Endo
  constL _ = ‚Ąď d

  -- ¬ß Constant-right endomorphism.
  constR : Endo
  constR _ = r d

  -- ¬ß The dual endomorphism.
  dual : Endo
  dual = Distinction-dual d

  -- ¬ß Dual sends ‚Ąď to r.
  dual-‚Ąď : dual (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° r d
  dual-‚Ąď with cover d (‚Ąď d)
  ... | inj‚āĀ _   = refl
  ... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)

  -- ¬ß Dual sends r to ‚Ąď.
  dual-r : dual (r d) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d
  dual-r with cover d (r d)
  ... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))
  ... | inj‚āā _   = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 3: Interpretation and classification.}
Each case label is realized as one of the four representative endomorphisms.
Conversely, every endofunction is classified by inspecting its values at
the two boundary points.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Interpret a case label as an endofunction.
  interpret : EndoCase ‚Üí Endo
  interpret case-constL = constL
  interpret case-constR = constR
  interpret case-id     = id
  interpret case-dual   = dual

  -- ¬ß Classify an endofunction by its boundary outputs.
  classify : Endo ‚Üí EndoCase
  classify f with cover d (f (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (f (r d))
  ... | inj‚āĀ _ | inj‚āĀ _ = case-constL
  ... | inj‚āā _ | inj‚āā _ = case-constR
  ... | inj‚āĀ _ | inj‚āā _ = case-id
  ... | inj‚āā _ | inj‚āĀ _ = case-dual
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 4: Soundness of classification.}
Classification followed by interpretation recovers the original
endomorphism at the two boundary points. The full pointwise statement
then follows from the carrier eliminator.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Soundness at ‚Ąď: interpretation recovers the value at ‚Ąď.
  sound-at-‚Ąď : (f : Endo) ‚Üí interpret (classify f) (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° f (‚Ąď d)
  sound-at-‚Ąď f with cover d (f (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (f (r d))
  ... | inj‚āĀ fl‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āĀ _     = sym fl‚Č°‚Ąď
  ... | inj‚āā fl‚Č°r | inj‚āā _     = sym fl‚Č°r
  ... | inj‚āĀ fl‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āā _     = sym fl‚Č°‚Ąď
  ... | inj‚āā fl‚Č°r | inj‚āĀ _     = trans dual-‚Ąď (sym fl‚Č°r)

  -- ¬ß Soundness at r: interpretation recovers the value at r.
  sound-at-r : (f : Endo) ‚Üí interpret (classify f) (r d) ‚Č° f (r d)
  sound-at-r f with cover d (f (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (f (r d))
  ... | inj‚āĀ _     | inj‚āĀ fr‚Č°‚Ąď = sym fr‚Č°‚Ąď
  ... | inj‚āā _     | inj‚āā fr‚Č°r = sym fr‚Č°r
  ... | inj‚āĀ _     | inj‚āā fr‚Č°r = sym fr‚Č°r
  ... | inj‚āā _     | inj‚āĀ fr‚Č°‚Ąď = trans dual-r (sym fr‚Č°‚Ąď)

  -- ¬ß Soundness: classification followed by interpretation recovers behavior.
  classify-sound : (f : Endo) ‚Üí interpret (classify f) ‚Čó f
  classify-sound f x = Distinction-elim d (sound-at-‚Ąď f) (sound-at-r f) x
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 5: Boundary determination and injectivity.}
Endofunctions are determined by their boundary values, again through the
eliminator. The interpretation map is injective because different case
labels produce different behaviour at $\ell$ or $r$.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Endofunctions are determined by their boundary values.
  endo-determined :
    (f g : Endo) ‚Üí
    f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° g (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
    f (r d) ‚Č° g (r d) ‚Üí
    f ‚Čó g
  endo-determined f g eq‚Ąď eqr x = Distinction-elim d eq‚Ąď eqr x

  -- ¬ß Interpretation is injective: distinct cases produce distinct behavior.
  interpret-injective :
    (c c' : EndoCase) ‚Üí
    interpret c ‚Čó interpret c' ‚Üí
    c ‚Č° c'
  interpret-injective case-constL case-constL _ = refl
  interpret-injective case-constL case-constR p = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (p (‚Ąď d)))
  interpret-injective case-constL case-id     p = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (p (r d)))
  interpret-injective case-constL case-dual   p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (trans (p (‚Ąď d)) dual-‚Ąď))

  interpret-injective case-constR case-constL p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (p (‚Ąď d))))
  interpret-injective case-constR case-constR _ = refl
  interpret-injective case-constR case-id     p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (p (‚Ąď d))))
  interpret-injective case-constR case-dual   p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (trans (p (r d)) dual-r)))

  interpret-injective case-id     case-constL p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (p (r d))))
  interpret-injective case-id     case-constR p = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (p (‚Ąď d)))
  interpret-injective case-id     case-id     _ = refl
  interpret-injective case-id     case-dual   p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (trans (p (‚Ąď d)) dual-‚Ąď))

  interpret-injective case-dual   case-constL p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (trans (sym (dual-‚Ąď)) (p (‚Ąď d)))))
  interpret-injective case-dual   case-constR p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (trans (sym (dual-r)) (p (r d))))
  interpret-injective case-dual   case-id     p =
    ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym (trans (sym (dual-‚Ąď)) (p (‚Ąď d)))))
  interpret-injective case-dual   case-dual   _ = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 6: Classification uniqueness.}
Soundness and injectivity together show that the label assigned by
\texttt{classify} is unique. Here the absolute statement is local and
proved: once a case label interprets to the same endomorphism, it must
be the classifier's label.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Classification is unique: the label is forced by soundness + injectivity.
  classify-unique : (f : Endo) ‚Üí (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí interpret c ‚Čó f ‚Üí c ‚Č° classify f
  classify-unique f c c‚Čóf =
    interpret-injective c (classify f) (‚Čó-trans c‚Čóf (‚Čó-sym (classify-sound f)))
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 7: The classifier is a left-inverse of interpretation.}
Applying the uniqueness statement to $f := \mathit{interpret}\,c$
shows that classification is a left inverse to interpretation.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß Classify followed by interpret followed by classify recovers the label.
  classify-interpret : (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí classify (interpret c) ‚Č° c
  classify-interpret c = sym (classify-unique (interpret c) c ‚Čó-refl)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 8: Endomorphisms modulo $\mathrel{\mathit{‚Čó}}$ form a four-element set.}
Taken together, the preceding lemmas identify \texttt{EndoCase} with the
quotient of \texttt{Endo} by pointwise equivalence. The record below
packages the corresponding maps together with their round-trip and
uniqueness properties.

\begin{code}
  -- ¬ß The bijection between EndoCase and (Endo / ‚Čó).
  record EndoCase-bijection : Set where
    field
      to        : Endo ‚Üí EndoCase
      from      : EndoCase ‚Üí Endo
      to-from   : (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí to (from c) ‚Č° c
      from-to   : (f : Endo) ‚Üí from (to f) ‚Čó f
      to-unique : (f : Endo) ‚Üí (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí from c ‚Čó f ‚Üí c ‚Č° to f

  k4-bijection : EndoCase-bijection
  k4-bijection = record
    { to        = classify
    ; from      = interpret
    ; to-from   = classify-interpret
    ; from-to   = classify-sound
    ; to-unique = classify-unique
    }
\end{code}

\section{Mutual Distinctness of Cases}
\label{sec:classification:mutual-distinctness}

The four case labels are pairwise distinct. Since they are constructors
of an inductive datatype, each inequality is verified in Agda by the
impossible pattern \texttt{()}. This is an absolute constructor fact,
not a rhetorical exclusion.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß All four EndoCase constructors are pairwise distinct.
EndoCase-distinct :
  (case-constL ‚Č° case-constR ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constL ‚Č° case-id     ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constL ‚Č° case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constR ‚Č° case-id     ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-constR ‚Č° case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•) √ó
  (case-id     ‚Č° case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•)
EndoCase-distinct =
  (őĽ ()) ,
  ((őĽ ()) ,
   ((őĽ ()) ,
    ((őĽ ()) ,
     ((őĽ ()) ,
      (őĽ ())))))
\end{code}

\section{Top-Level Classification Witnesses}
\label{sec:classification:top-level-witnesses}

The constructions inside the \texttt{K‚āĄ} module are now repackaged as
top-level lemmas. No additional hypotheses are introduced; the point is simply
to make soundness and uniqueness available in a form that can be cited
later without reopening the module.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Top-level K‚āĄ soundness witness.
k4-classification-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  ő£ EndoCase (őĽ c ‚Üí K‚āĄ.interpret d c ‚Čó f)
k4-classification-sound d f = K‚āĄ.classify d f , K‚āĄ.classify-sound d f

-- ¬ß Top-level K‚āĄ uniqueness witness.
k4-classification-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  (c‚āĀ c‚āā : EndoCase) ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.interpret d c‚āĀ ‚Čó f ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.interpret d c‚āā ‚Čó f ‚Üí
  c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā
k4-classification-unique d f c‚āĀ c‚āā p‚āĀ p‚āā =
  K‚āĄ.interpret-injective d c‚āĀ c‚āā (K‚āĄ.‚Čó-trans d p‚āĀ (K‚āĄ.‚Čó-sym d p‚āā))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.4: Endo(S) Classifies Into Exactly Four Cases}
\label{law:k4:endo-four-case-classification}

This law records the classifier introduced above. The word ``exactly'' is
justified by the soundness and uniqueness witnesses already proved inside
the module.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.4: Endo(S) classifies into exactly four cases.
law1-4-classify : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí EndoCase
law1-4-classify d = K‚āĄ.classify d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.5: Classification Is Sound}
\label{law:k4:classification-sound}

Soundness says that interpreting the label assigned to an endomorphism
recovers that endomorphism pointwise.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.5: Classification is sound.
law1-5-classify-sound : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí K‚āĄ.interpret d (K‚āĄ.classify d f) ‚Čó f
law1-5-classify-sound d f = snd (k4-classification-sound d f)
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.6: Endo Is Determined By Boundary Values}
\label{law:k4:boundary-determination}

On a two-class carrier, agreement at the two boundary points already
implies agreement everywhere.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.6: Endo is determined by boundary values.
law1-6-endo-determined :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f g : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  f (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° g (‚Ąď d) ‚Üí
  f (r d) ‚Č° g (r d) ‚Üí
  f ‚Čó g
law1-6-endo-determined d = K‚āĄ.endo-determined d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.7: Classification Is Unique}
\label{law:k4:classification-unique}

If a case label $c$ interprets to an endomorphism pointwise equal to
$f$, then $c$ is forced to be the label assigned to $f$ by the classifier.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.7: Classification is unique.
law1-7-classify-unique :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí
  (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.interpret d c ‚Čó f ‚Üí
  c ‚Č° K‚āĄ.classify d f
law1-7-classify-unique d f c p =
  k4-classification-unique d f c (fst (k4-classification-sound d f)) p (snd (k4-classification-sound d f))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.8: Classification Is a Bijection}
\label{law:k4:classification-bijection}

The preceding laws identify \texttt{EndoCase} with endomorphisms modulo
pointwise equality. The present statement records the left-inverse half
of that bijection explicitly.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.8: classify ‚ąė interpret = id at the case-label level.
law1-8-classify-interpret :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí
  K‚āĄ.classify d (K‚āĄ.interpret d c) ‚Č° c
law1-8-classify-interpret d = K‚āĄ.classify-interpret d
\end{code}

At this point the four-case closure is explicit. The four labels are
exhaustive and mutually distinct, so Figure~\ref{fig:k4-classification}
records the closure as the complete graph $K_4$.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (a) at (90:2.2) {$c_L$};
  \node (b) at (0:2.2) {$c_R$};
  \node (c) at (270:2.2) {$id$};
  \node (d) at (180:2.2) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (b);
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (c);
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (d);
  \draw[thick] (b) -- (c);
  \draw[thick] (b) -- (d);
  \draw[thick] (c) -- (d);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Exhaustive four-case endomorphism classification closes as the complete graph $K_4$.}
\label{fig:k4-classification}
\end{figure}

\chapter{Two Normal Form}
\label{ch:two-normal-form}

The endomorphism space has now been classified completely, but the carrier $S$ of a distinction is still abstract. Coverage reduces it to two boundary classes; what is not yet fixed is the explicit normal form of that carrier. This chapter supplies that normal form and proves that every distinction reaches it.

The cover yields maps in both directions between an arbitrary distinction carrier and the type \texttt{Two}. These maps are inverse up to the expected equalities, so under the conditions already fixed every distinction carrier is forced into isomorphism with a two-element type. At that point the remaining variation is not hidden structure; it is orientation, whether $\ell$ corresponds to \texttt{L} or to \texttt{R}.

\section{The Two Type}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:two-type}

The datatype \texttt{Two} is the explicit two-element object used to display that normal form. Its two constructors are distinct, and its cover is immediate by pattern matching. The later isomorphism theorem, not naming convention, makes it the normal form for arbitrary distinction carriers.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The canonical two-element type.
data Two : Set where
  L : Two
  R : Two

-- ¬ß L and R are distinct.
Two-L‚Č†R : L ‚Č† R
Two-L‚Č†R ()

-- ¬ß Exhaustive two-class coverage for Two.
Two-cover : (x : Two) ‚Üí (x ‚Č° L) ‚äé (x ‚Č° R)
Two-cover L = inj‚āĀ refl
Two-cover R = inj‚āā refl

-- ¬ß Two forms a canonical Distinction.
Two-distinction : Distinction
Two-distinction = record
  { S     = Two
  ; ‚Ąď     = L
  ; r     = R
  ; ‚Ąď‚Č†r   = Two-L‚Č†R
  ; cover = Two-cover
  }
\end{code}

This supplies the constructive witness whose existence was deferred in Chapter~\ref{ch:non-vacuity}. The object \texttt{Two-distinction} inhabits \texttt{Distinction} without further hypotheses, and it will be used throughout the remainder of the text whenever a concrete distinction is required.

\section{Distinction Isomorphism}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:distinction-isomorphism}

To compare distinctions, two related notions are useful. A \texttt{DistinctionIso} is a boundary-preserving isomorphism: it consists of inverse maps between the carriers that also send the distinguished boundary points to their counterparts. A \texttt{DistinctionEquiv} forgets this boundary data and remembers only the underlying equivalence.

Both notions are needed later. The stricter one is appropriate for normal-form statements, while the weaker one is the natural language for orientation and automorphism arguments.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Boundary-preserving isomorphism between distinctions.
record DistinctionIso (d‚āĀ d‚āā : Distinction) : Set1 where
  field
    to      : S d‚āĀ ‚Üí S d‚āā
    from    : S d‚āā ‚Üí S d‚āĀ
    to-from : (y : S d‚āā) ‚Üí to (from y) ‚Č° y
    from-to : (x : S d‚āĀ) ‚Üí from (to x) ‚Č° x
    to-‚Ąď    : to (‚Ąď d‚āĀ) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d‚āā
    to-r    : to (r d‚āĀ) ‚Č° r d‚āā

open DistinctionIso public

-- ¬ß Equivalence without boundary constraints.
record DistinctionEquiv (d‚āĀ d‚āā : Distinction) : Set1 where
  field
    to      : S d‚āĀ ‚Üí S d‚āā
    from    : S d‚āā ‚Üí S d‚āĀ
    to-from : (y : S d‚āā) ‚Üí to (from y) ‚Č° y
    from-to : (x : S d‚āĀ) ‚Üí from (to x) ‚Č° x

open DistinctionEquiv public

-- ¬ß Forgetting boundary data from an isomorphism.
forgetIso : {d‚āĀ d‚āā : Distinction} ‚Üí DistinctionIso d‚āĀ d‚āā ‚Üí DistinctionEquiv d‚āĀ d‚āā
forgetIso i = record
  { to      = DistinctionIso.to i
  ; from    = DistinctionIso.from i
  ; to-from = DistinctionIso.to-from i
  ; from-to = DistinctionIso.from-to i
  }
\end{code}

\section{Canonical Two Maps}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:canonical-two-maps}

For any distinction there are structurally determined maps to and from \texttt{Two}. The map \texttt{toTwo} sends an element to \texttt{L} or \texttt{R} according to the cover, while \texttt{fromTwo} sends the two constructors back to the distinguished boundary points.

The auxiliary lemmas \texttt{toTwo-‚Ąď} and \texttt{toTwo-r} record the behaviour of the forward map on the boundary. The round-trip identities then show that these maps form a boundary-preserving isomorphism.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The canonical boundary-preserving map to Two.
toTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d ‚Üí Two
toTwo d x with cover d x
... | inj‚āĀ _ = L
... | inj‚āā _ = R

-- ¬ß The canonical embedding from Two.
fromTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí Two ‚Üí S d
fromTwo d L = ‚Ąď d
fromTwo d R = r d

-- ¬ß toTwo sends ‚Ąď to L.
toTwo-‚Ąď : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo d (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° L
toTwo-‚Ąď d with cover d (‚Ąď d)
... | inj‚āĀ _   = refl
... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)

-- ¬ß toTwo sends r to R.
toTwo-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo d (r d) ‚Č° R
toTwo-r d with cover d (r d)
... | inj‚āā _   = refl
... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))

-- ¬ß fromTwo ‚ąė toTwo is the identity.
fromTwo-toTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (x : S d) ‚Üí fromTwo d (toTwo d x) ‚Č° x
fromTwo-toTwo d =
  Distinction-elim d
    (trans (cong (fromTwo d) (toTwo-‚Ąď d)) refl)
    (trans (cong (fromTwo d) (toTwo-r d)) refl)

-- ¬ß toTwo ‚ąė fromTwo is the identity.
toTwo-fromTwo : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (t : Two) ‚Üí toTwo d (fromTwo d t) ‚Č° t
toTwo-fromTwo d L = toTwo-‚Ąď d
toTwo-fromTwo d R = toTwo-r d
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 1.8: Every Distinction Is Isomorphic To Two}
\label{law:two-normal-form:isomorphic-to-two}

This is the basic normal-form theorem for distinctions. Here the absolute statement is proved: every distinction is isomorphic to \texttt{Two}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 1.8: Every distinction is isomorphic to Two.
two-normal-form : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionIso d Two-distinction
two-normal-form d = record
  { to      = toTwo d
  ; from    = fromTwo d
  ; to-from = toTwo-fromTwo d
  ; from-to = fromTwo-toTwo d
  ; to-‚Ąď    = toTwo-‚Ąď d
  ; to-r    = toTwo-r d
  }

law1-8-two-normal-form : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionIso d Two-distinction
law1-8-two-normal-form = two-normal-form
\end{code}

\section{Orientation}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:orientation}

An equivalence between a distinction and \texttt{Two} must send the two boundary witnesses to distinct images, because otherwise the inverse map would identify $\ell$ and $r$. Since \texttt{Two} has exactly two elements, the classification closes absolutely here: either the order of the boundary points is preserved or it is swapped.

The following definitions record these two orientations explicitly.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The swap map on Two.
swapTwo : Two ‚Üí Two
swapTwo L = R
swapTwo R = L

-- ¬ß Swap is involutive.
swapTwo-involutive : (t : Two) ‚Üí swapTwo (swapTwo t) ‚Č° t
swapTwo-involutive L = refl
swapTwo-involutive R = refl

-- ¬ß The swap-oriented map to Two.
toTwo-swap : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí S d ‚Üí Two
toTwo-swap d x = swapTwo (toTwo d x)

-- ¬ß Swap-oriented map sends ‚Ąď to R.
toTwo-swap-‚Ąď : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo-swap d (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° R
toTwo-swap-‚Ąď d = cong swapTwo (toTwo-‚Ąď d)

-- ¬ß Swap-oriented map sends r to L.
toTwo-swap-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí toTwo-swap d (r d) ‚Č° L
toTwo-swap-r d = cong swapTwo (toTwo-r d)

-- ¬ß The canonical equivalence (boundary-preserving).
two-normal-form-equiv : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction
two-normal-form-equiv d = forgetIso (two-normal-form d)

-- ¬ß The swap equivalence.
two-normal-form-equiv-swap : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction
two-normal-form-equiv-swap d = record
  { to      = toTwo-swap d
  ; from    = fromTwo d ‚ąė swapTwo
  ; to-from = őĽ t ‚Üí trans (cong (toTwo-swap d) (refl)) (trans (cong swapTwo (toTwo-fromTwo d (swapTwo t))) (swapTwo-involutive t))
  ; from-to = őĽ x ‚Üí trans (cong (fromTwo d) (swapTwo-involutive (toTwo d x))) (fromTwo-toTwo d x)
  }
  where
    _‚ąė_ : {A B C : Set} ‚Üí (B ‚Üí C) ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí A ‚Üí C
    (f ‚ąė g) x = f (g x)

-- ¬ß The two orientations of a Two normal form.
data TwoOrientation : Set where
  orient-preserve : TwoOrientation
  orient-swap     : TwoOrientation

-- ¬ß The two orientations are distinct.
preserve‚Č†swap : orient-preserve ‚Č° orient-swap ‚Üí ‚ä•
preserve‚Č†swap ()

swap‚Č†preserve : orient-swap ‚Č° orient-preserve ‚Üí ‚ä•
swap‚Č†preserve ()
\end{code}

The inverse law therefore forces the boundary images of any equivalence to remain distinct. The classifier below inspects the image of $\ell$ and thereby decides between the two resulting orientations; the exhaustiveness proof below closes the classification.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Equivalence images at boundary are distinct.
to-distinct-on-boundary :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  to e (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° to e (r d) ‚Üí ‚ä•
to-distinct-on-boundary d e eq =
  ‚Ąď‚Č†r d (trans (sym (from-to e (‚Ąď d))) (trans (cong (from e) eq) (from-to e (r d))))

-- ¬ß Classify an equivalence by its orientation.
orientation-classify :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (e : DistinctionEquiv d Two-distinction) ‚Üí
  TwoOrientation
orientation-classify d e with Two-cover (to e (‚Ąď d)) | Two-cover (to e (r d))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°L | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°L = ‚ä•-elim (to-distinct-on-boundary d e (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°L (sym tr‚Č°L)))
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°R | inj‚āā tr‚Č°R = ‚ä•-elim (to-distinct-on-boundary d e (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°R (sym tr‚Č°R)))
... | inj‚āĀ _ | inj‚āā _ = orient-preserve
... | inj‚āā _ | inj‚āĀ _ = orient-swap
\end{code}

\section{Automorphisms}
\label{sec:two-normal-form:automorphisms}

An automorphism of a distinction is an equivalence from the carrier to itself. Since the carrier has only two boundary classes, the admissible boundary behaviour closes to two possibilities: preserve the boundary points or exchange them. The proofs below make this precise by combining injectivity with the cover.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Automorphism of a distinction.
Aut : Distinction ‚Üí Set1
Aut d = DistinctionEquiv d d

-- ¬ß Automorphism transport is injective.
to-injective : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (a : Aut d) ‚Üí {x y : S d} ‚Üí to a x ‚Č° to a y ‚Üí x ‚Č° y
to-injective d a {x} {y} eq =
  trans (sym (from-to a x)) (trans (cong (from a) eq) (from-to a y))

-- ¬ß Automorphism images at boundary points are distinct.
to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí (a : Aut d) ‚Üí to a (‚Ąď d) ‚Č† to a (r d)
to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r d a eq = ‚Ąď‚Č†r d (to-injective d a eq)

-- ¬ß Dual boundary lemma: dual sends ‚Ąď to r.
Distinction-dual-‚Ąď : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí Distinction-dual d (‚Ąď d) ‚Č° r d
Distinction-dual-‚Ąď d with cover d (‚Ąď d)
... | inj‚āĀ _      = refl
... | inj‚āā ‚Ąď‚Č°r    = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) ‚Ąď‚Č°r)

-- ¬ß Dual boundary lemma: dual sends r to ‚Ąď.
Distinction-dual-r : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí Distinction-dual d (r d) ‚Č° ‚Ąď d
Distinction-dual-r d with cover d (r d)
... | inj‚āā _      = refl
... | inj‚āĀ r‚Č°‚Ąď    = ‚ä•-elim ((‚Ąď‚Č†r d) (sym r‚Č°‚Ąď))

-- ¬ß Automorphism classification: exactly id or dual.
Aut-sound :
  (d : Distinction) ‚Üí
  (a : Aut d) ‚Üí
  (to a ‚Čó id) ‚äé (to a ‚Čó Distinction-dual d)
Aut-sound d a with cover d (to a (‚Ąď d)) | cover d (to a (r d))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°‚Ąď = ‚ä•-elim (to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r d a (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď (sym tr‚Č°‚Ąď)))
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°r | inj‚āā tr‚Č°r = ‚ä•-elim (to-‚Ąď‚Č†to-r d a (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°r (sym tr‚Č°r)))
... | inj‚āĀ t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď | inj‚āā tr‚Č°r =
  inj‚āĀ (Distinction-elim d t‚Ąď‚Č°‚Ąď tr‚Č°r)
... | inj‚āā t‚Ąď‚Č°r | inj‚āĀ tr‚Č°‚Ąď =
  inj‚āā (Distinction-elim d
    (trans t‚Ąď‚Č°r (sym (Distinction-dual-‚Ąď d)))
    (trans tr‚Č°‚Ąď (sym (Distinction-dual-r d))))
\end{code}

This completes the automorphism analysis for distinctions. Figure~\ref{fig:k4-orbits}
records the induced symmetry action on the four endomorphism witnesses.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (a) at (90:2.2) {$c_L$};
  \node (b) at (0:2.2) {$c_R$};
  \node (c) at (270:2.2) {$id$};
  \node (d) at (180:2.2) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- (d) -- (a);
  \draw[dashed, thick] (a) -- (c);
  \draw[dashed, thick] (b) -- (d);
  \draw[->, very thick, fdRed] ($(a)+(0.0,0.35)$) arc[start angle=90,end angle=0,radius=2.2];
  \draw[->, very thick, fdRed] ($(c)+(0.0,-0.35)$) arc[start angle=270,end angle=180,radius=2.2];
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Automorphism action on the four-case witness space: preserve and swap survive as the only orbit structure.}
\label{fig:k4-orbits}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Law 1.9: The Two Isomorphism Is Unique}
\label{law:two-normal-form:isomorphism-unique}
