observe-comm : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí (x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí obs a x ‚Č°ŌČ observe a (forgetState x)
observe-comm a = comm (law9-3-factor-through-base (obs a) (base a))

-- ¬ß Law 9.5: Admissible observables collapse to base observables.
law9-5-observe-comm : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí (x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí obs a x ‚Č°ŌČ observe a (forgetState x)
law9-5-observe-comm = observe-comm

-- ¬ß Law 9.6: Base observable extracted from admissibility is unique.
law9-6-observe-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí
  (h‚āĀ h‚āā : DriftState ‚Üí Y) ‚Üí
  ((x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí obs a x ‚Č°ŌČ h‚āĀ (forgetState x)) ‚Üí
  ((x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí obs a x ‚Č°ŌČ h‚āā (forgetState x)) ‚Üí
  h‚āĀ ‚ČóŌČ h‚āā
law9-6-observe-unique a h‚āĀ h‚āā comm‚āĀ comm‚āā s =
  let
    f = obs a
    u : FactorsThroughBase f
    u = record { g = h‚āĀ ; comm = comm‚āĀ }

    v : FactorsThroughBase f
    v = record { g = h‚āā ; comm = comm‚āā }
  in
  law9-4-factor-unique u v s

-- ¬ß Indexed observable type.
IndexedObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
IndexedObs Y = DriftState‚Ąē ‚Üí Y

-- ¬ß Base observable type.
BaseObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
BaseObs Y = DriftState ‚Üí Y

-- ¬ß Lift base to indexed.
liftBase : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseObs Y ‚Üí IndexedObs Y
liftBase h x = h (forgetState x)

-- ¬ß Pack a base observable as admissible.
packObs : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseObs Y ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y
packObs h = record
  { obs  = liftBase h
  ; base = record { respects = őĽ x y eq ‚Üí congŌČ h eq }
  }

-- ¬ß Law 9.7: Indexed observables are unique given a base mediator.
law9-7-obs-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (h : BaseObs Y) ‚Üí
  (f‚āĀ f‚āā : IndexedObs Y) ‚Üí
  ((x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí f‚āĀ x ‚Č°ŌČ h (forgetState x)) ‚Üí
  ((x : DriftState‚Ąē) ‚Üí f‚āā x ‚Č°ŌČ h (forgetState x)) ‚Üí
  f‚āĀ ‚ČóŌČ f‚āā
law9-7-obs-unique h f‚āĀ f‚āā comm‚āĀ comm‚āā x =
  transŌČ (comm‚āĀ x) (symŌČ (comm‚āā x))

-- ¬ß Law 9.8: Canonical indexed presentation for any admissible observable.
law9-8-canonical-pack :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí
  obs a ‚ČóŌČ obs (packObs (observe a))
law9-8-canonical-pack a x = observe-comm a x

-- ¬ß Law 9.9: observe ‚ąė packObs is the identity.
law9-9-observe-pack-id :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (h : DriftState ‚Üí Y) ‚Üí
  observe (packObs h) ‚ČóŌČ h
law9-9-observe-pack-id h s = reflŌČ

\end{code}

The next definition embeds an ordinary small type into the ambient
universe used for observables. The wrapper has a single constructor, so
constructor injectivity and absurd patterns can still distinguish its
images. This makes it suitable for the counterexample below.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Lift an ordinary Set into SetŌČ.
data LiftŌČ (A : Set) : SetŌČ where
  liftŌČ : A ‚Üí LiftŌČ A

-- ¬ß Tick as a SetŌČ-valued observable on indexed drift states.
tickObs : DriftState‚Ąē ‚Üí LiftŌČ ‚Ąē
tickObs s = liftŌČ (tick s)

-- ¬ß Two states with identical base but distinct ticks.
zero-state : DriftState ‚Üí DriftState‚Ąē
zero-state b = ‚ü™ zero , b ‚üę

one-state : DriftState ‚Üí DriftState‚Ąē
one-state b = ‚ü™ suc zero , b ‚üę

-- ¬ß Same base witness for the two distinct-tick states.
same-base : (b : DriftState) ‚Üí forgetState (zero-state b) ‚Č°ŌČ forgetState (one-state b)
same-base b = reflŌČ

\end{code}

The observable \texttt{tickObs} separates two indexed states with the
same base state. It therefore cannot satisfy \texttt{RespectsBase}. The
collapse proved in this chapter applies only to base-respecting
observables; the indexed ledger remains real bookkeeping structure
precisely because this non-admissible observable is rejected.

\begin{code}

-- ¬ß Law 9.x: tick is not an admissible observable.
law9-x-tick-not-admissible :
  RespectsBase {Y = LiftŌČ ‚Ąē} tickObs ‚Üí ‚ä•
law9-x-tick-not-admissible rb =
  liftŌČ-distinct (respects rb (zero-state b‚āÄ) (one-state b‚āÄ) (same-base b‚āÄ))
  where
    b‚āÄ : DriftState
    b‚āÄ = ‚ü® lzero , Distinction‚ÜíDistinction‚Ąď D‚āÄ-distinction ‚ü©

    -- liftŌČ is a data constructor; liftŌČ zero ‚Č°ŌČ liftŌČ (suc zero) is impossible.
    liftŌČ-distinct : liftŌČ zero ‚Č°ŌČ liftŌČ (suc zero) ‚Üí ‚ä•
    liftŌČ-distinct ()
\end{code}

\subsection{Laws 9.10--9.14: Observational Normal Forms}
\label{laws:observables:normal-forms}

Once admissibility has been isolated, the normal form for admissible observables is forced by the factorization theorem: pass to the corresponding base observable and then pack it back into indexed form.

\phantomsection\label{law:observables:canonObs-sound}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Observational equality for admissible observables.
infix 4 _‚ČąObs_
_‚ČąObs_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČąObs_ a b = obs a ‚ČóŌČ obs b

-- ¬ß ‚ČąObs reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity.
‚ČąObs-refl : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs a
‚ČąObs-refl a x = reflŌČ

‚ČąObs-sym : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí {a b : AdmissibleObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs b ‚Üí b ‚ČąObs a
‚ČąObs-sym p x = symŌČ (p x)

‚ČąObs-trans : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí {a b c : AdmissibleObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs b ‚Üí b ‚ČąObs c ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs c
‚ČąObs-trans p q x = transŌČ (p x) (q x)

-- ¬ß Canonical normal form.
canonObs : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y
canonObs a = packObs (observe a)

-- ¬ß Law 9.10: Canonicalization is observationally sound.
law9-10-canonObs-sound :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí
  a ‚ČąObs canonObs a
law9-10-canonObs-sound a = law9-8-canonical-pack a

-- ¬ß Law 9.11: Canonicalization is idempotent up to observation.
law9-11-canonObs-idem :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí
  canonObs (canonObs a) ‚ČąObs canonObs a
law9-11-canonObs-idem a =
  let
    b = canonObs a
  in
  őĽ x ‚Üí symŌČ ((law9-10-canonObs-sound b) x)

-- ¬ß Canonical base observable type.
CanonicalObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
CanonicalObs Y = DriftState ‚Üí Y

-- ¬ß Observational iso record.
record ObsIso (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    to      : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí CanonicalObs Y
    from    : CanonicalObs Y ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y
    to-from : (h : CanonicalObs Y) ‚Üí to (from h) ‚ČóŌČ h
    from-to : (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs from (to a)

open ObsIso public

-- ¬ß Law 9.12: Observational collapse as an explicit iso structure.
law9-12-obsIso : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí ObsIso Y
law9-12-obsIso {Y} = record
  { to      = observe
  ; from    = packObs
  ; to-from = law9-9-observe-pack-id
  ; from-to = law9-10-canonObs-sound
  }

-- ¬ß Law 9.13: Any ObsIso normalization agrees with canonObs up to observation.
law9-13-obsIso-normalizes :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (i : ObsIso Y) ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí
  ObsIso.from i (ObsIso.to i a) ‚ČąObs canonObs a
law9-13-obsIso-normalizes i a =
  ‚ČąObs-trans
    {a = ObsIso.from i (ObsIso.to i a)}
    {b = a}
    {c = canonObs a}
    (‚ČąObs-sym {a = a} {b = ObsIso.from i (ObsIso.to i a)} (ObsIso.from-to i a))
    (law9-10-canonObs-sound a)

-- ¬ß Normalizer record.
record ObsNormalizer (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    norm  : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleObservable Y
    sound : (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs norm a
    idem  : (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí norm (norm a) ‚ČąObs norm a

open ObsNormalizer public

-- ¬ß Law 9.14: Any sound normalizer agrees with canonObs up to observation.
law9-14-normalizer-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (n : ObsNormalizer Y) ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí
  norm n a ‚ČąObs canonObs a
law9-14-normalizer-unique n a =
  ‚ČąObs-trans
    {a = norm n a}
    {b = a}
    {c = canonObs a}
    (‚ČąObs-sym {a = a} {b = norm n a} (sound n a))
    (law9-10-canonObs-sound a)
\end{code}

At this stage the development has shown both that the four-case classification persists beyond endomorphisms and that the indexed reachability apparatus collapses to normal presentations when only admissible information is retained. The next part turns this into an explicit theory of observables and fixpoints.

\part{Observables and Fixpoint}

The next part asks which quantities survive the passage from indexed presentations to genuine observables. By construction, admissible observables ignore bookkeeping data and depend only on the underlying reach structure. This is the setting in which the later invariant arguments are carried out.

\chapter{Admissible Reach Observables}
\label{ch:admissible-reach-observables}

The indexed reachability relation carries more information than should count as observable, because it remembers the bookkeeping index as well as the underlying reach proof. An admissible reach observable is therefore required to factor through the forgetful map to ordinary reachability.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Iterate successor.
iterSuc : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
iterSuc zero    n = n
iterSuc (suc k) n = iterSuc k (suc n)

-- ¬ß Count steps in a strict reach proof.
steps‚Āļ : {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí ‚Ąē
steps‚Āļ one      = suc zero
steps‚Āļ (more p) = suc (steps‚Āļ p)

-- ¬ß Lift a base reach proof to the indexed ledger with correct tick.
liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē :
  (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí
  (p : s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí
  (‚ü™ n , s ‚üę ‚Čļ‚Ąē ‚ü™ iterSuc (steps‚Āļ p) n , t ‚üę)
liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē n {s} one = one‚Ąē
liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē n (more p) = more‚Ąē (liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē (suc n) p)

-- ¬ß Forget after lift recovers the original proof.
forget-after-liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē :
  (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí
  (p : s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí
  forgetReach‚Āļ (liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē n p) ‚Č°ŌČ p
forget-after-liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē n one = reflŌČ
forget-after-liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē n (more p) = congŌČ more (forget-after-liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē (suc n) p)

-- ¬ß Admissible reach observable record.
record AdmissibleReachObservable (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    obsR  : {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí Y
    baseR : {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí Y
    commR : {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí obsR p ‚Č°ŌČ baseR (forgetReach‚Āļ p)

open AdmissibleReachObservable public
\end{code}

\subsection{Laws 10.0--10.1: Admissible Reach Observables Factor Through Forget}
\label{laws:reach-observables:factor-through-forget}

These two lemmas unpack the definition. An admissible reach observable is determined by its base component, and that base component is unique.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 10.0: Admissible reach observables are determined by base reachability.
law10-0-comm : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí obsR a p ‚Č°ŌČ baseR a (forgetReach‚Āļ p)
law10-0-comm a = commR a

-- ¬ß Law 10.1: Base reach observable mediator is unique.
law10-1-baseR-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (obsR' : {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí Y) ‚Üí
  (h‚āĀ h‚āā : {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí Y) ‚Üí
  (({s t : DriftState‚Ąē} (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí obsR' p ‚Č°ŌČ h‚āĀ (forgetReach‚Āļ p))) ‚Üí
  (({s t : DriftState‚Ąē} (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí obsR' p ‚Č°ŌČ h‚āā (forgetReach‚Āļ p))) ‚Üí
  ({s t : DriftState} (p : s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí h‚āĀ p ‚Č°ŌČ h‚āā p)
law10-1-baseR-unique obsR' h‚āĀ h‚āā comm‚āĀ comm‚āā {s} {t} p =
  let
    q = liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē zero p
    e = forget-after-liftReach‚Āļ‚Ąē zero p
  in
  transŌČ
    (symŌČ (congŌČ h‚āĀ e))
    (transŌČ
      (symŌČ (comm‚āĀ q))
      (transŌČ (comm‚āā q) (congŌČ h‚āā e)))
\end{code}

\subsection{Laws 10.2--10.3: Reach Observables And Transitivity}
\label{laws:reach-observables:transitivity}

The next pair of lemmas records compatibility of admissible reach observables with transitivity and extension of indexed reach proofs.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Indexed transitivity.
Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans : {s t u : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí (t ‚Čļ‚Ąē u) ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē u)
Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans one‚Ąē      q = more‚Ąē q
Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans (more‚Ąē p) q = more‚Ąē (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans p q)

-- ¬ß Indexed extension.
Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-extend : {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē stepState‚Ąē t)
Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-extend p = Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans p one‚Ąē

-- ¬ß Forget commutes with transitivity.
forgetReach‚Āļ-trans :
  {s t u : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí
  (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí (q : t ‚Čļ‚Ąē u) ‚Üí
  forgetReach‚Āļ (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans p q) ‚Č°ŌČ Reach‚Āļ-trans (forgetReach‚Āļ p) (forgetReach‚Āļ q)
forgetReach‚Āļ-trans one‚Ąē      q = reflŌČ
forgetReach‚Āļ-trans (more‚Ąē p) q = congŌČ more (forgetReach‚Āļ-trans p q)

-- ¬ß Forget commutes with extension.
forgetReach‚Āļ-extend :
  {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí
  (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí
  forgetReach‚Āļ (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-extend p) ‚Č°ŌČ Reach‚Āļ-extend (forgetReach‚Āļ p)
forgetReach‚Āļ-extend p =
  transŌČ (forgetReach‚Āļ-trans p one‚Ąē) reflŌČ

-- ¬ß Law 10.2: Admissible reach observables respect indexed transitivity.
law10-2-obsR-trans :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  {s t u : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí
  (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí (q : t ‚Čļ‚Ąē u) ‚Üí
  obsR a (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans p q) ‚Č°ŌČ baseR a (Reach‚Āļ-trans (forgetReach‚Āļ p) (forgetReach‚Āļ q))
law10-2-obsR-trans a p q =
  transŌČ (commR a (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-trans p q)) (congŌČ (baseR a) (forgetReach‚Āļ-trans p q))

-- ¬ß Law 10.3: Admissible reach observables respect indexed extension.
law10-3-obsR-extend :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí
  (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí
  obsR a (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-extend p) ‚Č°ŌČ baseR a (Reach‚Āļ-extend (forgetReach‚Āļ p))
law10-3-obsR-extend a p =
  transŌČ (commR a (Reach‚Āļ‚Ąē-extend p)) (congŌČ (baseR a) (forgetReach‚Āļ-extend p))
\end{code}

\subsection{Laws 10.4--10.6: Reach Observable Infrastructure}
\label{laws:reach-observables:infrastructure}

The following auxiliary definitions put admissible reach observables into the same normalisation framework used earlier for state observables.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Indexed reach observable type.
IndexedReachObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
IndexedReachObs Y = {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí Y

-- ¬ß Base reach observable type.
BaseReachObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
BaseReachObs Y = {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí Y

-- ¬ß Pointwise equality on indexed reach observables.
infix 4 _‚ČóRŌČ_ _‚ČóBaseRŌČ_
_‚ČóRŌČ_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí IndexedReachObs Y ‚Üí IndexedReachObs Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČóRŌČ_ {Y} f g = {s t : DriftState‚Ąē} ‚Üí (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí f p ‚Č°ŌČ g p

_‚ČóBaseRŌČ_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČóBaseRŌČ_ {Y} f g = {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (p : s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí f p ‚Č°ŌČ g p

-- ¬ß Lift base reach to indexed reach.
liftBaseR : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí IndexedReachObs Y
liftBaseR h p = h (forgetReach‚Āļ p)

-- ¬ß Pack a base reach observable as admissible.
packReach : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
packReach h = record
  { obsR  = liftBaseR h
  ; baseR = h
  ; commR = őĽ p ‚Üí reflŌČ
  }

-- ¬ß Law 10.4: Indexed reach observables are unique given a base mediator.
law10-4-obsR-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (h : BaseReachObs Y) ‚Üí
  (f‚āĀ f‚āā : IndexedReachObs Y) ‚Üí
  (({s t : DriftState‚Ąē} (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí f‚āĀ p ‚Č°ŌČ h (forgetReach‚Āļ p))) ‚Üí
  (({s t : DriftState‚Ąē} (p : s ‚Čļ‚Ąē t) ‚Üí f‚āā p ‚Č°ŌČ h (forgetReach‚Āļ p))) ‚Üí
  f‚āĀ ‚ČóRŌČ f‚āā
law10-4-obsR-unique h f‚āĀ f‚āā comm‚āĀ comm‚āā p =
  transŌČ (comm‚āĀ p) (symŌČ (comm‚āā p))

-- ¬ß Law 10.5: Canonical indexed presentation for any admissible reach observable.
law10-5-canonical-pack :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  obsR a ‚ČóRŌČ obsR (packReach (baseR a))
law10-5-canonical-pack a p = commR a p

-- ¬ß Law 10.6: baseR ‚ąė packReach is the identity.
law10-6-baseR-pack-id :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (h : BaseReachObs Y) ‚Üí
  baseR (packReach h) ‚ČóBaseRŌČ h
law10-6-baseR-pack-id h p = reflŌČ
\end{code}

\subsection{Laws 10.7--10.11: Reach Observational Normal Forms}
\label{laws:reach-observables:normal-forms}

Reach observables have the same normal-form problem as the earlier
state observables. An admissible reach observable cannot depend on the
particular indexed derivation by which reachability is presented; it
must factor through the base reach relation. The normalizer
\texttt{canonReach} performs exactly this operation: extract the base
observable and pack it back into indexed form.

The laws below record the consequences. Canonical packing is sound,
idempotent up to observational equality, and universal among sound
normalizers. Any other normalization procedure satisfying the same
soundness condition agrees observationally with \texttt{canonReach}.

\begin{code}
infix 4 _‚ČąReachObs_
_‚ČąReachObs_ : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí SetŌČ
_‚ČąReachObs_ a b = obsR a ‚ČóRŌČ obsR b

-- ¬ß ‚ČąReachObs reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity.
‚ČąReachObs-refl : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs a
‚ČąReachObs-refl a p = reflŌČ

‚ČąReachObs-sym : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí {a b : AdmissibleReachObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs b ‚Üí b ‚ČąReachObs a
‚ČąReachObs-sym p q = symŌČ (p q)

‚ČąReachObs-trans : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí {a b c : AdmissibleReachObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs b ‚Üí b ‚ČąReachObs c ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs c
‚ČąReachObs-trans p q r = transŌČ (p r) (q r)

-- ¬ß Canonical reach normal form.
canonReach : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
canonReach a = packReach (baseR a)

-- ¬ß Law 10.7: Canonical reach packing is observationally sound.
law10-7-canonReach-sound :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  a ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-7-canonReach-sound a = law10-5-canonical-pack a

-- ¬ß Law 10.8: Canonical reach packing is idempotent up to observation.
law10-8-canonReach-idem :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  canonReach (canonReach a) ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-8-canonReach-idem a =
  let
    b = canonReach a
  in
  őĽ p ‚Üí symŌČ ((law10-7-canonReach-sound b) p)

-- ¬ß Canonical reach observable type.
CanonicalReachObs : (Y : SetŌČ) ‚Üí SetŌČ
CanonicalReachObs Y = BaseReachObs Y

-- ¬ß Reach observational iso record.
record ReachObsIso (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    to      : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí CanonicalReachObs Y
    from    : CanonicalReachObs Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
    to-from : (h : CanonicalReachObs Y) ‚Üí to (from h) ‚ČóBaseRŌČ h
    from-to : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs from (to a)

open ReachObsIso public

-- ¬ß Law 10.9: Observational collapse as an explicit iso structure.
law10-9-reachObsIso : {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí ReachObsIso Y
law10-9-reachObsIso {Y} = record
  { to      = baseR
  ; from    = packReach
  ; to-from = law10-6-baseR-pack-id
  ; from-to = law10-7-canonReach-sound
  }

-- ¬ß Law 10.10: Any ReachObsIso normalization agrees with canonReach.
law10-10-reachObsIso-normalizes :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (i : ReachObsIso Y) ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  ReachObsIso.from i (ReachObsIso.to i a) ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-10-reachObsIso-normalizes i a =
  ‚ČąReachObs-trans
    {a = ReachObsIso.from i (ReachObsIso.to i a)}
    {b = a}
    {c = canonReach a}
    (‚ČąReachObs-sym {a = a} {b = ReachObsIso.from i (ReachObsIso.to i a)} (ReachObsIso.from-to i a))
    (law10-7-canonReach-sound a)

-- ¬ß Reach normalizer record.
record ReachObsNormalizer (Y : SetŌČ) : SetŌČ where
  field
    norm  : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí AdmissibleReachObservable Y
    sound : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs norm a
    idem  : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí norm (norm a) ‚ČąReachObs norm a

open ReachObsNormalizer public

-- ¬ß Law 10.11: Any sound reach normalizer agrees with canonReach.
law10-11-reach-normalizer-unique :
  {Y : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (n : ReachObsNormalizer Y) ‚Üí
  (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí
  norm n a ‚ČąReachObs canonReach a
law10-11-reach-normalizer-unique n a =
  ‚ČąReachObs-trans
    {a = norm n a}
    {b = a}
    {c = canonReach a}
    (‚ČąReachObs-sym {a = a} {b = norm n a} (sound n a))
    (law10-7-canonReach-sound a)
\end{code}

\chapter{Ranking Principle}
\label{ch:ranking-principle}

The ranking principle is the standard constructive obstruction to a
cycle. If every step strictly increases a natural-number rank, then no
finite path can return to its starting point: such a return would force
\texttt{suc (rank s) ‚Č§ rank s}, which contradicts the order structure
of $\mathbb{N}$.

The record \texttt{DriftRank} isolates the only data needed for this
argument. It assigns a rank to each drift state and proves that one
application of \texttt{stepState} raises that rank strictly. The
following laws extend the one-step estimate along positive reachability
and then use it to rule out cyclic derivations.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Ranking record.
record DriftRank : SetŌČ where
  field
    rank      : DriftState ‚Üí ‚Ąē
    rank-step : (s : DriftState) ‚Üí suc (rank s) ‚Č§ rank (stepState s)

open DriftRank public
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 11.0: Rank Is Monotone Along Reach\texorpdfstring{$^+$}{+}}
\label{law:ranking:monotone}

Positive reachability is generated by one or more applications of
\texttt{stepState}. The rank inequality is therefore propagated by
induction over the reachability witness: one step uses
\texttt{rank-step}, and a longer path composes that estimate with the
induction hypothesis.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 11.0: Rank is monotone along Reach‚Āļ.
law11-0-rank-mono : (r : DriftRank) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí rank r s ‚Č§ rank r t
law11-0-rank-mono r {s} one =
  ‚Č§-trans (‚Č§-step (rank r s)) (rank-step r s)
law11-0-rank-mono r {s} (more p) =
  ‚Č§-trans
    (‚Č§-trans (‚Č§-step (rank r s)) (rank-step r s))
    (law11-0-rank-mono r p)

-- ¬ß Strict monotonicity.
law11-0-rank-increases : (r : DriftRank) ‚Üí {s t : DriftState} ‚Üí (s ‚Čļ t) ‚Üí suc (rank r s) ‚Č§ rank r t
law11-0-rank-increases r {s} one = rank-step r s
law11-0-rank-increases r {s} (more p) =
  ‚Č§-trans (rank-step r s) (law11-0-rank-mono r p)
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 11.1: Ranking Forces Acyclicity}
\label{law:ranking:forces-acyclicity}

Once monotonicity has been established, acyclicity follows immediately: a cycle would force a strict successor inequality back onto the same natural number.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 11.1: Ranking forces DriftAcyclic.
law11-1-ranked-acyclic : DriftRank ‚Üí DriftAcyclic
law11-1-ranked-acyclic r =
  record
    { no-cycle =
        őĽ s p ‚Üí
          suc‚Č§-impossible (rank r s) (law11-0-rank-increases r p)
    }
\end{code}

\chapter{Presentation Fixpoint}
\label{ch:presentation-fixpoint}

The earlier normalization results show that admissible presentations collapse to canonical ones after the relevant factorization hypotheses are imposed. This chapter uses that result at the level of observables: once presentation-dependent residue has been removed, every admissible meta-observable factors through the same canonical extractor.

\subsection{Global Observable Elimination}
\label{sec:global-observable-elimination}

The point is to isolate what depends only on the invariant structure and what depends on the particular way that structure has been presented. The factorization statements below express this formally.

Schematically, the claim is that every admissible meta-observable factors through the canonical invariant basis:
$$
\mathrm{ObservableElim} :
\bigl(\text{admissible }K_4\text{-observable}\bigr)
\to
\bigl(\text{canonical invariant basis} \to Y\bigr),
$$

The figure below illustrates this elimination of presentation-dependent bookkeeping.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\resizebox{\linewidth}{!}{%
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.6cm]
  \node[concept] (idx) {indexed\\observable};
  \node[concept, below=1.4cm of idx] (reach) {reach\\observable};
  \coordinate (mid) at ($(idx)!0.5!(reach)$);
  \node[derives] (canon) at ($(mid)+(4.2,0)$) {canonical\\extractor};
  \node[concept, right=3.0cm of canon] (basis) {invariant\\basis of $K_4$};
  \node[derives, right=3.0cm of basis] (y) {$Y$};

  \draw[flow] (idx) -- node[label, above] {forget bookkeeping} (canon);
  \draw[flow] (reach) -- node[label, below] {erase witness order} (canon);
  \draw[flow] (canon) -- node[label, above] {forced factorization} (basis);
  \draw[flow] (basis) -- node[label, above] {evaluation} (y);

  \node[rectangle, rounded corners=1mm, draw=fdRed, text=fdRed, fill=white, above=0.55cm of canon] (residue) {tick index / labels / presentation noise};
  \draw[->, thick, fdRed] (residue.south) -- (canon.north);
\end{tikzpicture}%
}
\caption{Admissible observables factor through the canonical invariant basis once presentation-dependent bookkeeping has been removed.}
\label{fig:observable-fixpoint}
\end{figure}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Respecting ‚ČąObs.
record Respects‚ČąObs {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    respects : {a b : AdmissibleObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąObs b ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ F b

open Respects‚ČąObs public

-- ¬ß Factorization record for state meta-observables.
record FactorsThroughObserve {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    g    : (DriftState ‚Üí Y) ‚Üí Z
    comm : (a : AdmissibleObservable Y) ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ g (observe a)

open FactorsThroughObserve public

-- ¬ß Law 12.0: Meta-observables factor through observe.
law12-0-meta-observe-factor :
  {Y Z : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (F : AdmissibleObservable Y ‚Üí Z) ‚Üí
  Respects‚ČąObs F ‚Üí
  FactorsThroughObserve F
law12-0-meta-observe-factor F r =
  record
    { g = őĽ h ‚Üí F (packObs h)
    ; comm = őĽ a ‚Üí
        respects r (law9-10-canonObs-sound a)
    }

-- ¬ß Respecting ‚ČąReachObs.
record Respects‚ČąReachObs {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    respectsR : {a b : AdmissibleReachObservable Y} ‚Üí a ‚ČąReachObs b ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ F b

open Respects‚ČąReachObs public

-- ¬ß Factorization record for reach meta-observables.
record FactorsThroughBaseR {Y Z : SetŌČ} (F : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí Z) : SetŌČ where
  field
    gR    : BaseReachObs Y ‚Üí Z
    commR : (a : AdmissibleReachObservable Y) ‚Üí F a ‚Č°ŌČ gR (baseR a)

open FactorsThroughBaseR public

-- ¬ß Law 12.1: Meta-observables factor through baseR.
law12-1-meta-baseR-factor :
  {Y Z : SetŌČ} ‚Üí
  (F : AdmissibleReachObservable Y ‚Üí Z) ‚Üí
  Respects‚ČąReachObs F ‚Üí
  FactorsThroughBaseR F
law12-1-meta-baseR-factor F r =
  record
    { gR = őĽ h ‚Üí F (packReach h)
    ; commR = őĽ a ‚Üí
        respectsR r (law10-7-canonReach-sound a)
    }
\end{code}

\chapter{Graph Presentation}
\label{ch:graph-presentation}

At this point the surviving combinatorial object can be presented
explicitly. The four mutually distinct endomorphism classes supply four
vertices, and distinctness itself supplies the edge relation. Because
every pair of cases is distinct, every pair of vertices is connected.
The result is the complete graph $K_4$.

The numerical invariants of this graph are assembled in the next
chapter. Before introducing that ledger, the graph itself is defined
formally and its uniqueness up to isomorphism is made explicit.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Graph record.
record Graph : Set1 where
  field
    V    : Set
    Edge : V ‚Üí V ‚Üí Set
    edge-sym : {a b : V} ‚Üí Edge a b ‚Üí Edge b a
    edge-irr : (a : V) ‚Üí Edge a a ‚Üí ‚ä•

open Graph public

-- ¬ß Graph isomorphism record.
record GraphIso (G H : Graph) : Set1 where
  field
    to       : V G ‚Üí V H
    from     : V H ‚Üí V G
    to-from  : (y : V H) ‚Üí to (from y) ‚Č° y
    from-to  : (x : V G) ‚Üí from (to x) ‚Č° x
    edge-to  : {a b : V G} ‚Üí Edge G a b ‚Üí Edge H (to a) (to b)
    edge-from : {a b : V H} ‚Üí Edge H a b ‚Üí Edge G (from a) (from b)

open GraphIso public

-- ¬ß Transport inequality across equalities.
transport‚Č† :
  {A : Set} ‚Üí
  {a a' b b' : A} ‚Üí
  a ‚Č° a' ‚Üí
  b ‚Č° b' ‚Üí
  (a ‚Č† b) ‚Üí
  (a' ‚Č† b')
transport‚Č† ea eb neq eq' = neq (trans ea (trans eq' (sym eb)))
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 13.0: The Canonical K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Graph Has Edge = Inequality}
\label{law:graph-presentation:canonical-k4}

The canonical $K_4$ graph is constructed with \texttt{EndoCase} as its
vertex set and inequality as its edge relation. Symmetry and
irreflexivity of the edge relation are immediate from the corresponding
properties of $\neq$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 13.0: The canonical K‚āĄ graph.
K4GraphCanonical : Graph
K4GraphCanonical = record
  { V = EndoCase
  ; Edge = őĽ a b ‚Üí a ‚Č† b
  ; edge-sym = őĽ {a} {b} neq eq ‚Üí neq (sym eq)
  ; edge-irr = őĽ a loop ‚Üí loop refl
  }

-- ¬ß K‚āĄ graph presentation record.
record K4GraphPresentation : Set1 where
  field
    Vp      : Set
    toV     : Vp ‚Üí EndoCase
    fromV   : EndoCase ‚Üí Vp
    to-from : (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí toV (fromV c) ‚Č° c
    from-to : (v : Vp) ‚Üí fromV (toV v) ‚Č° v

open K4GraphPresentation public

-- ¬ß Present a graph from a K‚āĄ graph presentation.
presentedGraph : K4GraphPresentation ‚Üí Graph
presentedGraph p = record
  { V = Vp p
  ; Edge = őĽ v w ‚Üí toV p v ‚Č† toV p w
  ; edge-sym = őĽ {a} {b} neq eq ‚Üí neq (sym eq)
  ; edge-irr = őĽ a loop ‚Üí loop refl
  }
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 13.1: Any K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Graph Presentation Collapses To The Canonical Graph Up To Iso}
\label{law:graph-presentation:presentation-collapse}

Any other presentation of the same four vertices is obtained by
relabelling them. Once a bijection with \texttt{EndoCase} has been
fixed, the edge relation is transported along that bijection, and the
resulting graph is isomorphic to the standard one.

Figure~\ref{fig:graph-presentation-iso} depicts this relabelling principle.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\resizebox{\linewidth}{!}{%
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.8cm, every node/.style={circle, draw=fdBlue, thick, minimum size=9mm, fill=fdLight}]
  \node (p1) at (0,1.5) {$v_1$};
  \node (p2) at (1.6,0.2) {$v_2$};
  \node (p3) at (0,-1.1) {$v_3$};
  \node (p4) at (-1.6,0.2) {$v_4$};
  \draw[thick] (p1)--(p2)--(p3)--(p4)--cycle;
  \draw[thick] (p1)--(p3);
  \draw[thick] (p2)--(p4);

  \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=fdGray, right=2.9cm of p2] (iso) {$\cong$};

  \node (c1) at ($(iso)+(3.3,1.3)$) {$c_L$};
  \node (c2) at ($(iso)+(4.9,0)$) {$c_R$};
  \node (c3) at ($(iso)+(3.3,-1.3)$) {$id$};
  \node (c4) at ($(iso)+(1.7,0)$) {$dual$};
  \draw[thick] (c1)--(c2)--(c3)--(c4)--cycle;
  \draw[thick] (c1)--(c3);
  \draw[thick] (c2)--(c4);

  \draw[flow] (p1) -- node[label, above] {$\texttt{toV}$} (c1);
  \draw[flow] (p2) -- (c2);
  \draw[flow] (p3) -- (c3);
  \draw[flow] (p4) -- (c4);
\end{tikzpicture}%
}
\caption{Any four-vertex presentation of the endomorphism classes collapses, by relabelling, to the canonical graph on \texttt{EndoCase}.}
\label{fig:graph-presentation-iso}
\end{figure}

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 13.1: Presentation iso.
law13-1-presentation-iso : (p : K4GraphPresentation) ‚Üí GraphIso (presentedGraph p) K4GraphCanonical
law13-1-presentation-iso p = record
  { to = toV p
  ; from = fromV p
  ; to-from = to-from p
  ; from-to = from-to p
  ; edge-to = őĽ {a} {b} e ‚Üí e
  ; edge-from = őĽ {a} {b} e ‚Üí
      transport‚Č† (sym (to-from p a)) (sym (to-from p b)) e
  }
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 13.2: K\texorpdfstring{$_3$}{3} Cannot Host the Four Cases}
\label{law:graph-presentation:k3-failure}
\phantomsection\label{ch:K3-failure}

The obstruction to $K_3$ is purely finite and combinatorial: three
vertices cannot host four pairwise distinct cases. The proof below is
the corresponding pigeonhole argument carried out explicitly for the
three-element type \texttt{Three}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The three-element type: the candidate vertex set for K‚āÉ.
data Three : Set where
  v1 : Three
  v2 : Three
  v3 : Three

-- ¬ß Pigeonhole on Three: four pairwise distinct values do not fit.
no-four-distinct-Three :
  (a b c d : Three) ‚Üí
  a ‚Č† b ‚Üí a ‚Č† c ‚Üí a ‚Č† d ‚Üí b ‚Č† c ‚Üí b ‚Č† d ‚Üí c ‚Č† d ‚Üí ‚ä•
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v1 _  _  nab _   _   _   _   _   = nab refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v2 _  _  nab _   _   _   _   _   = nab refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v3 _  _  nab _   _   _   _   _   = nab refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v2 v1 _  _   nac _   _   _   _   = nac refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v2 v2 _  _   _   _   nbc _   _   = nbc refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v2 v3 v1 _   _   nad _   _   _   = nad refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v2 v3 v2 _   _   _   _   nbd _   = nbd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v2 v3 v3 _   _   _   _   _   ncd = ncd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v3 v1 _  _   nac _   _   _   _   = nac refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v3 v3 _  _   _   _   nbc _   _   = nbc refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v3 v2 v1 _   _   nad _   _   _   = nad refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v3 v2 v2 _   _   _   _   _   ncd = ncd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v1 v3 v2 v3 _   _   _   _   nbd _   = nbd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v1 v2 _  _   nac _   _   _   _   = nac refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v1 v1 _  _   _   _   nbc _   _   = nbc refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v1 v3 v2 _   _   nad _   _   _   = nad refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v1 v3 v1 _   _   _   _   nbd _   = nbd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v1 v3 v3 _   _   _   _   _   ncd = ncd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v3 v2 _  _   nac _   _   _   _   = nac refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v3 v3 _  _   _   _   nbc _   _   = nbc refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v3 v1 v2 _   _   nad _   _   _   = nad refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v3 v1 v3 _   _   _   _   nbd _   = nbd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v2 v3 v1 v1 _   _   _   _   _   ncd = ncd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v1 v3 _  _   nac _   _   _   _   = nac refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v1 v1 _  _   _   _   nbc _   _   = nbc refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v1 v2 v3 _   _   nad _   _   _   = nad refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v1 v2 v1 _   _   _   _   nbd _   = nbd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v1 v2 v2 _   _   _   _   _   ncd = ncd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v2 v3 _  _   nac _   _   _   _   = nac refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v2 v2 _  _   _   _   nbc _   _   = nbc refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v2 v1 v3 _   _   nad _   _   _   = nad refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v2 v1 v2 _   _   _   _   nbd _   = nbd refl
no-four-distinct-Three v3 v2 v1 v1 _   _   _   _   _   ncd = ncd refl

-- ¬ß Law 13.2: No injection from EndoCase into a three-element type.
law13-2-K3-fails :
  (f : EndoCase ‚Üí Three) ‚Üí
  ((x y : EndoCase) ‚Üí f x ‚Č° f y ‚Üí x ‚Č° y) ‚Üí
  ‚ä•
law13-2-K3-fails f inj =
  no-four-distinct-Three
    (f case-constL) (f case-constR) (f case-id) (f case-dual)
    (őĽ eq ‚Üí distinct-cL-cR (inj _ _ eq))
    (őĽ eq ‚Üí distinct-cL-id (inj _ _ eq))
    (őĽ eq ‚Üí distinct-cL-d  (inj _ _ eq))
    (őĽ eq ‚Üí distinct-cR-id (inj _ _ eq))
    (őĽ eq ‚Üí distinct-cR-d  (inj _ _ eq))
    (őĽ eq ‚Üí distinct-id-d  (inj _ _ eq))
  where
    distinct-cL-cR : case-constL ‚Č° case-constR ‚Üí ‚ä•
    distinct-cL-cR ()
    distinct-cL-id : case-constL ‚Č° case-id ‚Üí ‚ä•
    distinct-cL-id ()
    distinct-cL-d  : case-constL ‚Č° case-dual ‚Üí ‚ä•
    distinct-cL-d  ()
    distinct-cR-id : case-constR ‚Č° case-id ‚Üí ‚ä•
    distinct-cR-id ()
    distinct-cR-d  : case-constR ‚Č° case-dual ‚Üí ‚ä•
    distinct-cR-d  ()
    distinct-id-d  : case-id ‚Č° case-dual ‚Üí ‚ä•
    distinct-id-d  ()
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Law 13.3: \texorpdfstring{$K_5$}{K5} Cannot Arise}
\label{sec:k4-uniqueness:k5-cannot-arise}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The opposite direction is equally simple: the four cases already exhaust
the classification, so there is no fifth vertex to add. The next lemma
packages that observation in predicate form.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 13.3: no fifth EndoCase distinct from the four classified.
law13-3-K5-fails :
  (P : EndoCase ‚Üí Set) ‚Üí
  (P case-id     ‚Üí ‚ä•) ‚Üí
  (P case-dual   ‚Üí ‚ä•) ‚Üí
  (P case-constL ‚Üí ‚ä•) ‚Üí
  (P case-constR ‚Üí ‚ä•) ‚Üí
  (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí P c ‚Üí ‚ä•
law13-3-K5-fails _ ¬¨id _    _    _    case-id     pid = ¬¨id pid
law13-3-K5-fails _ _   ¬¨dl  _    _    case-dual   pdl = ¬¨dl pdl
law13-3-K5-fails _ _   _    ¬¨cL  _    case-constL pcl = ¬¨cL pcl
law13-3-K5-fails _ _   _    _    ¬¨cR  case-constR pcr = ¬¨cR pcr
\end{code}

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{The \texorpdfstring{$K_4$}{K4} Invariant Record}
\label{ch:unexpected-correspondence}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

With the graph now fixed, the next task is to collect its basic
combinatorial and spectral data into a single invariant record. At this
point the abstract classification results begin to take explicit
numerical form.

\section{What the Graph Contains}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:what-the-graph-contains}

Several entries of this ledger are immediate. The graph has four
vertices, every pair of distinct vertices is connected, and therefore
the edge count is
\[
  \binom{4}{2} = 6.
\]
Viewed as the boundary of a tetrahedron, it also carries the familiar
Euler data that will be used in the numerical constructions below.

\section{The Spectral Invariant}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:spectral-invariant}

The same graph also determines the spectral quantity used later. From
the Euler identity one obtains
\[
  V - E + F = 4 - 6 + 4 = 2,
\]
and hence
\[
  \mathcal{C} = V^d \cdot \chi + d^2 = 4^3 \cdot 2 + 3^2 = 128 + 9 = 137.
\]

Figure~\ref{fig:k4-ledger} gathers the ledger visually: the same
tetrahedral survivor carries the vertex count, edge count, Euler data,
and spectral split from which the later numerical invariants are
assembled.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, node distance=2.8cm]
  \coordinate (A) at (0,0);
  \coordinate (B) at (2.2,0);
  \coordinate (C) at (1.1,1.9);
  \coordinate (D) at (1.1,0.75);
  \draw[thick] (A)--(B)--(C)--cycle;
  \draw[thick] (A)--(D);
  \draw[thick] (B)--(D);
  \draw[thick] (C)--(D);
  \foreach \p/\name in {A/1,B/2,C/3,D/4} {\fill[fdBlue] (\p) circle (2.2pt);}

  \node[concept, left=1.7cm of A] (vtx) {$V = 4$};
  \node[concept, right=1.7cm of B] (edg) {$E = 6$};
  \node[concept, above=1.1cm of C] (chi) {$\chi = 2$};
  \node[derives, below=1.2cm of D] (spec) {$\mathrm{Spec}(L) = \{0,4,4,4\}$};
  \node[derives, right=1.5cm of spec] (alpha) {$\mathcal{C} = 137$};

  \draw[flow] (vtx.east) -- ($(A)!0.5!(C)$);
  \draw[flow] (edg.west) -- ($(B)!0.5!(C)$);
  \draw[flow] (chi.south) -- (C);
  \draw[flow] (spec.north) -- (D);
  \draw[flow] (spec.east) -- (alpha.west);
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The basic combinatorial and spectral data of $K_4$ assembled into the invariant ledger.}
\label{fig:k4-ledger}
\end{figure}

\section{What Follows, and Why}
\label{sec:unexpected-correspondence:what-follows-why}

Two further moves remain before this ledger can be read as numerical
output. The first is structural: the next chapter re-reads the ledger
as the description of an extension structure, on the simple ground
that distinction once made cannot be undone, and that nothing in the
surviving graph singles out a preferred direction. The second is
arithmetic: the part that follows develops the algebraic tools needed
to evaluate the invariants the ledger now carries. The order is not
optional. The arithmetic operates on quantities whose meaning has
already been fixed by the spatial reading.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{Forced Spatial Extension}
\label{ch:forced-spatial-extension}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

The invariant ledger of $K_4$ has been forced. What it \emph{is}, however,
has not yet been read off. The four numbers $V$, $E$, $d$, $\chi$ are not
a tuple of independent quantities a posteriori arranged on a graph: they
are the count, the connectivity, the dimensionality, and the closure
condition of one and the same surviving object. This chapter names that
object. The naming is not a definition added by hand; it is forced by
premises already proved.

\section{The Order of Forcing}
\label{sec:spatial-extension:order-of-forcing}

Three premises have been carried this far without being assembled.
Non-collapse of distinction (\hyperref[ch:drift-noncollapse]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:drift-noncollapse}})
forbids the re-identification of what has been told apart.
Drift non-triviality (\hyperref[ch:drift]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:drift}})
forbids that ``differs from'' is a single binary flag: there are several
independent ways in which two configurations may differ, and their count
is precisely $d$. The symmetry of $K_4$ (the orbit theorems of
\hyperref[ch:graph-presentation]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:graph-presentation}})
forbids that any vertex, edge, or independent direction is structurally
distinguishable from any other.

Read singly, each of these is an elimination theorem on the survivor.
Read together, they constrain a single object whose name is not
optional. The remainder of this chapter unfolds the constraint to its
unique fixed point.

\textbf{Constraint:} the survivor must be compatible with non-collapse,
with drift non-triviality, and with full $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-symmetry,
simultaneously and at every successor step.

\textbf{Elimination:} a passive set of $V^d$ tuples is eliminated by
non-collapse iterated through time; a one-dimensional or tree-like
extension is eliminated by drift non-triviality; an extension with a
preferred direction is eliminated by symmetry.

\textbf{Consequence:} what survives is an object equipped with $d$
independent monotone directions in which extension is permitted, with
no direction marked. The remaining sections of this chapter prove each
elimination explicitly.

\section{Irreversibility from Non-Collapse}
\label{sec:spatial-extension:irreversibility}

Non-collapse, as proved earlier, states that once $\ell \neq r$ has
been told apart, the two sides cannot be re-identified. The verb
\emph{cannot} carries a temporal load that the proof itself already
satisfies but that has not yet been named. To say that two distinguished
sides remain distinguished is not a claim about the moment of
distinction; it is a claim about every later moment as well. A reading
that confined non-collapse to a single instant would empty it: a
distinction that may be revoked at the next step is not a distinction.

The successor relation in the corpus is propositional. Decidable
inequality $\mathrm{Dec}(x \equiv y)$ is a propositional statement, and
propositional truth values are not mutable: a proof witness, once
constructed, persists through every later context in which it can be
referred to. Irreversibility of distinction is therefore not an
additional axiom but the unavoidable form non-collapse takes inside a
type theory that records its negations propositionally. The set of
distinctions made is monotone in any successor relation the system
admits.

\textbf{Constraint:} non-collapse must hold uniformly over all
successor steps, not only at the moment of distinction.

\textbf{Elimination:} the reading ``non-collapse holds in the moment of
distinction but may be revoked later'' is eliminated by the propositional
character of $\mathrm{Dec}(x \equiv y)$ already proved. A revocable
inequality is not an inequality at all.

\textbf{Consequence:} the configuration set $\mathcal{C}$ of
distinguishable states is monotone under any successor relation; it
may grow, it cannot shrink. This monotonicity is recorded in what
follows as a partial order on $\mathcal{C}$.

\section{Symmetry Forbids a Preferred Direction}
\label{sec:spatial-extension:isotropy}

Drift non-triviality leaves $d$ independent directions in which two
configurations may differ. Standing alone, this could still be
compatible with a structure in which one of those directions is
distinguished by the dynamics from the others: a tree, a fibration,
or any object with a preferred axis would qualify. The symmetry of
$K_4$ forbids exactly this.

The orbit theorems prove that $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4) \cong S_4$ acts
transitively on the vertices and on the edges of the surviving graph.
The transitive action extends to the independent directions of drift:
each pair of directions is connected by a graph automorphism. Suppose,
for contradiction, that the successor relation singled out one
direction $\mathbf{e}_*$ over the others. Then ``the preferred
direction'' would be a structural invariant of the system: a feature
the dynamics distinguishes from any other direction. The automorphism
that maps $\mathbf{e}_*$ to a non-preferred $\mathbf{e}_j$ would have
to preserve every structural feature; under it, the preferred
direction would now be $\mathbf{e}_j$. Either, then, the preference is
genuine and breaks $S_4$, or it was never structural to begin with.

The first horn is forbidden by the orbit theorems. Only the second
survives. Isotropy of extension is therefore a derived consequence of
the symmetry already proved, not a separate postulate.

\textbf{Constraint:} no direction of drift may be marked by a feature
that survives $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-symmetry.

\textbf{Elimination:} a structure with a distinguished extension axis
is eliminated by transitivity of the orbit action on directions. The
candidate ``preferred direction'' would have to be either a structural
invariant (forbidden) or a labelling (not a structural fact at all).

\textbf{Consequence:} extension is permitted equally along each of the
$d$ independent directions. No direction may be silently distinguished
in any later construction.

\section{The Surviving Object}
\label{sec:spatial-extension:surviving-object}

The three sections above each eliminate alternatives to a single object.
The object that survives all three eliminations is exhibited here.

A monotone configuration set with $d$ independent directions of
extension and no preferred direction among them is not a tree, not a
chain, and not a passive cardinality. It is a $d$-dimensional
extension structure: it has at least $V^d$ distinguishable
configurations (one for each independent assignment of states across
$d$ directions), it carries a partial order in which each successor
adds rather than removes a distinction, and its symmetry group is
exactly the symmetry group of $K_4$ that produced it. The book has,
since the elimination of $K_5$, called this surviving object the
graph $K_4$. The present chapter shows that the same object also
admits, under the premises just collected, the reading: \emph{the
minimal isotropic, monotone, $d$-dimensional extension in which
distinction may live}.

The two readings are not in competition. The graph reading exhibits
$K_4$ as a finite combinatorial object. The extension reading
exhibits $K_4$ as the carrier of $d$ independent monotone directions.
Both readings concern the same survivor; they fix different aspects
of it. Subsequent chapters use whichever aspect a particular argument
requires. Numerical evaluation in the form $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$
will require both: the count of independent configurations along the
$d$ extension directions ($V^d$), the closure condition under which
the extension is well-defined ($\chi$), and the cross-direction
correction term that the extension itself carries on its boundary
($d^2$).

\textbf{Constraint:} the object that survives non-collapse, drift
non-triviality, and isotropy must simultaneously be a finite graph
and a $d$-dimensional extension structure with $\mathrm{Aut} = S_4$.

\textbf{Construction:} no separate construction is needed. The graph
$K_4$ already proven in
\hyperref[ch:graph-presentation]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:graph-presentation}}
admits the extension reading because its three ingredients ---
non-collapse, drift non-triviality, isotropy --- are precisely the
ingredients used to characterise it as a graph.

\textbf{Consequence:} the four invariants of the ledger are not four
independent numbers but the count, the connectivity, the dimensionality,
and the closure condition of one structure. The arithmetic that follows
operates on this structure, not on a tuple.

\section{What This Reading Does Not Smuggle In}
\label{sec:spatial-extension:no-smuggling}

The extension reading is structural, not metric, not analytic, and not
physical. Four readings are explicitly \emph{not} added by it.

A metric: the extension structure carries neighbour relations along
each of the $d$ directions, but no distance function is defined on
configurations. Whether two configurations are ``far'' or ``near''
is, at this stage, undefined.

A continuous topology: the extension structure is at most a graph,
or a discrete poset of configurations. Continuity is not introduced
here, and would require a separate limit construction not undertaken
in this volume.

A physical interpretation: the extension structure is invariant under
the choice of what its configurations are. They may be physical
states, types in a type theory, objects in a category, or anything else
that carries the three premises of \S\ref{sec:spatial-extension:order-of-forcing}.
The companion volume \emph{Form} undertakes one such interpretation;
this volume does not.

A scalar value: the integer $\mathcal{C} = 137$ does not yet appear at
the level of this chapter. It is a particular invariant of the
extension structure, evaluated by the arithmetic of the next part.
The fact that the formula has the form $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$ rather
than another arithmetic combination is itself forced ---
\hyperref[ch:invariant-kinds]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:invariant-kinds}} closes
that question --- but the closure occurs after the arithmetic
infrastructure is in place.

\textbf{Constraint:} the extension reading must not silently confer
metric, topological, or physical structure on the survivor.

\textbf{Elimination:} four temptations are eliminated explicitly: a
distance function, a continuum, a physical referent, and a numerical
target. Each would require an independent derivation that is not
present at this stage.

\textbf{Consequence:} every later use of the extension reading rests
exactly on what has been proved here --- monotonicity, isotropy,
$d$-dimensional extension --- and on nothing else.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\part{Derived Arithmetic}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

With the graph-theoretic core in place, the development turns to
arithmetic. The strategy is cumulative: first finite index types and
basic truth values, then natural numbers, integers, and finally the
algebraic operations required for later invariant computations.

\chapter{Arithmetic Infrastructure}
\label{ch:arithmetic-infrastructure}

This chapter gathers the elementary discrete ingredients used throughout
the arithmetic part of the book. Most of them are standard, but they are
written out explicitly so that later constructions can be traced back to
concrete definitions inside the same formal development.

\section{Truth and Finite Indices}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:truth-finite-indices}

The first ingredients are the unit type and the finite index types used
to enumerate neighbours and vertices of $K_4$. Their role is elementary
but important: later sums, recursions, and case analyses are finite, and
decidable equality on these bounded types makes that finiteness explicit.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Unit type: trivial truth witness.
data ‚ä§ : Set where
  tt : ‚ä§

-- ¬ß Three-element index (neighbours of a K‚āĄ vertex).
data Fin3 : Set where
  f0 f1 f2 : Fin3

-- ¬ß Inequality on Fin3.
Fin3‚Č† : (i j : Fin3) ‚Üí Set
Fin3‚Č† i j = i ‚Č° j ‚Üí ‚ä•

f0‚Č†f1 : Fin3‚Č† f0 f1
f0‚Č†f1 ()

f0‚Č†f2 : Fin3‚Č† f0 f2
f0‚Č†f2 ()

f1‚Č†f2 : Fin3‚Č† f1 f2
f1‚Č†f2 ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on Fin3.
Fin3-decEq : (i j : Fin3) ‚Üí (i ‚Č° j) ‚äé (Fin3‚Č† i j)
Fin3-decEq f0 f0 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin3-decEq f1 f1 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin3-decEq f2 f2 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin3-decEq f0 f1 = inj‚āā f0‚Č†f1
Fin3-decEq f1 f0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí f0‚Č†f1 (sym e))
Fin3-decEq f0 f2 = inj‚āā f0‚Č†f2
Fin3-decEq f2 f0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí f0‚Č†f2 (sym e))
Fin3-decEq f1 f2 = inj‚āā f1‚Č†f2
Fin3-decEq f2 f1 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí f1‚Č†f2 (sym e))

-- ¬ß Four-element index (vertices of K‚āĄ).
data Fin4 : Set where
  g0 g1 g2 g3 : Fin4

-- ¬ß Inequality on Fin4.
Fin4‚Č† : (i j : Fin4) ‚Üí Set
Fin4‚Č† i j = i ‚Č° j ‚Üí ‚ä•

g0‚Č†g1 : Fin4‚Č† g0 g1
g0‚Č†g1 ()

g0‚Č†g2 : Fin4‚Č† g0 g2
g0‚Č†g2 ()

g0‚Č†g3 : Fin4‚Č† g0 g3
g0‚Č†g3 ()

g1‚Č†g2 : Fin4‚Č† g1 g2
g1‚Č†g2 ()

g1‚Č†g3 : Fin4‚Č† g1 g3
g1‚Č†g3 ()

g2‚Č†g3 : Fin4‚Č† g2 g3
g2‚Č†g3 ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on Fin4.
Fin4-decEq : (i j : Fin4) ‚Üí (i ‚Č° j) ‚äé (Fin4‚Č† i j)
Fin4-decEq g0 g0 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g1 g1 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g2 g2 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g3 g3 = inj‚āĀ refl
Fin4-decEq g0 g1 = inj‚āā g0‚Č†g1
Fin4-decEq g1 g0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g0‚Č†g1 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g0 g2 = inj‚āā g0‚Č†g2
Fin4-decEq g2 g0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g0‚Č†g2 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g0 g3 = inj‚āā g0‚Č†g3
Fin4-decEq g3 g0 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g0‚Č†g3 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g1 g2 = inj‚āā g1‚Č†g2
Fin4-decEq g2 g1 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g1‚Č†g2 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g1 g3 = inj‚āā g1‚Č†g3
Fin4-decEq g3 g1 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g1‚Č†g3 (sym e))
Fin4-decEq g2 g3 = inj‚āā g2‚Č†g3
Fin4-decEq g3 g2 = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí g2‚Č†g3 (sym e))
\end{code}

\section{Natural Number Extensions and Boolean Values}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:natural-extensions-booleans}

The next block introduces the basic arithmetic and Boolean operations on
\texttt{‚Ąē}. These definitions are routine, but they fix the exact
computational behaviour used later for iteration, comparison, and finite
combinatorics.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Successor injectivity.
suc-injective : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí suc m ‚Č° suc n ‚Üí m ‚Č° n
suc-injective refl = refl

-- ¬ß Antisymmetry of ‚Č§.
‚Č§-antisym : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí m ‚Č§ n ‚Üí n ‚Č§ m ‚Üí m ‚Č° n
‚Č§-antisym {zero} {zero} z‚Č§n z‚Č§n = refl
‚Č§-antisym {zero} {suc n} z‚Č§n ()
‚Č§-antisym {suc m} {zero} () _
‚Č§-antisym {suc m} {suc n} (s‚Č§s p) (s‚Č§s q) = cong suc (‚Č§-antisym p q)

-- ¬ß Totality of ‚Č§ on ‚Ąē.
‚Č§-total : (m n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (m ‚Č§ n) ‚äé (n ‚Č§ m)
‚Č§-total zero    _       = inj‚āĀ z‚Č§n
‚Č§-total _       zero    = inj‚āā z‚Č§n
‚Č§-total (suc m) (suc n) with ‚Č§-total m n
... | inj‚āĀ p = inj‚āĀ (s‚Č§s p)
... | inj‚āā p = inj‚āā (s‚Č§s p)

-- ¬ß Boolean type with built-in binding.
data Bool : Set where
  true  : Bool
  false : Bool

{-# BUILTIN BOOL  Bool  #-}
{-# BUILTIN TRUE  true  #-}
{-# BUILTIN FALSE false #-}

{-# BUILTIN NATURAL ‚Ąē #-}

-- ¬ß Built-in addition on ‚Ąē.
infixl 6 _+_
_+_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero  + n = n
suc m + n = suc (m + n)

-- ¬ß Built-in multiplication on ‚Ąē.
infixl 7 _*_
_*_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero  * n = zero
suc m * n = n + (m * n)

-- ¬ß Built-in monus (truncated subtraction) on ‚Ąē.
infixl 6 _‚ął_
_‚ął_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero  ‚ął n     = zero
suc m ‚ął zero  = suc m
suc m ‚ął suc n = m ‚ął n

{-# BUILTIN NATPLUS  _+_ #-}
{-# BUILTIN NATTIMES _*_ #-}
{-# BUILTIN NATMINUS _‚ął_ #-}

-- ¬ß Boolean strict comparison on ‚Ąē.
_<‚Ąē-bool_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí Bool
m <‚Ąē-bool zero   = false
zero <‚Ąē-bool suc _ = true
suc m <‚Ąē-bool suc n = m <‚Ąē-bool n

{-# BUILTIN NATLESS _<‚Ąē-bool_ #-}

-- ¬ß Boolean equality on ‚Ąē.
_==‚Ąē-bool_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí Bool
zero  ==‚Ąē-bool zero    = true
zero  ==‚Ąē-bool (suc _) = false
suc _ ==‚Ąē-bool zero    = false
suc m ==‚Ąē-bool suc n   = m ==‚Ąē-bool n

{-# BUILTIN NATEQUALS _==‚Ąē-bool_ #-}
\end{code}

\section{Integer Construction}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:integer-construction}

Integers are introduced next in signed normal form, together with a pair
representation that makes addition and negation easy to define. The
normalization map cancels common positive and negative contributions and
serves as the basic mechanism for keeping integer expressions in
canonical shape.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Raw addition on ‚Ąē (separate from BUILTIN _+_).
infixl 6 _+‚Ąē_

_+‚Ąē_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
zero +‚Ąē n = n
suc m +‚Ąē n = suc (m +‚Ąē n)

-- ¬ß Integer type: signed normal form.
data ‚Ą§ : Set where
  0‚Ą§    : ‚Ą§
  +suc_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
  -suc_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§

-- ¬ß Normalization: cancel common successors.
normalize‚Ą§ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
normalize‚Ą§ zero zero = 0‚Ą§
normalize‚Ą§ (suc a) zero = +suc a
normalize‚Ą§ zero (suc b) = -suc b
normalize‚Ą§ (suc a) (suc b) = normalize‚Ą§ a b

-- ¬ß Pair representation for componentwise arithmetic.
record Pair‚Ąē : Set where
  constructor mkPair‚Ąē
  field
    pos : ‚Ąē
    neg : ‚Ąē

open Pair‚Ąē public

-- ¬ß Embedding ‚Ą§ into pairs.
toPair‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Pair‚Ąē
toPair‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = mkPair‚Ąē zero zero
toPair‚Ą§ (+suc n) = mkPair‚Ąē (suc n) zero
toPair‚Ą§ (-suc n) = mkPair‚Ąē zero (suc n)

-- ¬ß Collapsing pairs to ‚Ą§.
fromPair‚Ą§ : Pair‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
fromPair‚Ą§ p = normalize‚Ą§ (pos p) (neg p)

-- ¬ß Integer addition via pair-level componentwise sum.
infixl 6 _+‚Ą§_

_+‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
x +‚Ą§ y =
  let px = toPair‚Ą§ x in
  let py = toPair‚Ą§ y in
  normalize‚Ą§ (pos px +‚Ąē pos py) (neg px +‚Ąē neg py)

-- ¬ß Integer negation via pair swap.
neg‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
neg‚Ą§ z =
  let p = toPair‚Ą§ z in
  normalize‚Ą§ (neg p) (pos p)
\end{code}

\section{Endomorphism Iteration}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:endomorphism-iteration}

Before returning to graph-specific constructions, the text records a
generic notion of iterating an endomorphism. This small abstraction
simplifies several later recurrence arguments.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Generic endomorphism type.
GenEndo : Set ‚Üí Set
GenEndo A = A ‚Üí A

-- ¬ß Function composition.
infixr 9 _‚ąė_
_‚ąė_ : {A B C : Set} ‚Üí (B ‚Üí C) ‚Üí (A ‚Üí B) ‚Üí A ‚Üí C
(f ‚ąė g) x = f (g x)

-- ¬ß Identity endomorphism.
idGenEndo : {A : Set} ‚Üí GenEndo A
idGenEndo x = x

-- ¬ß n-fold iteration.
powEndo : {A : Set} ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí GenEndo A ‚Üí GenEndo A
powEndo zero    f = idGenEndo
powEndo (suc n) f = f ‚ąė powEndo n f

-- ¬ß Law 14I.0: Zero iteration is identity.
law14I-0-powEndo-zero : {A : Set} ‚Üí (f : GenEndo A) ‚Üí powEndo zero f ‚Čó idGenEndo
law14I-0-powEndo-zero f x = refl

-- ¬ß Law 14I.1: Successor iteration unfolds.
law14I-1-powEndo-suc : {A : Set} ‚Üí (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (f : GenEndo A) ‚Üí powEndo (suc n) f ‚Čó (f ‚ąė powEndo n f)
law14I-1-powEndo-suc n f x = refl
\end{code}

\section{Integer Summation and Absolute Value}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:integer-summation-absolute-value}

The next definitions package several small pieces of integer
bookkeeping: finite sums over \texttt{Fin3} and \texttt{Fin4}, scalar
multiples, and absolute value.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Three-element sum.
sum3‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sum3‚Ą§ a b c = a +‚Ą§ (b +‚Ą§ c)

-- ¬ß Sum indexed by Fin3.
sumFin3‚Ą§ : (Fin3 ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sumFin3‚Ą§ f = sum3‚Ą§ (f f0) (f f1) (f f2)

-- ¬ß Four-element sum.
sum4‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sum4‚Ą§ a b c d = a +‚Ą§ (b +‚Ą§ (c +‚Ą§ d))

-- ¬ß Sum indexed by Fin4.
sumFin4‚Ą§ : (Fin4 ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí ‚Ą§
sumFin4‚Ą§ f = sum4‚Ą§ (f g0) (f g1) (f g2) (f g3)

-- ¬ß Scalar triple.
threeTimes‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
threeTimes‚Ą§ x = x +‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ x)

-- ¬ß Scalar quadruple.
fourTimes‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
fourTimes‚Ą§ x = sum4‚Ą§ x x x x

-- ¬ß Absolute value on ‚Ą§.
abs‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = 0‚Ą§
abs‚Ą§ (+suc n) = +suc n
abs‚Ą§ (-suc n) = +suc n
\end{code}

\section{Integer Order}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:integer-order}

An explicit order on integers is also needed. The definition below works
directly with the signed normal form and provides the non-ordering
relation used in strict comparison. The construction adds no independent
order axiom: it is obtained by case analysis on the constructors of
\texttt{‚Ą§}, using the order already encoded by the signed normal form.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Integer ordering.
infix 4 _‚Č§‚Ą§_ _<‚Ą§_

_‚Č§‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Set
0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = ‚ä§
0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc n) = ‚ä§
0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc n) = ‚ä•
(+suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = ‚ä•
(+suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc n) = suc m ‚Č§ suc n
(+suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc n) = ‚ä•
(-suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = ‚ä§
(-suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc n) = ‚ä§
(-suc m) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc n) = suc n ‚Č§ suc m

-- ¬ß Non-ordering witness.
_‚Čį‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Set
x ‚Čį‚Ą§ y = (x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y) ‚Üí ‚ä•

-- ¬ß Strict integer ordering.
_<‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí Set
_<‚Ą§_ x y = (x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y) √ó (y ‚Čį‚Ą§ x)
\end{code}

\section{K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Vertex Indexing}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:k4-vertex-indexing}

To move between finite combinatorics and the four endomorphism cases,
the development fixes an explicit bijection between \texttt{Fin4} and
\texttt{EndoCase}. This supplies a concrete vertex indexing for $K_4$;
the graph itself has already been fixed by the classification theorem.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Fin4 ‚Üí EndoCase bijection.
vertexAt : Fin4 ‚Üí EndoCase
vertexAt g0 = case-constL
vertexAt g1 = case-constR
vertexAt g2 = case-id
vertexAt g3 = case-dual

-- ¬ß EndoCase ‚Üí Fin4 inverse.
vertexIndex : EndoCase ‚Üí Fin4
vertexIndex case-constL = g0
vertexIndex case-constR = g1
vertexIndex case-id     = g2
vertexIndex case-dual   = g3

-- ¬ß Round-trip: vertexAt ‚ąė vertexIndex = id.
vertexAt-index : (v : EndoCase) ‚Üí vertexAt (vertexIndex v) ‚Č° v
vertexAt-index case-constL = refl
vertexAt-index case-constR = refl
vertexAt-index case-id     = refl
vertexAt-index case-dual   = refl

-- ¬ß Round-trip: vertexIndex ‚ąė vertexAt = id.
index-vertexAt : (i : Fin4) ‚Üí vertexIndex (vertexAt i) ‚Č° i
index-vertexAt g0 = refl
index-vertexAt g1 = refl
index-vertexAt g2 = refl
index-vertexAt g3 = refl
\end{code}

\section{Endomorphism Systems}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:endomorphism-systems}

The final preparatory block introduces endomorphism systems and their
morphisms. These abstractions later allow universal properties and
iteration statements to be expressed without repeating the same
commutation argument at each occurrence.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Setoid: carrier with equivalence relation.
record ESetoid : Set1 where
  field
    Obj    : Set
    Rel    : Obj ‚Üí Obj ‚Üí Set
    refl‚Čą  : (x : Obj) ‚Üí Rel x x
    sym‚Čą   : {x y : Obj} ‚Üí Rel x y ‚Üí Rel y x
    trans‚Čą : {x y z : Obj} ‚Üí Rel x y ‚Üí Rel y z ‚Üí Rel x z

open ESetoid public

-- ¬ß Endomorphism system: setoid + step.
record EndoSystem : Set1 where
  field
    ES    : ESetoid
    step : Obj ES ‚Üí Obj ES
    step-cong : {x y : Obj ES} ‚Üí Rel ES x y ‚Üí Rel ES (step x) (step y)

open EndoSystem public

-- ¬ß Morphism of endomorphism systems.
record ESHom (X Y : EndoSystem) : Set1 where
  field
    esmap  : Obj (ES X) ‚Üí Obj (ES Y)
    esmap-cong : {x y : Obj (ES X)} ‚Üí Rel (ES X) x y ‚Üí Rel (ES Y) (esmap x) (esmap y)
    escomm : (x : Obj (ES X)) ‚Üí Rel (ES Y) (esmap (step X x)) (step Y (esmap x))

open ESHom public

-- ¬ß Terminal endomorphism system.
record IsTerminal (T : EndoSystem) : Set1 where
  field
    !    : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí ESHom X T
    uniq : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí (f g : ESHom X T) ‚Üí (x : Obj (ES X)) ‚Üí Rel (ES T) (esmap f x) (esmap g x)

-- ¬ß Initial endomorphism system.
record IsInitial (I : EndoSystem) : Set1 where
  field
    !    : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí ESHom I X
    uniq : (X : EndoSystem) ‚Üí (f g : ESHom I X) ‚Üí (x : Obj (ES I)) ‚Üí Rel (ES X) (esmap f x) (esmap g x)

-- ¬ß Law 14J.0: Morphisms commute with n-fold iteration.
law14J-0-hom-iter :
  {X Y : EndoSystem} ‚Üí
  (h : ESHom X Y) ‚Üí
  (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí
  (x : Obj (ES X)) ‚Üí
  Rel (ES Y) (esmap h (powEndo n (step X) x)) (powEndo n (step Y) (esmap h x))
law14J-0-hom-iter {X} {Y} h zero x = refl‚Čą (ES Y) (esmap h x)
law14J-0-hom-iter {X} {Y} h (suc n) x =
  trans‚Čą (ES Y)
    (escomm h (powEndo n (step X) x))
    (step-cong Y (law14J-0-hom-iter h n x))
\end{code}

\subsection{Classification as Representability}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:classification-representability}

This section recasts the earlier classification theorem as a
representability statement. The four constructors of
\texttt{EndoCase} are not merely labels for previously separated cases;
under the soundness and uniqueness laws already proved, they provide a
complete coordinate system for the endomorphisms of a distinction. The
record below packages the forward interpretation, the reverse
classification, and the two round-trip laws into a single formal object.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The classification bijection as a universal property
record EndoRepresentability (d : Distinction) : Set where
  field
    label-to-endo   : EndoCase ‚Üí (S d ‚Üí S d)
    endo-to-label   : (S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí EndoCase
    roundtrip-sound : (f : S d ‚Üí S d) ‚Üí label-to-endo (endo-to-label f) ‚Čó f
    roundtrip-label : (c : EndoCase) ‚Üí endo-to-label (label-to-endo c) ‚Č° c
    label-injective : (c‚āĀ c‚āā : EndoCase) ‚Üí
      label-to-endo c‚āĀ ‚Čó label-to-endo c‚āā ‚Üí c‚āĀ ‚Č° c‚āā

theorem-endo-representability : (d : Distinction) ‚Üí EndoRepresentability d
theorem-endo-representability d = record
  { label-to-endo   = K‚āĄ.interpret d
  ; endo-to-label   = K‚āĄ.classify d
  ; roundtrip-sound = K‚āĄ.classify-sound d
  ; roundtrip-label = őĽ c ‚Üí
      sym (K‚āĄ.classify-unique d (K‚āĄ.interpret d c) c (K‚āĄ.‚Čó-refl d))
  ; label-injective = K‚āĄ.interpret-injective d
  }
\end{code}

\section{K\texorpdfstring{$_4$}{4} Coupling Infrastructure}
\label{sec:arithmetic-infrastructure:k4-coupling-infrastructure}

The next constructions describe couplings between copies of the four
endomorphism classes. Since the carrier has only four elements, every
relevant transformation can be written out explicitly, and the finitary
character of the argument remains completely visible.

\paragraph{Step 1: EndoCase Inequality and Decidable Equality.}
The first step is elementary: inequality and decidable equality are
recorded directly on \texttt{EndoCase} itself. The later symmetry
arguments require no hidden enumeration principle; each impossible
equality is discharged by an absurd pattern.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Inequality on EndoCase.
EndoCase‚Č† : (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí Set
EndoCase‚Č† a b = a ‚Č° b ‚Üí ‚ä•

case-constL‚Č†case-constR : EndoCase‚Č† case-constL case-constR
case-constL‚Č†case-constR ()

case-constL‚Č†case-id : EndoCase‚Č† case-constL case-id
case-constL‚Č†case-id ()

case-constL‚Č†case-dual : EndoCase‚Č† case-constL case-dual
case-constL‚Č†case-dual ()

case-constR‚Č†case-id : EndoCase‚Č† case-constR case-id
case-constR‚Č†case-id ()

case-constR‚Č†case-dual : EndoCase‚Č† case-constR case-dual
case-constR‚Č†case-dual ()

case-id‚Č†case-dual : EndoCase‚Č† case-id case-dual
case-id‚Č†case-dual ()

-- ¬ß Decidable equality on EndoCase.
EndoCase-decEq : (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí (a ‚Č° b) ‚äé (EndoCase‚Č† a b)
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-constL = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-constR = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-id     = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-dual   = inj‚āĀ refl
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-constR = inj‚āā case-constL‚Č†case-constR
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-constL = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constL‚Č†case-constR (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-id     = inj‚āā case-constL‚Č†case-id
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-constL = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constL‚Č†case-id (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constL case-dual   = inj‚āā case-constL‚Č†case-dual
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-constL = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constL‚Č†case-dual (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-id     = inj‚āā case-constR‚Č†case-id
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-constR = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constR‚Č†case-id (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-constR case-dual   = inj‚āā case-constR‚Č†case-dual
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-constR = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-constR‚Č†case-dual (sym e))
EndoCase-decEq case-id     case-dual   = inj‚āā case-id‚Č†case-dual
EndoCase-decEq case-dual   case-id     = inj‚āā (őĽ e ‚Üí case-id‚Č†case-dual (sym e))
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: Transposition on EndoCase.}
The next ingredient is a transposition on \texttt{EndoCase}. Because the
carrier has only four elements, the swap can be defined by complete case
distinction, and its involutive character can be checked by the same
explicit enumeration.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Transposition on EndoCase: swaps x‚ÜĒy, fixes others.
swapEndo : EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase
swapEndo case-constL case-constL z = z
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-constL = case-constR
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-constR = case-constL
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constL case-constR case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-constL = case-id
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-id     = case-constL
swapEndo case-constL case-id     case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-constL = case-dual
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constL case-dual   case-dual   = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-constL = case-constR
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-constR = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constR case-constL case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constR case-constR z = z
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-constR = case-id
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-id     = case-constR
swapEndo case-constR case-id     case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-constR = case-dual
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-constR case-dual   case-dual   = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-constL = case-id
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-id     = case-constL
swapEndo case-id case-constL case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-constR = case-id
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-id     = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-constR case-dual   = case-dual
swapEndo case-id case-id z = z
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-id     = case-dual
swapEndo case-id case-dual case-dual   = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-constL = case-dual
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-constL case-dual   = case-constL
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-constR = case-dual
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-id     = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-constR case-dual   = case-constR
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-constL = case-constL
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-constR = case-constR
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-id     = case-dual
swapEndo case-dual case-id     case-dual   = case-id
swapEndo case-dual case-dual z = z

-- ¬ß swapEndo is an involution (64-case proof).
swapEndo-involutive : (x y z : EndoCase) ‚Üí swapEndo x y (swapEndo x y z) ‚Č° z
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constL z = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constL case-dual   case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-constR z = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-constR case-dual   case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-id z = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-id case-dual case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-involutive case-dual case-dual z = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 3: Transposition Sends Source to Target.}
Once the transposition has been defined, the basic transport fact is
immediate: swapping \texttt{x} with \texttt{y} sends \texttt{x} to
\texttt{y}. This elementary observation is the mechanism behind the
permutation arguments used below.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß swapEndo sends x to y.
swapEndo-sends : (x y : EndoCase) ‚Üí swapEndo x y x ‚Č° y
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constL case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-constR case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-id     case-dual   = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-constL = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-constR = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-id     = refl
swapEndo-sends case-dual   case-dual   = refl
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 4: Permutations, Couplings, and Cross-Invariance.}
These transpositions are then packaged as permutations of
\texttt{EndoCase}. With that in place, a coupling is simply a relation
between two copies of the carrier, and cross-invariance means that this
relation is preserved under independent permutations in the two
coordinates.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Permutation record on EndoCase.
record EndoPerm : Set where
  field
    eto       : EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase
    efrom     : EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase
    eto-efrom : (y : EndoCase) ‚Üí eto (efrom y) ‚Č° y
    efrom-eto : (x : EndoCase) ‚Üí efrom (eto x) ‚Č° x

open EndoPerm public

-- ¬ß Transposition as permutation.
permSwap : (x y : EndoCase) ‚Üí EndoPerm
permSwap x y = record
  { eto = swapEndo x y
  ; efrom = swapEndo x y
  ; eto-efrom = swapEndo-involutive x y
  ; efrom-eto = swapEndo-involutive x y
  }

-- ¬ß Any vertex can be sent to any other by some permutation.
endoPerm-send : (a a' : EndoCase) ‚Üí ő£ EndoPerm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí eto ŌÉ a ‚Č° a')
endoPerm-send a a' = (permSwap a a' , swapEndo-sends a a')

-- ¬ß Coupling: relation between two copies of EndoCase.
Coupling : Set1
Coupling = EndoCase ‚Üí EndoCase ‚Üí Set

-- ¬ß Cross-invariance: coupling respected by independent permutations.
CrossInv : Coupling ‚Üí Set
CrossInv C = (ŌÉ ŌĄ : EndoPerm) ‚Üí (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí C a b ‚Üí C (eto ŌÉ a) (eto ŌĄ b)

-- ¬ß Heterogeneous transport over a two-argument predicate.
transport2 : {A B : Set} {P : A ‚Üí B ‚Üí Set} {a a' : A} {b b' : B} ‚Üí a ‚Č° a' ‚Üí b ‚Č° b' ‚Üí P a b ‚Üí P a' b'
transport2 {P = P} {a = a} {a' = a'} {b = b} {b' = b'} ea eb pab =
  subst (őĽ a0 ‚Üí P a0 b') ea (subst (őĽ b0 ‚Üí P a b0) eb pab)
\end{code}

\subsection{Law 14F.0: A Single Cross-Invariant Edge Forces All Edges}
\label{law:coupling:edge-forces-all}

Cross-invariance is extremely rigid in this setting. If one cross-edge
exists, permutations of the two factors move that edge to any prescribed
pair of vertices, so the coupling is full under the stated invariance
hypothesis. Dually, if one edge is absent under the same hypothesis, then
every edge is absent.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 14F.0: One edge forces all.
law14F-0-edge-forces-all : (C : Coupling) ‚Üí CrossInv C ‚Üí
  ő£ EndoCase (őĽ a0 ‚Üí ő£ EndoCase (őĽ b0 ‚Üí C a0 b0)) ‚Üí
  (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí C a b
law14F-0-edge-forces-all C inv (a0 , (b0 , c0)) a b =
  let sa = endoPerm-send a0 a in
  let sb = endoPerm-send b0 b in
  let ŌÉ = fst sa in
  let ŌĄ = fst sb in
  transport2 {P = C} (snd sa) (snd sb) (inv ŌÉ ŌĄ a0 b0 c0)

-- ¬ß Law 14F.1: One missing edge forces no edges.
law14F-1-nonedge-forces-none : (C : Coupling) ‚Üí CrossInv C ‚Üí
  ő£ EndoCase (őĽ a0 ‚Üí ő£ EndoCase (őĽ b0 ‚Üí ¬¨ (C a0 b0))) ‚Üí
  (a b : EndoCase) ‚Üí ¬¨ (C a b)
law14F-1-nonedge-forces-none C inv (a0 , (b0 , n0)) a b cab =
  let sa = endoPerm-send a a0 in
  let sb = endoPerm-send b b0 in
  let ŌÉ = fst sa in
  let ŌĄ = fst sb in
  n0 (transport2 {P = C} (snd sa) (snd sb) (inv ŌÉ ŌĄ a b cab))
\end{code}

\subsection{Self-Coupling and the Squaring Law}
\label{law:coupling:self-coupling-square}

For self-couplings this rigidity has a simple numerical consequence. A
full coupling on a carrier of size $n$ contains $n^2$ ordered pairs, and
