    (*‚Ą§-neg-left (+suc m) (+suc k))
    (trans
      (cong neg‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m k))
      refl)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: Multiplication by positive $\mathbb{N}^+$ preserves $\leq_{\mathbb{Z}}$.}
These shape lemmas are then used to show that multiplication by a
positive element of $\mathbb{N}^+$ preserves order on the right.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Multiplication by positive ‚Ąē‚Āļ preserves ‚Č§‚Ą§ (9-case proof).
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (d : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y ‚Üí (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right 0‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) _ =
  subst
    (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§‚Ą§ t)
    (sym (*‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc k)))
    tt
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right 0‚Ą§ (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) _ =
  let
    t = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)
    eqL : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č° 0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ (+suc k)
    eqL = sym (*‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc k))

    eqR : (+suc t) ‚Č° ((+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    eqR = sym (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k)

    base : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc t)
    base = tt
  in
  subst (őĽ r ‚Üí (0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ r) eqR
    (subst (őĽ l ‚Üí l ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc t)) eqL base)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right 0‚Ą§ (-suc n) d ()
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (+suc m) 0‚Ą§ d ()
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (+suc m) (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) (s‚Č§s p) =
  let
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    t‚āā = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)

    mulMono : (m *‚Ąē suc k) ‚Č§ (n *‚Ąē suc k)
    mulMono = ‚Č§-*‚Ąē-mono ≥ p (suc k)

    addMono : t‚āĀ ‚Č§ t‚āā
    addMono = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-monoň° mulMono k

    base : (+suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc t‚āā)
    base = s‚Č§s addMono
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m k))
    (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k)) base)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (+suc m) (-suc n) d ()
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (-suc m) 0‚Ą§ (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) _ =
  let
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    eqL : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č° ((-suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    eqL = sym (*‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq m k)

    eqR : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č° (0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    eqR = sym (*‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc k))

    base : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    base = tt
  in
  subst (őĽ r ‚Üí ((-suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ r) eqR
    (subst (őĽ l ‚Üí l ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§) eqL base)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (-suc m) (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) _ =
  let
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    t‚āā = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)
    eqL : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č° ((-suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    eqL = sym (*‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq m k)

    eqR : (+suc t‚āā) ‚Č° ((+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    eqR = sym (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k)

    base : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc t‚āā)
    base = tt
  in
  subst (őĽ r ‚Üí ((-suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ r) eqR
    (subst (őĽ l ‚Üí l ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc t‚āā)) eqL base)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (-suc m) (-suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) (s‚Č§s p) =
  let
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    t‚āā = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)

    mulMono : (n *‚Ąē suc k) ‚Č§ (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    mulMono = ‚Č§-*‚Ąē-mono ≥ p (suc k)

    addMono : t‚āā ‚Č§ t‚āĀ
    addMono = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-monoň° mulMono k

    base : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc t‚āā)
    base = s‚Č§s addMono
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym (*‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq m k))
    (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym (*‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq n k)) base)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 3: Cancellation of positive right factor.}
Once monotonicity for positive right factors is known, the corresponding
cancellation law can be proved by reversing the same analysis. The
argument again proceeds by a case distinction on the signs of the two
compared integers.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Cancellation: if x¬∑d ‚Č§ y¬∑d for positive d, then x ‚Č§ y (9-case proof).
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (d : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right 0‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p = tt
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right 0‚Ą§ (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p = tt
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right 0‚Ą§ (-suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p =
  let
    t : ‚Ąē
    t = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)

    rhsEq : ((-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č° (-suc t)
    rhsEq = *‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq n k

    p0 : (0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ ((-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    p0 = p

    p1 : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ ((-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    p1 = subst (őĽ s ‚Üí s ‚Č§‚Ą§ ((-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))) (*‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc k)) p0

    p' : 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc t)
    p' = subst (őĽ r ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ r) rhsEq p1
  in
  ‚ä•-elim p'
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right (+suc m) 0‚Ą§ (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p =
  let
    t = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    lhsPos : ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č° +suc t
    lhsPos = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m k

    p0 : ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    p0 = p

    p1 : ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    p1 = subst (őĽ r ‚Üí ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ r) (*‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc k)) p0

    p' : (+suc t) ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    p' = subst (őĽ s ‚Üí s ‚Č§‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§) lhsPos p1
  in
  ‚ä•-elim p'
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right (+suc m) (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p =
  let
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    t‚āā = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)

    lhsEq : (+suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č° ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    lhsEq = sym (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m k)

    rhsEq : (+suc t‚āā) ‚Č° ((+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    rhsEq = sym (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k)

    step : (+suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc t‚āā)
    step =
      ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym lhsEq)
        (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym rhsEq) p)

    natStep : suc t‚āĀ ‚Č§ suc t‚āā
    natStep = step

    t‚āĀ‚Č§t‚āā : t‚āĀ ‚Č§ t‚āā
    t‚āĀ‚Č§t‚āā = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-cancelň° (suc zero) t‚āĀ t‚āā natStep

    mulPart : (m *‚Ąē suc k) ‚Č§ (n *‚Ąē suc k)
    mulPart = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-cancelň° k (m *‚Ąē suc k) (n *‚Ąē suc k) t‚āĀ‚Č§t‚āā

    base : m ‚Č§ n
    base = ‚Č§-*‚Ąē-cancel ≥-suc k mulPart
  in
  s‚Č§s base
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right (+suc m) (-suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p =
  let
    t‚āĀ : ‚Ąē
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)

    t‚āā : ‚Ąē
    t‚āā = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)

    lhsPos : ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č° (+suc t‚āĀ)
    lhsPos = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m k

    rhsNeg : ((-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č° (-suc t‚āā)
    rhsNeg = *‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq n k

    p1 : ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc t‚āā)
    p1 = ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ rhsNeg p

    p2 : (+suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc t‚āā)
    p2 = subst (őĽ s ‚Üí s ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc t‚āā)) lhsPos p1
  in
  ‚ä•-elim p2
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right (-suc m) 0‚Ą§ (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p = tt
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right (-suc m) (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p = tt
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right (-suc m) (-suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) p =
  let
    t‚āĀ = k +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    t‚āā = k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k)

    lhsEq : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č° ((-suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    lhsEq = sym (*‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq m k)

    rhsEq : (-suc t‚āā) ‚Č° ((-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k))
    rhsEq = sym (*‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq n k)

    step : (-suc t‚āĀ) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (-suc t‚āā)
    step =
      ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym lhsEq)
        (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym rhsEq) p)

    natStep : suc t‚āā ‚Č§ suc t‚āĀ
    natStep = step

    t‚āā‚Č§t‚āĀ : t‚āā ‚Č§ t‚āĀ
    t‚āā‚Č§t‚āĀ = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-cancelň° (suc zero) t‚āā t‚āĀ natStep

    mulPart : (n *‚Ąē suc k) ‚Č§ (m *‚Ąē suc k)
    mulPart = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-cancelň° k (n *‚Ąē suc k) (m *‚Ąē suc k) t‚āā‚Č§t‚āĀ

    base : n ‚Č§ m
    base = ‚Č§-*‚Ąē-cancel ≥-suc k mulPart
  in
  s‚Č§s base
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: Nonneg and Strict Factor Monotonicity.}
From the positive-factor case one immediately obtains monotonicity for
arbitrary nonnegative right factors, and then strict monotonicity for
positive factors.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Nonneg right factor preserves ‚Č§‚Ą§.
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-nonneg-right : (x y z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ z ‚Üí (x *‚Ą§ z) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ z)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-nonneg-right x y 0‚Ą§ x‚Č§y _ =
  subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§)) (sym (*‚Ą§-zero-right x))
    (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ t) (sym (*‚Ą§-zero-right y)) tt)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-nonneg-right x y (+suc k) x‚Č§y _ =
  let
    d : ‚Ąē‚Āļ
    d = mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k

    step : (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
    step = ‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right x y d x‚Č§y

    lhs : (x *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č° (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
    lhs = refl

    rhs : (y *‚Ą§ (+suc k)) ‚Č° (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
    rhs = refl
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym lhs) (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym rhs) step)
‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-nonneg-right x y (-suc k) _ ()

-- ¬ß Integer squares are always nonneg: 0 ‚Č§ a * a.
square‚Ą§-nonneg : (a : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ a *‚Ą§ a
square‚Ą§-nonneg 0‚Ą§ =
  subst (0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§_) (sym (*‚Ą§-zero-left 0‚Ą§)) tt
square‚Ą§-nonneg (+suc n) =
  subst (_‚Č§‚Ą§ ((+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc n))) (*‚Ą§-zero-left (+suc n))
    (‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-nonneg-right 0‚Ą§ (+suc n) (+suc n) tt tt)
square‚Ą§-nonneg (-suc n) =
  let
    chain : (-suc n) *‚Ą§ (-suc n) ‚Č° (+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)
    chain = trans
      (*‚Ą§-neg-left (+suc n) (-suc n))
      (trans
        (cong neg‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-neg-right (+suc n) (+suc n)))
        (neg‚Ą§-involutive ((+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc n))))
  in
  subst (0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§_) (sym chain) (square‚Ą§-nonneg (+suc n))

-- ¬ß Strict order preserved by positive right factor.
<‚Ą§-mul-pos-right : {x y : ‚Ą§} ‚Üí (d : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí x <‚Ą§ y ‚Üí (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) <‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
<‚Ą§-mul-pos-right {x} {y} d (x‚Č§y , y‚Čįx) =
  let
    lePart : (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
    lePart = ‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-right x y d x‚Č§y

    notRev : (y *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Čį‚Ą§ (x *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
    notRev ydx‚Č§xdx = y‚Čįx (‚Č§‚Ą§-mul-pos-cancel-right y x d ydx‚Č§xdx)
  in
  lePart , notRev
\end{code}

\chapter{Absolute Value Laws}
\label{ch:absolute-value-laws}

The next chapter develops the algebraic properties of absolute value.
The aim is to connect the order-theoretic notion of magnitude with the
additive and multiplicative structure already established on
\texttt{‚Ą§}.

\section{Basic Properties}
\label{sec:absolute-value:basic-properties}

We begin with the elementary identities: behaviour at zero, invariance
under negation, idempotence, nonnegativity, and the fact that every
integer is bounded by its absolute value.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß abs of zero.
abs‚Ą§-zero : abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
abs‚Ą§-zero = refl

-- ¬ß abs absorbs negation.
abs‚Ą§-neg : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ (neg‚Ą§ z) ‚Č° abs‚Ą§ z
abs‚Ą§-neg 0‚Ą§ = refl
abs‚Ą§-neg (+suc n) = refl
abs‚Ą§-neg (-suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß abs is idempotent.
abs‚Ą§-idem : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ (abs‚Ą§ z) ‚Č° abs‚Ą§ z
abs‚Ą§-idem 0‚Ą§ = refl
abs‚Ą§-idem (+suc n) = refl
abs‚Ą§-idem (-suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß abs is nonneg.
abs‚Ą§-nonneg : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ z
abs‚Ą§-nonneg 0‚Ą§ = tt
abs‚Ą§-nonneg (+suc n) = tt
abs‚Ą§-nonneg (-suc n) = tt

-- ¬ß Every integer is bounded by its absolute value.
‚Č§‚Ą§-abs‚Ą§ : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí z ‚Č§‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ z
‚Č§‚Ą§-abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = tt
‚Č§‚Ą§-abs‚Ą§ (+suc n) = ‚Č§-refl (suc n)
‚Č§‚Ą§-abs‚Ą§ (-suc n) = tt

-- ¬ß abs zero implies zero.
abs‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ z ‚Č° 0‚Ą§ ‚Üí z ‚Č° 0‚Ą§
abs‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero 0‚Ą§ _ = refl
abs‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero (+suc n) ()
abs‚Ą§-zero‚Üízero (-suc n) ()
\end{code}

\section{Magnitude and Subadditivity}
\label{sec:absolute-value:magnitude-subadditivity}

To prepare the triangle inequality, absolute value is related to the
natural-number magnitude obtained by forgetting the sign. This makes it
possible to prove subadditivity by reducing the problem to an estimate
on \texttt{normalize‚Ą§}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Natural magnitude.
mag‚Ą§ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ąē
mag‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = zero
mag‚Ą§ (+suc n) = suc n
mag‚Ą§ (-suc n) = suc n

-- ¬ß Embedding ‚Ąē into ‚Ą§.
from‚Ąē‚Ą§ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ą§
from‚Ąē‚Ą§ zero = 0‚Ą§
from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (suc n) = +suc n

-- ¬ß abs equals from‚Ąē‚Ą§ of magnitude.
abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ z ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ z)
abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ = refl
abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ (+suc n) = refl
abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ (-suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß Transport ‚Č§ along right equality.
‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥ : {a b c : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí a ‚Č§ b ‚Üí b ‚Č° c ‚Üí a ‚Č§ c
‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥ {a} p eq = subst (őĽ t ‚Üí a ‚Č§ t) eq p

-- ¬ß Successor weakening.
‚Č§-weaken-suc ≥ : {a b : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí a ‚Č§ b ‚Üí a ‚Č§ suc b
‚Č§-weaken-suc ≥ {a} {b} p = ‚Č§-trans p (‚Č§-step b)

-- ¬ß Double successor weakening.
‚Č§-weaken-suc¬≤ ≥ : {a b : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí a ‚Č§ b ‚Üí a ‚Č§ suc (suc b)
‚Č§-weaken-suc¬≤ ≥ p = ‚Č§-weaken-suc ≥ (‚Č§-weaken-suc ≥ p)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: Subadditivity Core.}
The core estimate is that normalization cannot increase total
magnitude. Once this has been established, the triangle inequality
follows by transporting the result through the embedding from
\texttt{‚Ąē} to \texttt{‚Ą§}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß mag of normalize‚Ą§ is bounded by input sum.
magNormalize‚Č§sum : (a b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí mag‚Ą§ (normalize‚Ą§ a b) ‚Č§ (a +‚Ąē b)
magNormalize‚Č§sum zero zero = ‚Č§-refl zero
magNormalize‚Č§sum (suc a) zero =
  ‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥
    (‚Č§-refl (suc a))
    (sym (+‚Ąē-zero-right (suc a)))
magNormalize‚Č§sum zero (suc b) = ‚Č§-refl (suc b)
magNormalize‚Č§sum (suc a) (suc b) =
  ‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥
    (‚Č§-weaken-suc¬≤ ≥ (magNormalize‚Č§sum a b))
    rhs
  where
    rhs : suc (suc (a +‚Ąē b)) ‚Č° (suc a +‚Ąē suc b)
    rhs = sym (cong suc (+‚Ąē-suc-right a b))

-- ¬ß Magnitude is subadditive for +‚Ą§.
mag‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-subadd : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí mag‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y) ‚Č§ (mag‚Ą§ x +‚Ąē mag‚Ą§ y)
mag‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-subadd x y =
  ‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥
    (magNormalize‚Č§sum (pos px +‚Ąē pos py) (neg px +‚Ąē neg py))
    sumReassoc
  where
    px : Pair‚Ąē
    px = toPair‚Ą§ x

    py : Pair‚Ąē
    py = toPair‚Ą§ y

    cong‚āā : {A B C : Set} ‚Üí (f : A ‚Üí B ‚Üí C) ‚Üí {a a' : A} ‚Üí {b b' : B} ‚Üí a ‚Č° a' ‚Üí b ‚Č° b' ‚Üí f a b ‚Č° f a' b'
    cong‚āā f refl refl = refl

    pairSumMag : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (pos (toPair‚Ą§ z) +‚Ąē neg (toPair‚Ą§ z)) ‚Č° mag‚Ą§ z
    pairSumMag 0‚Ą§ = refl
    pairSumMag (+suc n) = +‚Ąē-zero-right (suc n)
    pairSumMag (-suc n) = refl

    pairSumMagPx : (pos px +‚Ąē neg px) ‚Č° mag‚Ą§ x
    pairSumMagPx = pairSumMag x

    pairSumMagPy : (pos py +‚Ąē neg py) ‚Č° mag‚Ą§ y
    pairSumMagPy = pairSumMag y

    sumReassoc :
      ((pos px +‚Ąē pos py) +‚Ąē (neg px +‚Ąē neg py))
        ‚Č°
      (mag‚Ą§ x +‚Ąē mag‚Ą§ y)
    sumReassoc =
      trans
        (shuffle‚Ąē (pos px) (pos py) (neg px) (neg py))
        (cong‚āā _+‚Ąē_ pairSumMagPx pairSumMagPy)

-- ¬ß Transport ‚Ąē-‚Č§ into ‚Č§‚Ą§ for nonneg integers.
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí m ‚Č§ n ‚Üí from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m ‚Č§‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono {zero} {zero} _ = tt
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono {zero} {suc n} _ = tt
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono {suc m} {zero} ()
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono {suc m} {suc n} p = p

-- ¬ß from‚Ąē‚Ą§ is additive.
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ : (m n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (m +‚Ąē n)
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ zero zero = refl
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ zero (suc n) = refl
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ (suc m) zero = refl
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ (suc m) (suc n) = refl

-- ¬ß abs is subadditive on ‚Ą§ (triangle core).
abs‚Ą§-subadd : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (abs‚Ą§ x +‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ y)
abs‚Ą§-subadd x y =
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym lhsEq) (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym rhsEq) step‚āĀ)
  where
    step‚āĀ : from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ x +‚Ąē mag‚Ą§ y)
    step‚āĀ = from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono (mag‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-subadd x y)

    lhsEq : abs‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y) ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y))
    lhsEq = abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ (x +‚Ą§ y)

    rhsEq : abs‚Ą§ x +‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ y ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ x +‚Ąē mag‚Ą§ y)
    rhsEq =
      trans
        (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ y) (abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ x))
        (trans
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ x) +‚Ą§ t) (abs‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mag‚Ą§ y))
          (from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ (mag‚Ą§ x) (mag‚Ą§ y)))
\end{code}

\section{Multiplicative Compatibility}
\label{sec:absolute-value:multiplicative-compatibility}

Absolute value is also compatible with multiplication. The first step
treats multiplication by a positive natural, after which full
multiplicativity follows by a direct sign analysis on the two factors.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß abs commutes with multiplication by positive ‚Ąē‚Āļ.
abs‚Ą§-mul-pos-right : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (d : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ (z *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č° (abs‚Ą§ z *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)
abs‚Ą§-mul-pos-right 0‚Ą§ d =
  trans
    (cong abs‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-zero-left (‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)))
    (sym (*‚Ą§-zero-left (‚Āļto‚Ą§ d)))
abs‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (+suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) =
  let mulPosForm : (+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k) ‚Č° +suc (k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k))
      mulPosForm = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k
  in
  trans
    (trans (cong abs‚Ą§ mulPosForm) refl)
    (sym mulPosForm)

abs‚Ą§-mul-pos-right (-suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) =
  let mulNegForm : (-suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k) ‚Č° -suc (k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k))
      mulNegForm = *‚Ą§-neg-pos-eq n k
      mulPosForm : (+suc n) *‚Ą§ (+suc k) ‚Č° +suc (k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k))
      mulPosForm = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k
  in
  trans
    (trans (cong abs‚Ą§ mulNegForm) refl)
    (sym mulPosForm)
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: Full Multiplicativity.}
With the positive-factor case in hand, the general statement is obtained
by splitting into the four sign combinations.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß abs is fully multiplicative.
abs‚Ą§-mul : (x y : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ (x *‚Ą§ y) ‚Č° (abs‚Ą§ x *‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ y)
abs‚Ą§-mul 0‚Ą§ y =
  let
    lhs : abs‚Ą§ (0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ y) ‚Č° abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    lhs = cong abs‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-zero-left y)

    rhs : (abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ *‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ y) ‚Č° abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    rhs = *‚Ą§-zero-left (abs‚Ą§ y)
  in
  trans lhs (sym rhs)
abs‚Ą§-mul x 0‚Ą§ =
  let
    lhs : abs‚Ą§ (x *‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§) ‚Č° abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    lhs = cong abs‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-zero-right x)

    rhs : (abs‚Ą§ x *‚Ą§ abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§) ‚Č° abs‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§
    rhs =
      trans
        (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ x *‚Ą§ t) abs‚Ą§-zero)
        (*‚Ą§-zero-right (abs‚Ą§ x))
  in
  trans lhs (sym rhs)
abs‚Ą§-mul (+suc m) (+suc n) =
  let
    prodEq : (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n) ‚Č° +suc (n +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc n))
    prodEq = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m n
  in
  trans (cong abs‚Ą§ prodEq) (sym prodEq)
abs‚Ą§-mul (+suc m) (-suc n) =
  let
    prodEq : (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n) ‚Č° +suc (n +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc n))
    prodEq = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m n

    absProd : abs‚Ą§ ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)) ‚Č° (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)
    absProd = trans (cong abs‚Ą§ prodEq) (sym prodEq)
  in
  trans
    (cong abs‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-neg-right (+suc m) (+suc n)))
    (trans (abs‚Ą§-neg ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n))) absProd)
abs‚Ą§-mul (-suc m) (+suc n) =
  let
    prodEq : (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n) ‚Č° +suc (n +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc n))
    prodEq = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m n

    absProd : abs‚Ą§ ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)) ‚Č° (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)
    absProd = trans (cong abs‚Ą§ prodEq) (sym prodEq)
  in
  trans
    (cong abs‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-neg-left (+suc m) (+suc n)))
    (trans (abs‚Ą§-neg ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n))) absProd)
abs‚Ą§-mul (-suc m) (-suc n) =
  let
    mulEq : (-suc m) *‚Ą§ (-suc n) ‚Č° (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)
    mulEq =
      trans
        (*‚Ą§-neg-right (neg‚Ą§ (+suc m)) (+suc n))
        (trans
          (cong neg‚Ą§ (*‚Ą§-neg-left (+suc m) (+suc n)))
          (neg‚Ą§-involutive ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n))))

    prodEq : (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n) ‚Č° +suc (n +‚Ąē (m *‚Ąē suc n))
    prodEq = *‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq m n

    absProd : abs‚Ą§ ((+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)) ‚Č° (+suc m) *‚Ą§ (+suc n)
    absProd = trans (cong abs‚Ą§ prodEq) (sym prodEq)
  in
  trans (cong abs‚Ą§ mulEq) absProd

-- ¬ß If -b ‚Č§ a ‚Č§ b then |a| ‚Č§ b.
abs‚Ą§-within-bound : (a b : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (neg‚Ą§ b) ‚Č§‚Ą§ a ‚Üí a ‚Č§‚Ą§ b ‚Üí abs‚Ą§ a ‚Č§‚Ą§ b
abs‚Ą§-within-bound 0‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ _ _ = tt
abs‚Ą§-within-bound 0‚Ą§ (+suc n) _ _ = tt
abs‚Ą§-within-bound 0‚Ą§ (-suc n) _ neg-bound = neg-bound
abs‚Ą§-within-bound (+suc a) b _ upper = upper
abs‚Ą§-within-bound (-suc a) b lower _ =
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (neg‚Ą§-involutive b) (neg‚Ą§-antitone-‚Č§‚Ą§ lower)
\end{code}

\chapter{Additive Monotonicity and Positive Natural Laws}
\label{ch:additive-monotonicity-positive-naturals}

The next stage returns to monotonicity, now combining additive order
arguments with the explicit type of positive naturals. The goal is to
move cleanly between natural inequalities, integer inequalities, and the
normalized presentations used earlier.

\section{Nonneg Monotonicity Bridge}
\label{sec:additive-monotonicity:nonneg-bridge}

We begin by relating nonnegative integers to their natural-number
representatives. This bridge allows monotonicity statements on
\texttt{‚Ąē} to be transferred directly to addition on the nonnegative
cone of \texttt{‚Ą§}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Nonneg left monotonicity via from‚Ąē‚Ą§.
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ąē-monoň° : {a b : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí a ‚Č§ b ‚Üí (c : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (c +‚Ąē a) ‚Č§‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (c +‚Ąē b)
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ąē-monoň° p c = from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mono (‚Č§-+‚Ąē-monoň° p c)

-- ¬ß Nonneg right monotonicity via from‚Ąē‚Ą§.
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ąē-mono ≥ : {a b : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí a ‚Č§ b ‚Üí (c : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (a +‚Ąē c) ‚Č§‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (b +‚Ąē c)
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ąē-mono ≥ {a} {b} p c =
  let
    lhs : from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (a +‚Ąē c) ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (c +‚Ąē a)
    lhs = cong from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (+‚Ąē-comm a c)

    rhs : from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (b +‚Ąē c) ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (c +‚Ąē b)
    rhs = cong from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (+‚Ąē-comm b c)

    base : from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (c +‚Ąē a) ‚Č§‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (c +‚Ąē b)
    base = ‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ąē-monoň° p c
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym lhs) (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym rhs) base)

-- ¬ß Nonneg +‚Ą§ right monotonicity.
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥-nonneg : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí m ‚Č§ n ‚Üí (k : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ k) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ k)
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥-nonneg {m} {n} p k =
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym (from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ m k))
    (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym (from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ n k))
      (‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ąē-mono ≥ p k))

-- ¬ß Reflect ‚Č§‚Ą§ back to ‚Ąē-‚Č§ for nonneg integers.
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-reflect : {m n : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m ‚Č§‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n ‚Üí m ‚Č§ n
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-reflect {zero} {zero} _ = z‚Č§n
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-reflect {zero} {suc n} _ = z‚Č§n
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-reflect {suc m} {zero} ()
‚Č§‚Ą§-from‚Ąē‚Ą§-reflect {suc m} {suc n} p = p

-- ¬ß Nonnegativity forces from‚Ąē‚Ą§ form.
0‚Č§‚Ą§‚Üífrom‚Ąē‚Ą§ : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ z ‚Üí ő£ ‚Ąē (őĽ n ‚Üí z ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n)
0‚Č§‚Ą§‚Üífrom‚Ąē‚Ą§ 0‚Ą§ _ = zero , refl
0‚Č§‚Ą§‚Üífrom‚Ąē‚Ą§ (+suc n) _ = suc n , refl
0‚Č§‚Ą§‚Üífrom‚Ąē‚Ą§ (-suc n) ()

-- ¬ß Both-slot nonneg monotonicity.
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono-nonneg‚āā : {m m' n n' : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí m ‚Č§ m' ‚Üí n ‚Č§ n' ‚Üí
  (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m' +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n')
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono-nonneg‚āā {m} {m'} {n} {n'} m‚Č§m' n‚Č§n' =
  let
    step‚āĀ : (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m' +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n)
    step‚āĀ = ‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥-nonneg m‚Č§m' n

    step‚āā : (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m' +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m' +‚Ą§ from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n')
    step‚āā =
      ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (+‚Ą§-comm (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n) (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m'))
        (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (+‚Ą§-comm (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n') (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ m'))
          (‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥-nonneg n‚Č§n' m'))
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-trans step‚āĀ step‚āā
\end{code}

\section{Normalize--Cross Bridge}
\label{sec:additive-monotonicity:normalize-cross-bridge}

The next comparison rewrites order between normalized pairs into a
cross-sum inequality on naturals. This translation is what makes the
later order arguments compatible with the pair-level algebra developed
earlier.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Forward: normalize order implies cross-sum inequality.
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross : (a b c d : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ a b ‚Č§‚Ą§ normalize‚Ą§ c d ‚Üí (a +‚Ąē d) ‚Č§ (c +‚Ąē b)
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross (suc a) (suc b) c d p =
  let ih : (a +‚Ąē d) ‚Č§ (c +‚Ąē b)
      ih = normalize‚Č§‚Üícross a b c d p

      lifted : (suc (a +‚Ąē d)) ‚Č§ (suc (c +‚Ąē b))
      lifted = s‚Č§s ih

      rhsEq : (c +‚Ąē suc b) ‚Č° suc (c +‚Ąē b)
      rhsEq = +‚Ąē-suc-right c b
  in
  subst (őĽ t ‚Üí (suc a +‚Ąē d) ‚Č§ t) (sym rhsEq) lifted
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross a b (suc c) (suc d) p =
  let ih : (a +‚Ąē d) ‚Č§ (c +‚Ąē b)
      ih = normalize‚Č§‚Üícross a b c d p

      lifted : (suc (a +‚Ąē d)) ‚Č§ (suc (c +‚Ąē b))
      lifted = s‚Č§s ih

      lhsEq : (a +‚Ąē suc d) ‚Č° suc (a +‚Ąē d)
      lhsEq = +‚Ąē-suc-right a d
  in
  subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ (suc c +‚Ąē b)) (sym lhsEq) lifted

normalize‚Č§‚Üícross zero zero zero zero _ = z‚Č§n
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross zero zero (suc c) zero _ = z‚Č§n
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross zero zero zero (suc d) ()
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross (suc a) zero zero zero ()
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross (suc a) zero (suc c) zero p =
  let
    lhsEq : (suc a +‚Ąē zero) ‚Č° suc a
    lhsEq = cong suc (+‚Ąē-zero-right a)

    rhsEq : (suc c +‚Ąē zero) ‚Č° suc c
    rhsEq = cong suc (+‚Ąē-zero-right c)
  in
  subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ (suc c +‚Ąē zero)) (sym lhsEq)
    (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí (suc a) ‚Č§ t) (sym rhsEq) p)
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross (suc a) zero zero (suc d) ()
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross zero (suc b) zero zero _ = z‚Č§n
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross zero (suc b) (suc c) zero _ = z‚Č§n
normalize‚Č§‚Üícross zero (suc b) zero (suc d) p = p

-- ¬ß Backward: cross-sum inequality implies normalize order.
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ : (a b c d : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (a +‚Ąē d) ‚Č§ (c +‚Ąē b) ‚Üí normalize‚Ą§ a b ‚Č§‚Ą§ normalize‚Ą§ c d
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ (suc a) (suc b) c d p with subst (őĽ t ‚Üí (suc a +‚Ąē d) ‚Č§ t) (+‚Ąē-suc-right c b) p
... | s‚Č§s q = cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ a b c d q
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ a b (suc c) (suc d) p with subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ (suc c +‚Ąē b)) (+‚Ąē-suc-right a d) p
... | s‚Č§s q = cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ a b c d q

cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ zero zero zero zero _ = tt
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ zero zero (suc c) zero _ = tt
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ zero zero zero (suc d) ()
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ (suc a) zero zero zero ()
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ (suc a) zero (suc c) zero p =
  let
    lhsEq : (suc a +‚Ąē zero) ‚Č° suc a
    lhsEq = cong suc (+‚Ąē-zero-right a)

    rhsEq : (suc c +‚Ąē zero) ‚Č° suc c
    rhsEq = cong suc (+‚Ąē-zero-right c)

    p' : (suc a) ‚Č§ (suc c)
    p' =
      subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ (suc c)) lhsEq
        (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí (suc a +‚Ąē zero) ‚Č§ t) rhsEq p)
  in
  p'
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ (suc a) zero zero (suc d) ()
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ zero (suc b) zero zero _ = tt
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ zero (suc b) (suc c) zero _ = tt
cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ zero (suc b) zero (suc d) p = p
\end{code}

\section{General Additive Monotonicity}
\label{sec:additive-monotonicity:general-additive-monotonicity}

We can now return to addition on integers in full generality. The
pair-based description of order makes it possible to prove monotonicity
and additive cancellation without leaving the concrete normal forms.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Right monotonicity of +‚Ą§ (general).
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥ : {x y : ‚Ą§} ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y ‚Üí (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (x +‚Ą§ z) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y +‚Ą§ z)
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥ {x} {y} x‚Č§y z =
  let
    px = toPair‚Ą§ x
    py = toPair‚Ą§ y
    pz = toPair‚Ą§ z

    ax = pos px
    bx = neg px
    ay = pos py
    by = neg py
    az = pos pz
    bz = neg pz

    x‚Č§y' : normalize‚Ą§ ax bx ‚Č§‚Ą§ normalize‚Ą§ ay by
    x‚Č§y' =
      ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (sym (from-toPair‚Ą§ y))
        (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (sym (from-toPair‚Ą§ x)) x‚Č§y)

    crossXY : (ax +‚Ąē by) ‚Č§ (ay +‚Ąē bx)
    crossXY = normalize‚Č§‚Üícross ax bx ay by x‚Č§y'

    k : ‚Ąē
    k = az +‚Ąē bz

    base : (k +‚Ąē (ax +‚Ąē by)) ‚Č§ (k +‚Ąē (ay +‚Ąē bx))
    base = ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-monoň° crossXY k

    lhsEq : ((ax +‚Ąē az) +‚Ąē (by +‚Ąē bz)) ‚Č° (k +‚Ąē (ax +‚Ąē by))
    lhsEq =
      trans
        (shuffle‚Ąē ax az by bz)
        (+‚Ąē-comm (ax +‚Ąē by) k)

    rhsEq : ((ay +‚Ąē az) +‚Ąē (bx +‚Ąē bz)) ‚Č° (k +‚Ąē (ay +‚Ąē bx))
    rhsEq =
      trans
        (shuffle‚Ąē ay az bx bz)
        (+‚Ąē-comm (ay +‚Ąē bx) k)

    sumCross : ((ax +‚Ąē az) +‚Ąē (by +‚Ąē bz)) ‚Č§ ((ay +‚Ąē az) +‚Ąē (bx +‚Ąē bz))
    sumCross =
      subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ ((ay +‚Ąē az) +‚Ąē (bx +‚Ąē bz))) (sym lhsEq)
        (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí (k +‚Ąē (ax +‚Ąē by)) ‚Č§ t) (sym rhsEq) base)
  in
  cross‚Üínormalize‚Č§ (ax +‚Ąē az) (bx +‚Ąē bz) (ay +‚Ąē az) (by +‚Ąē bz) sumCross

-- ¬ß Left monotonicity of +‚Ą§.
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-monoň° : {x y : ‚Ą§} ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y ‚Üí (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (z +‚Ą§ x) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (z +‚Ą§ y)
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-monoň° {x} {y} x‚Č§y z =
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č°ň° (+‚Ą§-comm x z)
    (‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (+‚Ą§-comm y z)
      (‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥ x‚Č§y z))

-- ¬ß Both-slot monotonicity of +‚Ą§.
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono : {x y u v : ‚Ą§} ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ y ‚Üí u ‚Č§‚Ą§ v ‚Üí (x +‚Ą§ u) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (y +‚Ą§ v)
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono {x} {y} {u} {v} x‚Č§y u‚Č§v =
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-trans (‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥ x‚Č§y u) (‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-monoň° u‚Č§v y)

-- ¬ß Right additive cancellation for ‚Č§‚Ą§.
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-cancel ≥ : (x y z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí x ‚Č§‚Ą§ (z +‚Ą§ y) ‚Üí (x +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y) ‚Č§‚Ą§ z
‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-cancel ≥ x y z p =
  let
    step : (x +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y) ‚Č§‚Ą§ ((z +‚Ą§ y) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y)
    step = ‚Č§‚Ą§-+‚Ą§-mono ≥ p (neg‚Ą§ y)

    rhsEq : ((z +‚Ą§ y) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y) ‚Č° z
    rhsEq =
      trans
        (+‚Ą§-assoc z y (neg‚Ą§ y))
        (trans
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí z +‚Ą§ t) (+‚Ą§-inv-right y))
          (+‚Ą§-zero-right z))
  in
  ‚Č§‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ rhsEq step
\end{code}

\paragraph{Step 2: ‚Ąē‚Āļ Multiplication Homomorphism.}
The next lemmas connect positive naturals with integer multiplication. In particular, they show that the embedding from \texttt{‚Ąē} to \texttt{‚Ą§} behaves multiplicatively on positive denominators and that doubling a positive integer places it strictly above $1$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß from‚Ąē‚Ą§ is multiplicative for ‚Ąē‚Āļ.
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mul-‚Āļ : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (d : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí (from‚Ąē‚Ą§ n *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č° from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (n *‚Ąē ‚Āļto‚Ąē d)
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mul-‚Āļ zero d =
  trans
    (*‚Ą§-zero-left (‚Āļto‚Ą§ d))
    (cong from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (sym (*‚Ąē-zero-left (‚Āļto‚Ąē d))))
from‚Ąē‚Ą§-mul-‚Āļ (suc n) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ k) =
  let
    natForm : (suc n *‚Ąē suc k) ‚Č° suc (k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k))
    natForm = refl

    rhs : from‚Ąē‚Ą§ (suc n *‚Ąē suc k) ‚Č° +suc (k +‚Ąē (n *‚Ąē suc k))
    rhs = cong from‚Ąē‚Ą§ natForm
  in
  trans
    (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq n k)
    (sym rhs)

-- ¬ß 1 < 2z for z positive.
one‚Ą§<twoTimes-pos : (z : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí 0‚Ą§ <‚Ą§ z ‚Üí one‚Ą§ <‚Ą§ (z +‚Ą§ z)
one‚Ą§<twoTimes-pos z zpos with 0<‚Ą§‚Üípos z zpos
... | (m , z‚Č°) =
  <‚Ą§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ą§ t) (sym z‚Č°)) (lePart , notRev)
  where
    twoTimes : (+suc m) +‚Ą§ (+suc m) ‚Č° +suc (m +‚Ąē suc m)
    twoTimes =
      trans
        (from‚Ąē‚Ą§-+‚Ą§ (suc m) (suc m))
        (cong from‚Ąē‚Ą§ refl)

    lePart : one‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ ((+suc m) +‚Ą§ (+suc m))
    lePart =
      let
        lePos : one‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ (+suc (m +‚Ąē suc m))
        lePos = s‚Č§s z‚Č§n
      in
        subst (őĽ t ‚Üí one‚Ą§ ‚Č§‚Ą§ t) (sym twoTimes) lePos

    no-suc‚Č§zero : {t : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí suc t ‚Č§ zero ‚Üí ‚ä•
    no-suc‚Č§zero ()

    impossible : (+suc (m +‚Ąē suc m)) ‚Č§‚Ą§ one‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚ä•
    impossible (s‚Č§s pNat) =
      let
        pNat' : suc (m +‚Ąē m) ‚Č§ zero
        pNat' = subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ zero) (+‚Ąē-suc-right m m) pNat
      in
      no-suc‚Č§zero pNat'

    notRev : ((+suc m) +‚Ą§ (+suc m)) ‚Čį‚Ą§ one‚Ą§
    notRev q = impossible (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§‚Ą§ one‚Ą§) twoTimes q)
\end{code}

\section{Laws of \texorpdfstring{$\mathbb{N}^+$}{N+}}
\label{sec:positive-naturals:laws}

The positive naturals are then given their expected algebraic laws. Since they are represented by predecessors, these statements reduce directly to the multiplication laws already proved on \texttt{‚Ąē} and \texttt{‚Ą§}.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Commutativity of ‚Ąē‚Āļ multiplication.
*‚Āļ-comm : (x y : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí x *‚Āļ y ‚Č° y *‚Āļ x
*‚Āļ-comm (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ a) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ b) =
  cong mk‚Ąē‚Āļ (trans lhsNorm (sym rhsNorm))
  where
    lhsNorm : (a *‚Ąē suc b) +‚Ąē b ‚Č° (a +‚Ąē b) +‚Ąē (a *‚Ąē b)
    lhsNorm =
      trans
        (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ąē b) (*‚Ąē-suc-right-+‚Ąē a b))
        (trans
          (+‚Ąē-assoc a (a *‚Ąē b) b)
          (trans
            (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí a +‚Ąē t) (+‚Ąē-comm (a *‚Ąē b) b))
            (sym (+‚Ąē-assoc a b (a *‚Ąē b)))))

    rhsNorm : (b *‚Ąē suc a) +‚Ąē a ‚Č° (a +‚Ąē b) +‚Ąē (a *‚Ąē b)
    rhsNorm =
      trans
        (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ąē a) (*‚Ąē-suc-right-+‚Ąē b a))
        (trans
          (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí (b +‚Ąē t) +‚Ąē a) (*‚Ąē-comm b a))
          (trans
            (+‚Ąē-assoc b (a *‚Ąē b) a)
            (trans
              (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí b +‚Ąē t) (+‚Ąē-comm (a *‚Ąē b) a))
              (trans
                (swapHead‚Ąē b a (a *‚Ąē b))
                (sym (+‚Ąē-assoc a b (a *‚Ąē b)))))))

-- ¬ß ‚Āļto‚Ą§ is a multiplicative homomorphism.
‚Āļto‚Ą§-*‚Āļ : (x y : ‚Ąē‚Āļ) ‚Üí ‚Āļto‚Ą§ (x *‚Āļ y) ‚Č° (‚Āļto‚Ą§ x) *‚Ą§ (‚Āļto‚Ą§ y)
‚Āļto‚Ą§-*‚Āļ (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ a) (mk‚Ąē‚Āļ b) =
  sym
    (trans
      (*‚Ą§-pos-pos-eq a b)
      (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí +suc t) (+‚Ąē-comm b (a *‚Ąē suc b))))
\end{code}

With these positive-denominator tools available, the development is one
step away from the simplex evaluation. Before that step is taken, the
form of the evaluating expression must be accounted for. The next chapter
shows that the four invariants of the closed record are not arithmetic
quantities of the same kind, and that this difference of kind --- not a
choice of formula --- determines whether two of them combine by product
or by sum.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{The Kind Classification of Invariants}
\label{ch:invariant-kinds}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

The closed $K_4$ record carries four invariants: the vertex count $V$,
the edge count $E$, the dimensional invariant $d$, and the Euler
characteristic $\chi$. Treated as a tuple in $\mathbb{N}^4$, they
admit innumerable arithmetic combinations. Treated according to what
each one \emph{counts}, they fall into four pairwise distinct kinds,
and the form of any combination of them is no longer free.

This chapter exhibits the kind classification, proves the pairwise
disjointness of the four kinds, and reads off the consequence: an
expression that combines two invariants of the \emph{same} kind is a
product (the categorical operation that respects the shared kind);
an expression that combines two invariants of \emph{disjoint} kinds is
a sum (the categorical operation that admits both kinds without
identifying them). The rule is the standard Curry--Howard reading of
the data type below: same constructor accumulates multiplicatively,
different constructors accumulate additively under the unique
forgetful map into $\mathbb{N}$.

\section{Four Kinds, Not Four Numbers}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:four-kinds}

The four invariants of the closed record are not interchangeable
quantities. Each is a specific kind of count, attached to a specific
piece of structure of $K_4$.

The vertex count $V$ counts the elements of a finite set: the four
distinguishable configurations carried by the survivor. It is a
\emph{configurational} quantity. Its powers $V^d$ count tuples of
configurations across $d$ independent extension directions; they are
configurational as well.

The edge count $E$ counts unordered pairs of configurations that stand
in the relation \emph{is distinguished from}. Its constructor is the
combinatorial number $\binom{V}{2}$. It is a \emph{relational} quantity.

The dimensional invariant $d$ counts the independent monotone directions
of extension exhibited in
\hyperref[ch:forced-spatial-extension]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:forced-spatial-extension}}.
On a complete graph it equals $V - 1$, the dimension of the non-trivial
eigenspace of the Laplacian, and the embedding dimension of the
simplex carrier. Its constructor is dimension. It is a \emph{directional}
quantity. Its square $d^2$ is also directional: it counts ordered pairs
of independent directions, the natural cross-direction term of an
extension structure.

The Euler characteristic $\chi = V - E + F$ is the alternating sum of
the cell counts of the simplicial closure. It is a global invariant of
the closed structure: the witness that the survivor closes on itself
without leaving cells unaccounted for. Its constructor is the
alternating sum of a complex. It is a \emph{topological} quantity.

The topological kind is irreducible: $\chi$ depends on the face count
$F$, and $F$ is not a function of $V$, $E$, $d$ alone. A simplicial
complex on the same vertex and edge counts may close with different
face counts; the closure datum is independent of the lower-dimensional
incidence data. The kind classification therefore cannot collapse $\chi$
into the configurational, relational, or directional kind: doing so
would silently identify $F$ with a value derivable from $V$, $E$, $d$,
which is false in general and only happens to coincide on $K_4$.

The arithmetic identities that hold among these invariants on $K_4$ ---
$E = \binom{V}{2}$, $d = V - 1$, $\chi = V + F - E$ --- are equations
between values, not identifications between constructors. The
constructors stay disjoint at the type level. This disjointness is the
content of the next section.

\textbf{Constraint:} the four invariants of the closed record must be
classified by what they count, not by what they evaluate to.

\textbf{Construction:} a four-element data type whose constructors
correspond to the four kinds, and an assignment of each invariant to
its constructor.

\textbf{Consequence:} the four invariants can no longer be treated as a
homogeneous tuple. Any later expression in them carries a kind label
on every factor.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The four kinds of invariant attached to the closed K‚āĄ record.
data InvariantKind : Set where
  configurational : InvariantKind  -- counts elements of a finite set
  relational      : InvariantKind  -- counts unordered pairs
  directional     : InvariantKind  -- counts independent extension directions
  topological     : InvariantKind  -- alternating sum of cell counts

-- ¬ß Kind assignments for the four K‚āĄ invariants.
V-kind : InvariantKind
V-kind = configurational

E-kind : InvariantKind
E-kind = relational

d-kind : InvariantKind
d-kind = directional

Ōá-kind : InvariantKind
Ōá-kind = topological
\end{code}

\section{Pairwise Disjointness}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:disjointness}

The four constructors of \texttt{InvariantKind} are pairwise distinct in
the strict sense: no two of them are propositionally equal. This is the
standard property of constructors in a data type with no equations
imposed; the absurd-pattern proofs witness it explicitly.

The disjointness is what carries the Curry--Howard reading. If two
invariants have the same kind, they are inhabitants of the same
constructor and combine inside that kind. If two invariants have
disjoint kinds, no map identifies their constructors, and any
combination must record both kinds separately. Under the unique
forgetful map from \texttt{InvariantKind}-tagged quantities to plain
naturals, the first reading turns into multiplication of the values
(the categorical product reduces to numerical product), and the second
into addition (the coproduct reduces to numerical sum).

\textbf{Constraint:} the four kinds must be pairwise distinguishable
inside the type theory.

\textbf{Elimination:} every candidate identification between two
distinct constructors is eliminated by the absurd pattern. Six
identifications would be needed; six are eliminated.

\textbf{Consequence:} no kind collapses into another. The
classification is rigid.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The six pairwise disjointness lemmas.
configurational‚ČĘrelational : ¬¨ (configurational ‚Č° relational)
configurational‚ČĘrelational ()

configurational‚ČĘdirectional : ¬¨ (configurational ‚Č° directional)
configurational‚ČĘdirectional ()

configurational‚ČĘtopological : ¬¨ (configurational ‚Č° topological)
configurational‚ČĘtopological ()

relational‚ČĘdirectional : ¬¨ (relational ‚Č° directional)
relational‚ČĘdirectional ()

relational‚ČĘtopological : ¬¨ (relational ‚Č° topological)
relational‚ČĘtopological ()

directional‚ČĘtopological : ¬¨ (directional ‚Č° topological)
directional‚ČĘtopological ()
\end{code}

\section{The Forgetful Map: Kind-Tagged Arithmetic}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:forgetful}

The disjointness lemmas leave the four kinds rigidly distinguishable
inside the type theory. The simplex evaluation, however, is a plain
natural number; somewhere the kind labels must be discharged. The
discharge is not arbitrary. It is the unique forgetful map from
kind-tagged values into $\mathbb{N}$ that respects the categorical
structure of the kind labels.

A kind-tagged value is a natural number carrying its kind as a type
index. Within a shared kind, two such values combine multiplicatively
under the categorical product: the resulting tag is the shared kind,
and the underlying value is the product of the values. Across disjoint
kinds, two such values combine additively under the categorical
coproduct: the operation requires a disjointness witness at the type
level, and the underlying value is the sum of the values. The
forgetful reductions are not asserted; they are computed by
\texttt{refl}.

\textbf{Constraint:} the kind labels must be discharged by a map into
$\mathbb{N}$ that respects the categorical operations on tagged
values --- product within a shared kind, coproduct across disjoint
kinds.

\textbf{Construction:} the indexed record \texttt{Tagged} below carries
a value at a given kind. The combinators $\_\otimes\_$ and
$\_\oplus[\_]\_$ implement the product and the coproduct; the
disjointness witness is an obligation in the type of the coproduct
combinator and cannot be supplied for two values of the same kind.
The forgetful reductions hold definitionally.

\textbf{Consequence:} the verbal claim ``shared kind $\to$ product
$\to$ multiplication; disjoint kinds $\to$ coproduct $\to$ addition''
is now a definitional reduction inside the type theory. The
forgetful map is unique up to definitional equality, and the
arithmetic shape of the simplex evaluation is forced by the type of
its tagged operands.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß A natural number carrying its kind as a type index.
record Tagged (k : InvariantKind) : Set where
  constructor tag
  field value : ‚Ąē
open Tagged public

-- ¬ß Product within a shared kind: values multiply.
infixl 7 _‚äó_
_‚äó_ : ‚ąÄ {k} ‚Üí Tagged k ‚Üí Tagged k ‚Üí Tagged k
tag m ‚äó tag n = tag (m *‚Ąē n)

-- ¬ß Coproduct across disjoint kinds: values add. The disjointness
-- witness is required at the type level; two values of the same kind
-- cannot use this combinator.
infixl 6 _‚äē[_]_
_‚äē[_]_ : ‚ąÄ {j k} ‚Üí Tagged j ‚Üí ¬¨ (j ‚Č° k) ‚Üí Tagged k ‚Üí ‚Ąē
tag m ‚äē[ _ ] tag n = m +‚Ąē n

-- ¬ß Forgetful reductions: the verbal claim becomes a definitional
-- equality. Both proofs are refl.
forget-‚äó : ‚ąÄ {k} (x y : Tagged k)
         ‚Üí value (x ‚äó y) ‚Č° value x *‚Ąē value y
forget-‚äó (tag _) (tag _) = refl

forget-‚äē : ‚ąÄ {j k} (x : Tagged j) (p : ¬¨ (j ‚Č° k)) (y : Tagged k)
         ‚Üí (x ‚äē[ p ] y) ‚Č° value x +‚Ąē value y
forget-‚äē (tag _) _ (tag _) = refl
\end{code}

\section{Bulk and Boundary}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:bulk-boundary}

The expression that the next chapter will evaluate has two terms. The
first, $V^d \cdot \chi$, multiplies a configurational quantity by a
topological one. The second, $d^2$, is purely directional. The two
terms combine additively.

The structural reading of this shape is now forced. Inside the first
term, configurational and topological invariants meet. They occupy
distinct kinds, so they cannot collapse; but they describe the same
underlying object --- the closed extension structure --- evaluated in
two compatible aspects (its configurations and its closure condition).
Their categorical combination is a product of factors that share the
same carrier, and the forgetful functor sends this to numerical
multiplication. The first term is the \emph{bulk} of the evaluation:
configurations weighted by closure.

The second term draws on a kind disjoint from both factors of the bulk.
The directional invariant $d$ counts neither configurations nor closure
cells; it counts the independent extension axes carrying the structure.
A boundary correction along these axes, expressed as the count of
ordered pairs of axes, has nowhere to merge with the bulk multiplicatively
without identifying its kind with one already present in the bulk. The
absurd patterns of the previous section forbid that identification. The
remaining categorical operation between disjoint kinds is the coproduct,
which the forgetful functor sends to numerical addition.

\textbf{Constraint:} the bulk term and the boundary term draw on kinds
related by the disjointness lemmas of the previous section.

\textbf{Construction:} the record below witnesses the kind assignments
of both terms and exhibits the disjointness needed for the additive
combination.

\textbf{Consequence:} the form $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$ is no longer a
choice. It is the only combination of these four invariants whose
inner factors share their kind and whose outer factors do not.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Bulk and boundary draw on disjoint kinds ‚Üí forced additive combination.
record BulkBoundaryIndependence : Set where
  field
    bulk-factor‚āĀ  : InvariantKind   -- V (and hence VŠĶą)
    bulk-factor‚āā  : InvariantKind   -- Ōá
    boundary-kind : InvariantKind   -- d (and hence d¬≤)
    bulk‚āĀ-is-configurational : bulk-factor‚āĀ  ‚Č° configurational
    bulk‚āā-is-topological     : bulk-factor‚āā  ‚Č° topological
    boundary-is-directional  : boundary-kind ‚Č° directional
    bulk‚āĀ-disjoint-boundary  : ¬¨ (bulk-factor‚āĀ  ‚Č° boundary-kind)
    bulk‚āā-disjoint-boundary  : ¬¨ (bulk-factor‚āā  ‚Č° boundary-kind)

theorem-bulk-boundary-independent : BulkBoundaryIndependence
theorem-bulk-boundary-independent = record
  { bulk-factor‚āĀ             = configurational
  ; bulk-factor‚āā             = topological
  ; boundary-kind            = directional
  ; bulk‚āĀ-is-configurational = refl
  ; bulk‚āā-is-topological     = refl
  ; boundary-is-directional  = refl
  ; bulk‚āĀ-disjoint-boundary  = configurational‚ČĘdirectional
  ; bulk‚āā-disjoint-boundary  = őĽ p ‚Üí directional‚ČĘtopological (sym p)
  }
\end{code}

\section{Source-Indexing: Why Exactly Four Kinds}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:source-indexing}

The four kinds of the previous sections are not a choice of taxonomy.
They are the four primitive cardinality sources of a closed simplicial
$d$-extension on $K_4$, and no fifth source exists. The classification
is therefore not imposed; it is read off the record.

A closed simplicial $d$-extension on $K_4$ carries cells in dimensions
$0$, $1$, and $2$, terminating at the top-dimensional face count
because $K_4$ is the closure of the $3$-simplex and admits no
independent higher-dimensional stratum. The numerical readings
available on the closed structure are therefore exactly four: the
count of $0$-cells, the count of $1$-cells, the count of $2$-cells,
and the ambient direction count $d$ established by
\hyperref[ch:forced-spatial-extension]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:forced-spatial-extension}}.
Anything else read off the record is a function of these four sources,
not a fifth source, because the record carries no datum beyond cells
and directions.

Of these four sources, the $2$-cell count $F$ is not refinement-stable:
a barycentric subdivision changes $F$ without changing the topological
content of the closure. The closure-stable substitute is the
alternating sum $\chi = V - E + F$, which absorbs $F$ into a
refinement-invariant combination. The four primitive cardinality
sources of the closed record therefore collapse, under
refinement-stability, to: $V$ (configurational, the $0$-cell
reading), $E$ (relational, the $1$-cell reading), $d$ (directional,
the ambient direction reading), and $\chi$ (topological, the
refinement-stable closure reading).

The kind classification of the previous sections is the source-indexing
of the record itself: each kind names the structural slot from which
its invariant is read. A fifth kind would require a fifth slot. The
strata $\{0,1,2\}$ exhaust the cell dimensions present in the closed
$3$-simplex; the direction count exhausts the non-cell data the
record carries. No fifth slot exists.

\textbf{Constraint:} every $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-invariant numerical
extract of the closed record must factor through one of the record's
primitive cardinality sources.

\textbf{Elimination:} a fifth kind is eliminated by the structural
exhaustion of the record's slots: no cell stratum beyond $\{0,1,2\}$
exists in the closed $3$-simplex, and no non-cell datum beyond
direction is carried by the record.

\textbf{Consequence:} the four-element data type \texttt{InvariantKind}
is not a choice of categorisation. It is the unique enumeration of
the record's primitive sources, and the four invariants are the
unique refinement-stable readings of those sources. The disjointness
lemmas of \S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:disjointness} are then not the
rigidity of an external classification, but the rigidity of the
record's own slot structure.

\section{The Extract Concept: From Binary Distinction to Cardinality}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:extract}

The previous section spoke of an ``$\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-invariant
numerical extract of the closed record'' as if the notion were
self-evident. It is not. If the entire programme of this book is
that everything follows from a single binary distinction, the notion
of \emph{extract} cannot be exempt: it must itself be forced by the
distinction, not imported from outside.

A binary distinction has no internal labels. The two sides are
distinguishable from each other, but neither side carries an identity
beyond ``not the other''. Whatever is read off such a distinction
must therefore not depend on which side is which: any reading that
does depend on a labelling of sides is not a reading of the
distinction itself, but a reading of an extra structure quietly
attached to it. This is the only constraint a binary distinction
imposes on its readings, and it is enough to fix what an extract is.

Iterated, the binary distinction produces the closed $K_4$ record
through the elimination chain of the earlier chapters. The internal
symmetries of that record --- its relabellings of the four
configurations that preserve the distinction structure --- are
exactly $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$. A reading that depends on a particular
labelling of the four configurations smuggles in information the
distinction did not provide. A reading that is constant on every
$\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-orbit of the record's data uses only what the
distinction itself supplies.

\emph{An extract is a reading of the closed record that is constant
on every $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-orbit.} This is not a definition imposed
on top of the record. It is the only kind of reading the binary
distinction admits.

The orbit structure of $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$ on the record is now read
off the record itself. The action is transitive on each cell stratum:
on the four vertices, on the six edges, on the four $2$-faces, and on
the unique $3$-cell. A function constant on each transitive orbit is
determined by the cardinality of that orbit alone. The available
cardinalities are therefore exactly four: the vertex count $V = 4$,
the edge count $E = 6$, the face count $F = 4$, and the top-cell
count $1$. The top-cell count is constant across all closed
$3$-simplices and carries no further information; it is absorbed into
the closure invariant. The remaining three cardinalities, plus the
ambient direction count $d$ established in
\hyperref[ch:forced-spatial-extension]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:forced-spatial-extension}},
are the four primitive cardinality sources of the closed record.

Refinement-stability collapses $(V, E, F)$ to $(V, E, \chi)$ via the
alternating sum, as already established. The four refinement-stable
cardinality sources of the closed record are therefore $V$, $E$, $d$,
$\chi$. \emph{Every} extract --- every reading of the closed record
that respects the binary distinction's lack of internal labels ---
factors through these four. There is no reading outside of them.

\textbf{Constraint:} the binary distinction provides no internal
labels. A reading that depends on a labelling reads structure the
distinction did not supply.

\textbf{Construction:} an extract is a function on the cardinalities
of the orbits of $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$ on the closed record's strata.
The type below records the four cardinality sources; an extract is a
function from this type into $\mathbb{N}$.

\textbf{Consequence:} the verbal claim ``every $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-invariant
numerical extract factors through the four slots'' is now a
type-level fact: an extract is, by the only definition the binary
distinction admits, a function of $V$, $E$, $d$, $\chi$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The four refinement-stable cardinality sources of the closed
-- K‚āĄ record. Each field is the cardinality of a single Aut(K‚āĄ)-orbit,
-- with the face count F absorbed into Ōá by refinement-stability.
record CardinalitySources : Set where
  constructor sources
  field
    src-V : ‚Ąē  -- |vertex orbit|        : configurational
    src-E : ‚Ąē  -- |edge orbit|          : relational
    src-d : ‚Ąē  -- |independent axes|    : directional
    src-Ōá : ‚Ąē  -- alternating closure   : topological
open CardinalitySources public

-- ¬ß An extract is a function from the cardinality sources into ‚Ąē.
-- This is the only shape of reading admitted by a binary distinction
-- with no internal labels: it depends solely on orbit cardinalities,
-- not on choices of labelling within an orbit.
Extract : Set
Extract = CardinalitySources ‚Üí ‚Ąē

-- ¬ß Witness that the four kind-tagged invariants are themselves
-- extracts: each is a projection from the cardinality sources, hence
-- trivially a function on them.
extract-V : Extract
extract-V = src-V

extract-E : Extract
extract-E = src-E

extract-d : Extract
extract-d = src-d

extract-Ōá : Extract
extract-Ōá = src-Ōá
\end{code}

\section{What This Forces about the Formula's Form}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:form-forced}

The classification above and the disjointness lemmas of the previous
section together fix the form of the simplex evaluation. Three points
are now removed from the list of choices, and a fourth --- the
exponents within each term --- is fixed by minimality.

The exponentiation in $V^d$ is fixed: configurational multiplied $d$
times by itself counts the independent assignments of configurations
across the $d$ extension directions. Each factor is configurational;
they accumulate multiplicatively. No additive form $V \cdot d$ would
respect the configurational kind, because $d$ is directional, not a
multiplier of configurations. Lower powers are also eliminated: a
bulk built on $V$ alone, or on $V^k$ with $k < d$, would count
configurations across only $k$ of the $d$ extension directions,
silently distinguishing those $k$ directions from the remaining ones.
That distinction is forbidden by the isotropy result of
\hyperref[sec:spatial-extension:isotropy]{\S\ref*{sec:spatial-extension:isotropy}}.
The exponent must therefore be exactly $d$.

The multiplication by $\chi$ is fixed: $\chi$ is topological, distinct
from $V$ but shared with the closed extension structure that $V^d$
counts. Configurational and topological factors are kinds of the same
underlying object and accumulate as a categorical product, which the
forgetful functor sends to numerical multiplication. An additive
$V^d + \chi$ would treat $\chi$ as a kind disjoint from $V^d$, which
the bulk reading forbids: $\chi$ is the closure condition under which
the configurations are counted, not a separate accountant.

The addition of $d^2$ is fixed: $d^2$ is directional, disjoint from
both kinds of the bulk. The categorical combination between disjoint
kinds is the coproduct, sent to numerical addition. A multiplicative
$V^d \cdot \chi \cdot d^2$ would identify the directional kind with
one of the bulk kinds, contradicting the disjointness lemmas.

The exponent of the boundary term is fixed by codimension, not by
choice. The bulk $V^d \cdot \chi$ accounts for the closed
$d$-dimensional interior. A boundary correction lives one codimension
below: on the $(d-1)$-dimensional surface of the $d$-extension. The
structural content of a codimension-$1$ surface inside a
$d$-dimensional extension is intrinsically binary: each surface
direction comes paired with the unique transverse direction across
the surface. The boundary correction therefore records ordered pairs
$(\text{along}, \text{transverse})$ of independent directions, and
the count of such pairs is exactly $d \cdot d = d^2$. The exponent
$2$ is the binary along--transverse arity of a codimension-$1$
surface, not a parameter choice. A linear $d$ would record only one
of the two boundary directions and miss the transverse coupling; a
cubic $d^3$ would record cross-direction structure of arity $3$,
which a codimension-$1$ boundary does not carry. Both are eliminated
by the codimension structure, not by enumeration.

\textbf{Constraint:} every binary connector in the simplex evaluation
must be the categorical operation respecting the kinds of its
operands; the bulk exponent must be the isotropic one across all
$d$ extension directions; and the boundary exponent must be the
along--transverse arity of a codimension-$1$ surface.

\textbf{Elimination:} every binary form other than $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$
that combines exactly these four invariants either identifies kinds
the disjointness lemmas keep apart, fails to multiply factors of the
shared bulk kind, breaks isotropy across the $d$ directions of the
bulk, or violates the binary along--transverse arity of the boundary.
The first two failures are eliminated by the lemmas of
\S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:disjointness} and the bulk witness of
\S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:bulk-boundary}; the third by the isotropy
result of \hyperref[sec:spatial-extension:isotropy]{\S\ref*{sec:spatial-extension:isotropy}};
the fourth by the codimension structure of the present section.

\textbf{Consequence:} the simplex evaluation defined in the next
section is the unique combination of $V$, $E$, $d$, $\chi$ whose form
respects the kind classification, the bulk--boundary split, and the
codimension structure. The integer it produces is therefore not the
value of one definition among many; it is the value of the only
structurally admissible combination of the four refinement-stable
invariants of the closed record.

\section{Audit of Eliminated Freedoms}
\label{sec:invariant-kinds:audit}

The simplex evaluation carries no free parameters. This audit names
each degree of freedom that a generic four-invariant expression would
carry, and the structural fact that eliminates it. No item below is
closed by enumeration; each is closed by a single structural witness.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Choice of which invariants enter.} Eliminated by
    \S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:extract} together with
    \S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:source-indexing}: an extract is
    forced by the binary distinction to be a function on the
    cardinality sources of $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$-orbits, and those
    sources are exhausted by $V$, $E$, $d$, $\chi$. No fifth
    cardinality source exists, and no reading outside the four
    factors through the binary distinction at all.

  \item \textbf{Choice of arithmetic operation between two invariants}
    (sum vs.\ product). Eliminated by
    \S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:disjointness} together with the
    forgetful map of \S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:forgetful}: shared
    kind admits the categorical product $\_\otimes\_$, whose forgetful
    reduction is multiplication; disjoint kinds admit the categorical
    coproduct $\_\oplus[\_]\_$, whose forgetful reduction is addition.
    Both reductions hold by \texttt{refl}.

  \item \textbf{Choice of which factors are bulk and which are
    boundary.} Eliminated by the bulk--boundary witness of
    \S\ref{sec:invariant-kinds:bulk-boundary}: configurational and
    topological share the closed extension as their carrier and
    accumulate inside the bulk; directional is disjoint from both
    and forms the boundary.

  \item \textbf{Choice of bulk exponent.} Eliminated by the isotropy
    result of
    \hyperref[sec:spatial-extension:isotropy]{\S\ref*{sec:spatial-extension:isotropy}}:
    the bulk must count configurations across all $d$ extension
    directions symmetrically. Any exponent other than $d$ would
    distinguish a subset of the directions from the rest.

  \item \textbf{Choice of boundary exponent.} Eliminated by the
    codimension structure of the previous section: a codimension-$1$
    surface of a $d$-dimensional extension carries the binary
    along--transverse pair of directions. The exponent $2$ is the
    arity of this pair, not a parameter.

  \item \textbf{Choice of explicit role for the relational kind.}
    Eliminated by the alternating sum $\chi = V - E + F$: the
    relational invariant $E$ enters the evaluation through $\chi$,
    not as an independent factor. An additional explicit $E$-term
    would either duplicate the contribution already absorbed into
    the closure invariant or introduce a non-refinement-stable
    reading of the closure.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Constraint:} the simplex evaluation carries six structural
choices, each tied to a degree of freedom that a generic combination
of four invariants admits.

\textbf{Elimination:} every one of the six choices is forced by a
single structural witness named above. None remains free, and none is
closed by enumeration.

\textbf{Consequence:} the form $V^d \cdot \chi + d^2$ is the unique
combination of the four refinement-stable invariants of the closed
$K_4$ record that respects every structural fact carried by the
record. The integer it produces on $K_4$ is therefore not the value
of one candidate among many. It is the value of the only structurally
admissible candidate.

Each of the six structural witnesses listed above is mechanically
discharged in
\hyperref[ch:mechanical-closure]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:mechanical-closure}}
by a record that consumes existing book lemmas: the orbit theorems
of \hyperref[ch:forced-edge-matching-orbits]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:forced-edge-matching-orbits}}
for the Aut-related items, the simplex constants of
\hyperref[ch:simplex-invariants-natural-multiplication]{Chapter~\ref*{ch:simplex-invariants-natural-multiplication}}
for the closure and boundary items, and the simplex evaluation of
the next section for the value~$137$.

The arithmetic that carries out the evaluation is now ready. The
following chapter introduces the rational numbers used by the wider
measurement framework, and the simplex evaluation itself.

\chapter{Rational Numbers and Measurement}
\label{ch:rational-numbers-measurement}

Rational numbers are represented here as integer numerators over positive denominators. Equality, order, and distance are then defined by the familiar cross-multiplication formulas, expressed entirely inside the arithmetic already developed for \texttt{‚Ą§} and \texttt{‚Ąē‚Āļ}.

\section{Rational Arithmetic}
\label{sec:rational-measurement:rational-arithmetic}

The basic rational operations are defined in the usual way by clearing denominators. Because denominators live in \texttt{‚Ąē‚Āļ}, no additional side conditions are needed to keep the constructions well formed.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Rational number: integer numerator over positive denominator.
record ‚Ąö : Set where
  constructor _/_
  field
    num : ‚Ą§
    den : ‚Ąē‚Āļ

open ‚Ąö public

infix 4 _‚ČÉ‚Ąö_

-- ¬ß Setoid equality: cross-multiplication.
_‚ČÉ‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí Set
(a / b) ‚ČÉ‚Ąö (c / d) = (a *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č° (c *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ b)

infixl 6 _+‚Ąö_ _-‚Ąö_
infixl 7 _*‚Ąö_

-- ¬ß Rational addition.
_+‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
(a / b) +‚Ąö (c / d) = ((a *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) +‚Ą§ (c *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ b)) / (b *‚Āļ d)

-- ¬ß Rational multiplication.
_*‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
(a / b) *‚Ąö (c / d) = (a *‚Ą§ c) / (b *‚Āļ d)

-- ¬ß Rational negation.
-‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
-‚Ąö (a / b) = neg‚Ą§ a / b

-- ¬ß Rational subtraction.
_-‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
p -‚Ąö q = p +‚Ąö (-‚Ąö q)

-- ¬ß Distinguished rationals.
0‚Ąö 1‚Ąö : ‚Ąö
0‚Ąö = 0‚Ą§ / one‚Āļ
1‚Ąö = one‚Ą§ / one‚Āļ

infix 4 _‚Č§‚Ąö_ _<‚Ąö_

-- ¬ß Rational order: cross-multiply and compare integers.
_‚Č§‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí Set
(a / b) ‚Č§‚Ąö (c / d) = (a *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) ‚Č§‚Ą§ (c *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ b)

-- ¬ß Strict rational order.
_<‚Ąö_ : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí Set
(a / b) <‚Ąö (c / d) = (a *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) <‚Ą§ (c *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ b)

-- ¬ß Rational distance.
dist‚Ąö : ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö ‚Üí ‚Ąö
dist‚Ąö (a / b) (c / d) = abs‚Ą§ ((a *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ d) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (c *‚Ą§ ‚Āļto‚Ą§ b)) / (b *‚Āļ d)
\end{code}

\section{Simplex Evaluation: \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{C} = 137$}{\textit{C} = 137}}
\label{sec:rational-measurement:simplex-eval-137}
\phantomsection\label{sec:simplex-eval}

We now return to the distinguished value
\[
  \mathcal{C} = V^d \cdot \chi + d^2 = 137.
\]
The point of this section is not merely to recompute it, but to show that it remains singled out when compared with nearby enumerated families of formulas built from the same invariant stock.

Using the previously established values of $V$, $d$, and $\chi$, the defining expression reduces by direct computation to $137$. The records that follow compare this canonical value with alternative pair-sum, power-formula, and three-term constructions.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Natural exponentiation.
_^_ : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
x ^ zero = suc zero
x ^ suc n = x * (x ^ n)

-- ¬ß Simplex evaluation from K‚āĄ invariants.
simplex-eval : ‚Ąē
simplex-eval = (simplex-vertices ^ simplex-degree) * simplex-chi + (simplex-degree * simplex-degree)

-- ¬ß Law 15A.0: the derived integer is exactly 137.
law15A-0-simplex-eval-137 : simplex-eval ‚Č° 137
law15A-0-simplex-eval-137 = refl

-- ¬ß Redundant witness (same computation, explicit name).
derived-integer : ‚Ąē
derived-integer =
  (simplex-vertices ^ simplex-degree) * simplex-chi
  + (simplex-degree * simplex-degree)

law15A-0-derived-integer-137 : derived-integer ‚Č° 137
law15A-0-derived-integer-137 = refl
\end{code}

\subsection{Candidate elimination within enumerated families}
\label{sec:simplex-eval-exhaustion}

The following records make that comparison explicit. Several natural rival families are enumerated from the same invariant stock, and direct computation shows that none of them displace the canonical formula.

\paragraph{Pair-product sums.}
The first family consists of pairwise sums of basic quadratic products of the $K_4$ invariants.
\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Products of pairs of K‚āĄ invariants
p-VV p-VE p-Vd p-VŌá p-EE p-Ed p-EŌá p-dd p-dŌá p-ŌáŌá : ‚Ąē
p-VV = simplex-vertices * simplex-vertices       -- 16
p-VE = simplex-vertices * simplex-edges           -- 24
p-Vd = simplex-vertices * simplex-degree          -- 12
p-VŌá = simplex-vertices * simplex-chi             -- 8
p-EE = simplex-edges * simplex-edges               -- 36
p-Ed = simplex-edges * simplex-degree              -- 18
p-EŌá = simplex-edges * simplex-chi                 -- 12
p-dd = simplex-degree * simplex-degree             -- 9
p-dŌá = simplex-degree * simplex-chi                -- 6
p-ŌáŌá = simplex-chi * simplex-chi                   -- 4

-- ¬ß No pair-sum of K‚āĄ products gives 137.
record NoPairSumGives137 : Set where
  field
    t-VV+VE : ¬¨ (p-VV + p-VE ‚Č° 137)   -- 40
    t-VV+EE : ¬¨ (p-VV + p-EE ‚Č° 137)   -- 52
    t-VV+Ed : ¬¨ (p-VV + p-Ed ‚Č° 137)   -- 34
    t-VE+EE : ¬¨ (p-VE + p-EE ‚Č° 137)   -- 60
    t-VE+Ed : ¬¨ (p-VE + p-Ed ‚Č° 137)   -- 42
    t-VE+dd : ¬¨ (p-VE + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 33
    t-EE+dd : ¬¨ (p-EE + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 45
    t-EE+Vd : ¬¨ (p-EE + p-Vd ‚Č° 137)   -- 48
    t-EE+VŌá : ¬¨ (p-EE + p-VŌá ‚Č° 137)   -- 44
    t-Ed+Vd : ¬¨ (p-Ed + p-Vd ‚Č° 137)   -- 30
    t-VV+dd : ¬¨ (p-VV + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 25
    t-VV+EŌá : ¬¨ (p-VV + p-EŌá ‚Č° 137)   -- 28

law15A-1-no-pair-sum-137 : NoPairSumGives137
law15A-1-no-pair-sum-137 = record
  { t-VV+VE = őĽ () ; t-VV+EE = őĽ () ; t-VV+Ed = őĽ ()
  ; t-VE+EE = őĽ () ; t-VE+Ed = őĽ () ; t-VE+dd = őĽ ()
  ; t-EE+dd = őĽ () ; t-EE+Vd = őĽ () ; t-EE+VŌá = őĽ ()
  ; t-Ed+Vd = őĽ () ; t-VV+dd = őĽ () ; t-VV+EŌá = őĽ ()
  }
\end{code}

\paragraph{Power formulas.}
The second family tests formulas built from powers $X^Y$, together with simple affine modifications of those powers.
\begin{code}
-- ¬ß All power combinations X^Y with X, Y ‚ąą {V, E, d, Ōá}.
pow-Vd pow-VŌá pow-VE pow-dV pow-dE pow-dŌá pow-ŌáV pow-ŌáE pow-Ed pow-EV pow-EŌá : ‚Ąē
pow-Vd = simplex-vertices ^ simplex-degree        -- 4¬≥ = 64
pow-VŌá = simplex-vertices ^ simplex-chi           -- 4¬≤ = 16
pow-VE = simplex-vertices ^ simplex-edges         -- 4‚Ā∂ = 4096
pow-dV = simplex-degree ^ simplex-vertices        -- 3‚Āī = 81
pow-dE = simplex-degree ^ simplex-edges           -- 3‚Ā∂ = 729
pow-dŌá = simplex-degree ^ simplex-chi             -- 3¬≤ = 9
pow-ŌáV = simplex-chi ^ simplex-vertices           -- 2‚Āī = 16
pow-ŌáE = simplex-chi ^ simplex-edges              -- 2‚Ā∂ = 64
pow-Ed = simplex-edges ^ simplex-degree           -- 6¬≥ = 216
pow-EV = simplex-edges ^ simplex-vertices         -- 6‚Āī = 1296
pow-EŌá = simplex-edges ^ simplex-chi              -- 6¬≤ = 36

-- ¬ß Exhaustive power-formula tests.
record AllPowerFormulaTests : Set where
  field
    -- ¬ß X^Y ¬∑ Ōá + d¬≤ for various X, Y (our formula uses V^d ¬∑ Ōá + d¬≤ = 137)
    alt-dV-Ōá-dd  : ¬¨ (pow-dV * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 81¬∑2+9 = 171
    alt-VŌá-Ōá-dd  : ¬¨ (pow-VŌá * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 16¬∑2+9 = 41
    alt-dŌá-Ōá-dd  : ¬¨ (pow-dŌá * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 9¬∑2+9 = 27
    alt-Ed-Ōá-dd  : ¬¨ (pow-Ed * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 216¬∑2+9 = 441
    alt-EŌá-Ōá-dd  : ¬¨ (pow-EŌá * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137)   -- 36¬∑2+9 = 81

    -- ¬ß The structural identity: Ōá^E = V^d (not an alternative)
    alt-ŌáE-is-Vd     : pow-ŌáE ‚Č° pow-Vd                        -- 2‚Ā∂ = 4¬≥
    alt-ŌáE-same       : pow-ŌáE * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137     -- confirms identity

    -- ¬ß X^Y + Z for various Z (no Ōá multiplier)
    alt-Vd-dd    : ¬¨ (pow-Vd + p-dd ‚Č° 137)             -- 64+9 = 73
    alt-dV-dd    : ¬¨ (pow-dV + p-dd ‚Č° 137)             -- 81+9 = 90
    alt-dV-EE    : ¬¨ (pow-dV + p-EE ‚Č° 137)             -- 81+36 = 117
    alt-dV-VE    : ¬¨ (pow-dV + p-VE ‚Č° 137)             -- 81+24 = 105

    -- ¬ß The winner
    the-formula  : pow-Vd * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137

law15A-2-all-power-formulas : AllPowerFormulaTests
law15A-2-all-power-formulas = record
  { alt-dV-Ōá-dd  = őĽ ()
  ; alt-VŌá-Ōá-dd  = őĽ ()
  ; alt-dŌá-Ōá-dd  = őĽ ()
  ; alt-Ed-Ōá-dd  = őĽ ()
  ; alt-EŌá-Ōá-dd  = őĽ ()
  ; alt-ŌáE-is-Vd = refl
  ; alt-ŌáE-same  = refl
  ; alt-Vd-dd    = őĽ ()
  ; alt-dV-dd    = őĽ ()
  ; alt-dV-EE    = őĽ ()
  ; alt-dV-VE    = őĽ ()
  ; the-formula  = refl
  }
\end{code}

\paragraph{Verdict.}
Taken together, these checks show that the canonical two-term expression remains distinguished within the enumerated candidate families.
\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Law 15A.3: 137 survives the enumerated candidate families.
record SimplexEval137-Exclusivity : Set where
  field
    no-pair-sums   : NoPairSumGives137
    power-formulas : AllPowerFormulaTests
    only-K4        : simplex-eval ‚Č° 137

law15A-3-simplexEval137-exclusivity : SimplexEval137-Exclusivity
law15A-3-simplexEval137-exclusivity = record
  { no-pair-sums   = law15A-1-no-pair-sum-137
  ; power-formulas = law15A-2-all-power-formulas
  ; only-K4        = refl
  }
\end{code}

\paragraph{Three-term decompositions.}
One can also ask how $137$ appears in three-term decompositions. The next record classifies the most relevant examples and separates the genuinely canonical one from merely accidental decompositions.

\begin{enumerate}
\item The decomposition $\chi + d^3 + E^2 d = 2 + 27 + 108$ reaches $137$, but it does so by isolating a cubic bulk term of $135$. In other words, the identity exists, yet it no longer reflects the compact invariant pattern that singled out the canonical two-term expression.
\item The decomposition $d^2 + V^2\chi + V^2E = 9 + 32 + 96$ also totals $137$, but here the last two summands collapse to $V^d \cdot \chi = 128$. This is therefore not a rival formula; it is the canonical expression written in expanded form.
\item The decomposition $1 + V^d + VEd = 1 + 64 + 72$ reaches $137$ only by adjoining the external constant $1$. Since $1$ is not among the basic $K_4$ invariants, this representation records an arithmetic coincidence rather than an intrinsic invariant identity.
\end{enumerate}

\noindent
The accompanying discussion explains how these decompositions relate to
the canonical formula and where they cease to reflect intrinsic $K_4$
structure.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Three-term decomposition classification.

-- ¬ß Triple 1: Ōá + d¬≥ + E¬≤d = 2 + 27 + 108 = 137.
triple-1-sum : simplex-chi + (simplex-degree * simplex-degree * simplex-degree)
             + (simplex-edges * simplex-edges * simplex-degree) ‚Č° 137
triple-1-sum = refl

-- ¬ß The two cubic terms sum to 135, which carries prime factor 5.
triple-1-cubic-part : (simplex-degree * simplex-degree * simplex-degree)
                    + (simplex-edges * simplex-edges * simplex-degree) ‚Č° 135
triple-1-cubic-part = refl

-- ¬ß 45 = d¬≤ + E¬≤ is not 2,3-smooth: it has prime factor 5.
triple-1-factor-45 : (simplex-degree * simplex-degree + simplex-edges * simplex-edges) ‚Č° 45
triple-1-factor-45 = refl

-- ¬ß Triple 2: d¬≤ + V¬≤Ōá + V¬≤E = 9 + 32 + 96 = 137.
triple-2-sum : p-dd + (p-VV * simplex-chi) + (p-VV * simplex-edges) ‚Č° 137
triple-2-sum = refl

-- ¬ß Triple 2 collapses: V¬≤Ōá + V¬≤E = V^d ¬∑ Ōá (the canonical bulk term).
triple-2-collapse : (p-VV * simplex-chi) + (p-VV * simplex-edges) ‚Č° pow-Vd * simplex-chi
triple-2-collapse = refl     -- 32 + 96 = 128 = 64 ¬∑ 2

-- ¬ß Triple 2 is the canonical formula V^d ¬∑ Ōá + d¬≤ in disguise.
triple-2-is-canonical : p-dd + ((p-VV * simplex-chi) + (p-VV * simplex-edges)) ‚Č° simplex-eval
triple-2-is-canonical = refl

-- ¬ß Triple 3: 1 + V^d + VEd = 1 + 64 + 72 = 137.
triple-3-sum : 1 + pow-Vd + (simplex-vertices * simplex-edges * simplex-degree) ‚Č° 137
triple-3-sum = refl

-- ¬ß The summand 1 is not a K‚āĄ invariant: 1 ‚ąČ {V, E, d, Ōá} = {4, 6, 3, 2}.
identity-not-V : ¬¨ (1 ‚Č° simplex-vertices)
identity-not-V = őĽ ()
identity-not-E : ¬¨ (1 ‚Č° simplex-edges)
identity-not-E = őĽ ()
identity-not-d : ¬¨ (1 ‚Č° simplex-degree)
identity-not-d = őĽ ()
identity-not-Ōá : ¬¨ (1 ‚Č° simplex-chi)
identity-not-Ōá = őĽ ()

-- ¬ß Packaging: three-term classification.
record ThreeTermClassification : Set where
  field
    t1-exists       : simplex-chi + (simplex-degree * simplex-degree * simplex-degree)
                    + (simplex-edges * simplex-edges * simplex-degree) ‚Č° 137
    t2-exists       : p-dd + (p-VV * simplex-chi) + (p-VV * simplex-edges) ‚Č° 137
    t3-exists       : 1 + pow-Vd + (simplex-vertices * simplex-edges * simplex-degree) ‚Č° 137
    t2-is-canonical : (p-VV * simplex-chi) + (p-VV * simplex-edges) ‚Č° pow-Vd * simplex-chi
    t1-escapes      : (simplex-degree * simplex-degree + simplex-edges * simplex-edges) ‚Č° 45
    t3-identity-out : ¬¨ (1 ‚Č° simplex-chi)

law15A-4-three-term-classification : ThreeTermClassification
law15A-4-three-term-classification = record
  { t1-exists       = refl
  ; t2-exists       = refl
  ; t3-exists       = refl
  ; t2-is-canonical = refl
  ; t1-escapes      = refl
  ; t3-identity-out = őĽ ()
  }
\end{code}

\paragraph{Unified decomposition record.}
The preceding calculations are then assembled into a single record summarizing both the exclusivity results and the three-term classification.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Unified decomposition record: the two-term formula survives every enumerated rival.
record SimplexEval137-Forced : Set where
  field
    exclusivity      : SimplexEval137-Exclusivity
    three-term-class : ThreeTermClassification

law15A-5-simplexEval137-forced : SimplexEval137-Forced
law15A-5-simplexEval137-forced = record
  { exclusivity      = law15A-3-simplexEval137-exclusivity
  ; three-term-class = law15A-4-three-term-classification
  }
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Exhaustive degree-bounded pair-sum closure}

The previous rival families were selected because they are the most
natural visible alternatives. One can push the comparison further by
checking all atomic monomials in $V$, $E$, $d$, and $\chi$ of total
degree at most $3$, together with all ordered pair-sums built from those
values. This is a finite $35\times 35$ grid. The computation below
counts the ordered pairs that sum to $137$ and obtains zero, so the
entire degree-bounded search space misses the value. In that precise
sense, the canonical formula first enters at degree $4$.
\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Exhaustive degree-‚Č§3 pair-sum closure for 137.

private
  -- ¬ß Indicator: 1 if m ‚Č° n, 0 otherwise.  Uses BUILTIN ‚ął.
  isEq‚Ąē : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
  isEq‚Ąē m n = 1 ‚ął ((m ‚ął n) + (n ‚ął m))

  -- ¬ß One-side hit count against the 35 monomial values.
  hits137-against : ‚Ąē ‚Üí ‚Ąē
  hits137-against v =
      isEq‚Ąē (v + 1)   137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 4)   137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 6)   137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 3)   137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 2)   137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 16)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 24)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 12)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 8)   137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 36)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 18)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 12)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 9)   137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 6)   137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 4)   137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 64)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 96)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 48)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 32)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 144) 137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 72)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 48)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 36)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 24)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 16)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 216) 137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 108) 137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 72)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 54)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 36)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 24)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 27)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 18)  137
    + isEq‚Ąē (v + 12)  137 + isEq‚Ąē (v + 8)   137

  -- ¬ß Total pair-sum hits: sum hits137-against over all 35 v.
  totalPairHits137 : ‚Ąē
  totalPairHits137 =
      hits137-against 1   + hits137-against 4   + hits137-against 6
    + hits137-against 3   + hits137-against 2
    + hits137-against 16  + hits137-against 24  + hits137-against 12
    + hits137-against 8   + hits137-against 36  + hits137-against 18
    + hits137-against 12  + hits137-against 9   + hits137-against 6
    + hits137-against 4
    + hits137-against 64  + hits137-against 96  + hits137-against 48
    + hits137-against 32  + hits137-against 144 + hits137-against 72
    + hits137-against 48  + hits137-against 36  + hits137-against 24
    + hits137-against 16  + hits137-against 216 + hits137-against 108
    + hits137-against 72  + hits137-against 54  + hits137-against 36
    + hits137-against 24  + hits137-against 27  + hits137-against 18
    + hits137-against 12  + hits137-against 8

-- ¬ß No degree-‚Č§3 ordered pair-sum reaches 137.
theorem-no-pair-sum-degree-3 : totalPairHits137 ‚Č° 0
theorem-no-pair-sum-degree-3 = refl

-- ¬ß Single-monomial closure: 137 itself is not a degree-‚Č§3 monomial value.
private
  hits137-single : ‚Ąē
  hits137-single =
      isEq‚Ąē 1   137 + isEq‚Ąē 4   137 + isEq‚Ąē 6   137 + isEq‚Ąē 3   137
    + isEq‚Ąē 2   137
    + isEq‚Ąē 16  137 + isEq‚Ąē 24  137 + isEq‚Ąē 12  137 + isEq‚Ąē 8   137
    + isEq‚Ąē 36  137 + isEq‚Ąē 18  137 + isEq‚Ąē 12  137 + isEq‚Ąē 9   137
    + isEq‚Ąē 6   137 + isEq‚Ąē 4   137
    + isEq‚Ąē 64  137 + isEq‚Ąē 96  137 + isEq‚Ąē 48  137 + isEq‚Ąē 32  137
    + isEq‚Ąē 144 137 + isEq‚Ąē 72  137 + isEq‚Ąē 48  137 + isEq‚Ąē 36  137
    + isEq‚Ąē 24  137 + isEq‚Ąē 16  137 + isEq‚Ąē 216 137 + isEq‚Ąē 108 137
    + isEq‚Ąē 72  137 + isEq‚Ąē 54  137 + isEq‚Ąē 36  137 + isEq‚Ąē 24  137
    + isEq‚Ąē 27  137 + isEq‚Ąē 18  137 + isEq‚Ąē 12  137 + isEq‚Ąē 8   137

theorem-137-not-single-mono : hits137-single ‚Č° 0
theorem-137-not-single-mono = refl

-- ¬ß Closure record: degree-‚Č§3 pair-sums and singletons both miss 137.
record DegreeBoundedExhaustion : Set where
  field
    no-single-mono : hits137-single ‚Č° 0
    no-pair-sum    : totalPairHits137 ‚Č° 0
    canonical-deg4 : pow-Vd * simplex-chi + p-dd ‚Č° 137
