  (curvature-reference-only-ref-upper ,
  curvature-reference-only-not-forced)
\end{code}

Invariance blocks the reference-only rival in all three simplicial dimensions. The same argument structure extends to the remaining filters: in each case, a concrete counterexample passes all filters but one, proving that filter essential. The derivation closes when every filter is traced to a graph-theoretic property of $K_4$.

\paragraph{Filter derivation: the lower bound is non-redundant.}
The zero law assigns weight~$0$ to every simplex. It satisfies locality (propagation: $P(v,v) = 0$ trivially), $K_4$-invariance (trivially), and the upper bracket ($0 \leq 1$). It fails the lower bracket: $1 \leq 0$ is false. Without the lower bound, the zero law survives in every simplicial dimension.

The lower bound is not a design choice. $K_4$ is the complete graph on four vertices: every possible edge, face, and vertex exists. A weight of~$0$ on an existing simplex declares it structurally absent---it carries no content, contributes nothing to the simplicial complex. This contradicts completeness. Non-degeneracy ($\geq 1$ on the reference simplex) is forced by the fact that $K_4$ has no missing simplices.

\paragraph{Filter derivation: the upper bound is non-redundant.}
The doubled law (propagation: $P(v,w) = 2 \cdot \mathrm{adjacent}(v,w)$) and the constant-$2$ law (action, curvature) are invariant and non-degenerate. They satisfy locality (propagation) and the lower bracket. They fail the upper bracket: $2 \leq 1$ is false. Without the upper bound, these rivals survive.

The upper bound is forced by the zero-parameter constraint. In~$\mathbb{N}$, once non-degeneracy fixes a lower bound of~$1$, any value $n > 1$ requires an external input distinguishing it from~$1$. That input would be a free parameter. Since the system admits none, the weight collapses to the minimal non-degenerate value. Minimality ($\leq 1$ on the reference simplex) is the arithmetical shadow of having zero free parameters.

\paragraph{Filter derivation: locality is non-redundant (propagation).}
The constant-$1$ law assigns weight~$1$ to every vertex pair, including the diagonal. It is $K_4$-invariant, non-degenerate, and minimal. It fails locality: $P(v,v) = 1 \neq 0$. Without locality, this rival survives in the propagation dimension.

Locality is forced by $K_4$ being a simple graph. A simple graph has no self-loops: there is no edge from a vertex to itself. The propagation kernel must vanish on the diagonal because there is no simplex to propagate along. This constraint has no analogue in the action and curvature dimensions, where every simplex is a distinct combinatorial object.

\paragraph{The derivation theorem.}
Four structural properties of $K_4$---transitivity of $\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$ on each simplicial orbit, completeness, simplicity, and the absence of free parameters---generate exactly the admissibility filters used in the three forced-constant chapters. Each property produces one filter:
%
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.36\linewidth}p{0.20\linewidth}p{0.22\linewidth}@{}}
\textbf{$K_4$ property} & \textbf{Filter} & \textbf{Derived from} \\
$\mathrm{Aut}(K_4)$ transitivity & invariance & labeling independence \\
completeness (no missing simplices) & lower bound $\geq 1$ & non-degeneracy \\
zero free parameters & upper bound $\leq 1$ & minimality \\
simplicity (no self-loops) & locality ($P(v,v)=0$) & propagation only \\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
%
Completeness is proved by the three forced-constant theorems (each filter set forces a unique unit weight). Irredundancy is proved by ten essentiality witnesses: four for propagation, three each for action and curvature. Every proper subset of filters admits a concrete rival that the remaining filters cannot block.

Taken together, these results establish both sides of the claim. The
three forcing theorems show completeness: once all admissibility data
are present, only the unit law survives in each simplicial dimension.
The ten essentiality witnesses show irredundancy: if any one condition
is removed, a concrete rival remains. The admissibility basis is thus
not an external layer placed over $K_4$, but a minimal filter system
recovered from the graph-theoretic properties listed above.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß¬ß Filter derivation: every filter is essential, every filter is derived.

-- ¬ß Propagation: without locality, the constant-1 law survives.
propagation-needs-locality :
  IsK4PermInvariant propagation-all-one √ó
  ReferenceEdgeLower propagation-all-one √ó
  ReferenceEdgeUpper propagation-all-one √ó
  (propagation-all-one v‚āÄ v‚āÄ ‚Č° adjacent v‚āÄ v‚āÄ ‚Üí ‚ä•)
propagation-needs-locality =
  propagation-all-one-invariant ,
  (propagation-all-one-ref-lower ,
  (propagation-all-one-ref-upper ,
  propagation-all-one-not-adj))

-- ¬ß Propagation: without the lower bound, the zero law survives.
propagation-needs-lower :
  IsLocal propagation-zero √ó
  IsK4PermInvariant propagation-zero √ó
  ReferenceEdgeUpper propagation-zero √ó
  (propagation-zero v‚āÄ v‚āĀ ‚Č° adjacent v‚āÄ v‚āĀ ‚Üí ‚ä•)
propagation-needs-lower =
  propagation-zero-local ,
  (propagation-zero-invariant ,
  (propagation-zero-ref-upper ,
  propagation-zero-not-adj))

-- ¬ß Propagation: without the upper bound, the doubled law survives.
propagation-needs-upper :
  IsLocal propagation-double √ó
  IsK4PermInvariant propagation-double √ó
  ReferenceEdgeLower propagation-double √ó
  (propagation-double v‚āÄ v‚āĀ ‚Č° adjacent v‚āÄ v‚āĀ ‚Üí ‚ä•)
propagation-needs-upper =
  propagation-double-local ,
  (propagation-double-invariant ,
  (propagation-double-ref-lower ,
  propagation-double-not-adj))

-- ¬ß Action: without the lower bound, the zero action survives.
action-needs-lower :
  IsActionInvariant action-zero √ó
  ReferenceTriangleUpper action-zero √ó
  (action-zero tri-012 ‚Č° 1 ‚Üí ‚ä•)
action-needs-lower =
  action-zero-invariant ,
  (action-zero-ref-upper ,
  action-zero-not-forced)

-- ¬ß Action: without the upper bound, the constant-2 action survives.
action-needs-upper :
  IsActionInvariant action-two √ó
  ReferenceTriangleLower action-two √ó
  (action-two tri-012 ‚Č° 1 ‚Üí ‚ä•)
action-needs-upper =
  action-two-invariant ,
  (action-two-ref-lower ,
  action-two-not-forced)

-- ¬ß Curvature: without the lower bound, the zero curvature survives.
curvature-needs-lower :
  IsCurvatureInvariant curvature-zero √ó
  ReferenceVertexUpper curvature-zero √ó
  (curvature-zero v‚āÄ ‚Č° 1 ‚Üí ‚ä•)
curvature-needs-lower =
  curvature-zero-invariant ,
  (curvature-zero-ref-upper ,
  curvature-zero-not-forced)

-- ¬ß Curvature: without the upper bound, the constant-2 curvature survives.
curvature-needs-upper :
  IsCurvatureInvariant curvature-two √ó
  ReferenceVertexLower curvature-two √ó
  (curvature-two v‚āÄ ‚Č° 1 ‚Üí ‚ä•)
curvature-needs-upper =
  curvature-two-invariant ,
  (curvature-two-ref-lower ,
  curvature-two-not-forced)
\end{code}

The filter architecture is closed. Ten essentiality witnesses prove irredundancy; three forced-constant theorems prove completeness. The admissibility basis is the unique minimal complete filter set on the $K_4$ simplicial complex, derived from the graph-theoretic identity of $K_4$ as a complete, simple, vertex-transitive graph with no free parameters.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{Forced Edge and Matching Orbits}
\label{ch:forced-edge-matching-orbits}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

The previous chapter worked with ordered pairs of distinct vertices.
Edges, however, carry no orientation in $K_4$, because adjacency is
symmetric. One therefore has to pass from ordered pairs to unordered
pairs of distinct vertices and then ask how the automorphism group acts
on these new objects. The same question appears one step higher for
perfect matchings, which partition the four vertices into two disjoint
pairs.

This chapter carries out that passage. It defines edges as unordered
distinct pairs, proves that every edge is equivalent to one of the six
standard ones, lifts the vertex action to edges, classifies the three
perfect matchings, and shows that both edges and matchings form single
Aut$(K_4)$-orbits. The resulting orbit counts are then assembled into a
single record and used in the rigidity arguments that follow.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Edges as unordered pairs}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:edges-unordered}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

A $K_4$ edge is an unordered pair of distinct vertices. The distinctness witness excludes the diagonal, so the type matches the edge set of a simple graph.

The record \texttt{K4Edge} therefore stores two vertices together with a
proof that they are different, and the relation \texttt{\_$\approx$E\_}
identifies the two possible orientations of the same geometric edge.
The map \texttt{swap-edge} is the obvious involution reversing the order
of the endpoints, and the basic lemmas simply record that this reversal
does not change the edge class.

\begin{code}
record K4Edge : Set where
  constructor edge
  field
    src      : K4Vertex
    dst      : K4Vertex
    distinct : src ‚Č† dst

open K4Edge public

swap-edge : K4Edge ‚Üí K4Edge
swap-edge e = record
  { src      = dst e
  ; dst      = src e
  ; distinct = őĽ p ‚Üí distinct e (sym p)
  }

data _‚ČąE_ : K4Edge ‚Üí K4Edge ‚Üí Set where
  same-orient : (e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge) ‚Üí
                src e‚āĀ ‚Č° src e‚āā ‚Üí dst e‚āĀ ‚Č° dst e‚āā ‚Üí e‚āĀ ‚ČąE e‚āā
  swap-orient : (e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge) ‚Üí
                src e‚āĀ ‚Č° dst e‚āā ‚Üí dst e‚āĀ ‚Č° src e‚āā ‚Üí e‚āĀ ‚ČąE e‚āā

‚ČąE-refl : (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí e ‚ČąE e
‚ČąE-refl e = same-orient e e refl refl

swap-edge-‚ČąE : (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí swap-edge e ‚ČąE e
swap-edge-‚ČąE e = swap-orient (swap-edge e) e refl refl

swap-edge-involutive : (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí swap-edge (swap-edge e) ‚ČąE e
swap-edge-involutive e = same-orient (swap-edge (swap-edge e)) e refl refl
\end{code}

The six concrete edges of $K_4$---all $\binom{4}{2}$ unordered pairs of vertices---are named once for use in the matching classification.

\begin{code}
e01 e02 e03 e12 e13 e23 : K4Edge
e01 = edge v‚āÄ v‚āĀ (őĽ ())
e02 = edge v‚āÄ v‚āā (őĽ ())
e03 = edge v‚āÄ v‚āÉ (őĽ ())
e12 = edge v‚āĀ v‚āā (őĽ ())
e13 = edge v‚āĀ v‚āÉ (őĽ ())
e23 = edge v‚āā v‚āÉ (őĽ ())
\end{code}

These six names exhaust the edge type. The classification theorem checks all ordered vertex pairs, discards the diagonal cases, and groups the remaining pairs by orientation.

The proof of \texttt{edge-classify} is a complete case distinction on
the sixteen ordered vertex pairs. Four diagonal cases are impossible by
the distinctness witness, and the remaining twelve off-diagonal cases
fall into six classes paired by orientation reversal. In this way the
list of six edges is shown to be exhaustive rather than merely
convenient.

\begin{code}
edge-classify : (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí
                (e ‚ČąE e01) ‚äé (e ‚ČąE e02) ‚äé (e ‚ČąE e03) ‚äé
                (e ‚ČąE e12) ‚äé (e ‚ČąE e13) ‚äé (e ‚ČąE e23)
edge-classify (edge v‚āÄ v‚āÄ d) = ‚ä•-elim (d refl)
edge-classify (edge v‚āÄ v‚āĀ d) = inj‚āĀ (same-orient _ _ refl refl)
edge-classify (edge v‚āÄ v‚āā d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (same-orient _ _ refl refl))
edge-classify (edge v‚āÄ v‚āÉ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (same-orient _ _ refl refl)))
edge-classify (edge v‚āĀ v‚āÄ d) = inj‚āĀ (swap-orient _ _ refl refl)
edge-classify (edge v‚āĀ v‚āĀ d) = ‚ä•-elim (d refl)
edge-classify (edge v‚āĀ v‚āā d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (same-orient _ _ refl refl))))
edge-classify (edge v‚āĀ v‚āÉ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (same-orient _ _ refl refl)))))
edge-classify (edge v‚āā v‚āÄ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (swap-orient _ _ refl refl))
edge-classify (edge v‚āā v‚āĀ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (swap-orient _ _ refl refl))))
edge-classify (edge v‚āā v‚āā d) = ‚ä•-elim (d refl)
edge-classify (edge v‚āā v‚āÉ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (same-orient _ _ refl refl)))))
edge-classify (edge v‚āÉ v‚āÄ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (swap-orient _ _ refl refl)))
edge-classify (edge v‚āÉ v‚āĀ d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ (swap-orient _ _ refl refl)))))
edge-classify (edge v‚āÉ v‚āā d) = inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (inj‚āā (swap-orient _ _ refl refl)))))
edge-classify (edge v‚āÉ v‚āÉ d) = ‚ä•-elim (d refl)
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The induced edge action}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:edge-action}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

A permutation of vertices induces an action on edges by transporting both endpoints. Injectivity preserves distinctness, so the construction descends directly to the edge type.

The natural action on edges is obtained by transporting both endpoints.
Injectivity of the underlying permutation preserves distinctness, so the
result is again a valid edge. The lemma \texttt{edge-act-swap} records
that this transported action is compatible with the orientation quotient,
and \texttt{edge-act-id} is the expected normalization at the identity.

\begin{code}
edge-act : K4Perm ‚Üí K4Edge ‚Üí K4Edge
edge-act ŌÉ e = record
  { src      = k4to ŌÉ (src e)
  ; dst      = k4to ŌÉ (dst e)
  ; distinct = őĽ p ‚Üí distinct e (k4-injective ŌÉ (src e) (dst e) p)
  }

edge-act-swap : (ŌÉ : K4Perm) ‚Üí (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí
                edge-act ŌÉ (swap-edge e) ‚ČąE swap-edge (edge-act ŌÉ e)
edge-act-swap ŌÉ e = same-orient _ _ refl refl
\end{code}

The identity permutation acts trivially on every edge, as expected.

\begin{code}
id-perm : K4Perm
id-perm = record
  { k4to       = őĽ v ‚Üí v
  ; k4from     = őĽ v ‚Üí v
  ; k4-to-from = őĽ _ ‚Üí refl
  ; k4-from-to = őĽ _ ‚Üí refl
  }

edge-act-id : (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí edge-act id-perm e ‚ČąE e
edge-act-id e = same-orient _ _ refl refl
\end{code}

The induced action is unique among functions that transport the two endpoints of an edge, possibly with reversed orientation. The predicate \texttt{LiftsEdgeAction} isolates exactly these competitors, and the uniqueness proof is a case split on the orientation tag.

The predicate \texttt{LiftsEdgeAction} isolates exactly the admissible
competitors: an action may preserve or reverse orientation, but it may
not send an edge to anything outside the image of its two endpoints
under the vertex permutation. Under this hypothesis,
\texttt{edge-act-unique} is immediate by a two-case analysis on the
orientation tag. Pointwise endpoint transport is therefore the only
induced action on edges up to \texttt{\_$\approx$E\_}.

\begin{code}
LiftsEdgeAction : (K4Perm ‚Üí K4Edge ‚Üí K4Edge) ‚Üí Set
LiftsEdgeAction act = (ŌÉ : K4Perm) ‚Üí (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí
  ((src (act ŌÉ e) ‚Č° k4to ŌÉ (src e)) √ó (dst (act ŌÉ e) ‚Č° k4to ŌÉ (dst e)))
  ‚äé
  ((src (act ŌÉ e) ‚Č° k4to ŌÉ (dst e)) √ó (dst (act ŌÉ e) ‚Č° k4to ŌÉ (src e)))

edge-act-lifts : LiftsEdgeAction edge-act
edge-act-lifts ŌÉ e = inj‚āĀ (refl , refl)

edge-act-unique : (act : K4Perm ‚Üí K4Edge ‚Üí K4Edge) ‚Üí
                  LiftsEdgeAction act ‚Üí
                  (ŌÉ : K4Perm) ‚Üí (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí
                  act ŌÉ e ‚ČąE edge-act ŌÉ e
edge-act-unique act lifts ŌÉ e with lifts ŌÉ e
... | inj‚āĀ (ps , pd) = same-orient _ _ ps pd
... | inj‚āā (ps , pd) = swap-orient _ _ ps pd
\end{code}

Every \texttt{K4Perm} has an inverse obtained by swapping its forward
and backward maps.

\begin{code}
k4Perm-inv : K4Perm ‚Üí K4Perm
k4Perm-inv ŌÉ = record
  { k4to       = k4from ŌÉ
  ; k4from     = k4to ŌÉ
  ; k4-to-from = k4-from-to ŌÉ
  ; k4-from-to = k4-to-from ŌÉ
  }
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Single edge orbit}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:edge-orbit}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

To show that edges form a single orbit, it suffices to send a fixed reference edge to the source and target and compose the resulting permutations. This is the same conjugation argument used earlier for ordered pairs.

The proof of edge transitivity is the standard conjugation argument. One
chooses a permutation sending $(v_0,v_1)$ to the endpoints of the source
edge and another sending $(v_0,v_1)$ to the endpoints of the target
edge; composing the second with the inverse of the first yields a
permutation carrying one edge to the other. Hence all six edges lie in a
single Aut$(K_4)$-orbit.

\begin{code}
edge-transitive : (e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge) ‚Üí
                  ő£ K4Perm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí edge-act ŌÉ e‚āĀ ‚ČąE e‚āā)
edge-transitive e‚āĀ e‚āā =
  let em‚āĀ = edge-map (src e‚āĀ) (dst e‚āĀ) (distinct e‚āĀ)
      em‚āā = edge-map (src e‚āā) (dst e‚āā) (distinct e‚āā)
      ŌÉ‚āĀ  = fst em‚āĀ
      p‚āĀs = fst (snd em‚āĀ)
      p‚āĀd = snd (snd em‚āĀ)
      ŌÉ‚āā  = fst em‚āā
      p‚āās = fst (snd em‚āā)
      p‚āād = snd (snd em‚āā)
      ŌÉ   = k4Perm-comp ŌÉ‚āā (k4Perm-inv ŌÉ‚āĀ)
      qs  : k4to ŌÉ (src e‚āĀ) ‚Č° src e‚āā
      qs  = trans (cong (k4to ŌÉ‚āā)
                        (trans (cong (k4from ŌÉ‚āĀ) (sym p‚āĀs))
                               (k4-from-to ŌÉ‚āĀ v‚āÄ)))
                  p‚āās
      qd  : k4to ŌÉ (dst e‚āĀ) ‚Č° dst e‚āā
      qd  = trans (cong (k4to ŌÉ‚āā)
                        (trans (cong (k4from ŌÉ‚āĀ) (sym p‚āĀd))
                               (k4-from-to ŌÉ‚āĀ v‚āĀ)))
                  p‚āād
  in (ŌÉ , same-orient (edge-act ŌÉ e‚āĀ) e‚āā qs qd)
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{\texorpdfstring{Perfect matchings of $K_4$}{Perfect matchings of K4}}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:matchings}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

A perfect matching partitions the four vertices into two disjoint pairs. Once the partner of $v_0$ is fixed, the second pair is determined, so only three cases remain.

Once the partner of $v_0$ is chosen, the remaining edge is determined by
the two leftover vertices. This leaves exactly three possibilities,
recorded by the constructors of \texttt{K4Matching}. The function
\texttt{matching-edges} simply unfolds each constructor into the
corresponding pair of skew edges.

\begin{code}
data K4Matching : Set where
  m01-23 : K4Matching
  m02-13 : K4Matching
  m03-12 : K4Matching

matching-edges : K4Matching ‚Üí K4Edge √ó K4Edge
matching-edges m01-23 = e01 , e23
matching-edges m02-13 = e02 , e13
matching-edges m03-12 = e03 , e12
\end{code}

The three constructors list the obvious candidates, but the classification theorem still has to show that there is no fourth matching. It does so by case analysis on the partner of $v_0$.

To prove exhaustion, the development widens the search space to
\texttt{MatchingCandidate}, whose only datum is the chosen partner of
$v_0$ together with a proof that this choice is distinct from $v_0$.
Case distinction on that partner does the rest: the choice $v_0$ is
impossible, while the remaining three choices correspond exactly to the
three named matchings. In this sense the classification theorem is a
genuine exhaustion result rather than a restatement of the data type.

\begin{code}
record MatchingCandidate : Set where
  field
    partner-of-v‚āÄ : K4Vertex
    distinct‚āÄ     : v‚āÄ ‚Č† partner-of-v‚āÄ

open MatchingCandidate public

matching-classify : (c : MatchingCandidate) ‚Üí
                    (partner-of-v‚āÄ c ‚Č° v‚āĀ) ‚äé
                    (partner-of-v‚āÄ c ‚Č° v‚āā) ‚äé
                    (partner-of-v‚āÄ c ‚Č° v‚āÉ)
matching-classify
  record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āÄ ; distinct‚āÄ = d } = ‚ä•-elim (d refl)
matching-classify
  record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āĀ ; distinct‚āÄ = _ } = inj‚āĀ refl
matching-classify
  record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āā ; distinct‚āÄ = _ } = inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ refl)
matching-classify
  record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āÉ ; distinct‚āÄ = _ } = inj‚āā (inj‚āā refl)

candidate‚Üímatching : MatchingCandidate ‚Üí K4Matching
candidate‚Üímatching c with matching-classify c
... | inj‚āĀ _        = m01-23
... | inj‚āā (inj‚āĀ _) = m02-13
... | inj‚āā (inj‚āā _) = m03-12

matching‚Üícandidate : K4Matching ‚Üí MatchingCandidate
matching‚Üícandidate m01-23 = record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āĀ ; distinct‚āÄ = őĽ () }
matching‚Üícandidate m02-13 = record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āā ; distinct‚āÄ = őĽ () }
matching‚Üícandidate m03-12 = record { partner-of-v‚āÄ = v‚āÉ ; distinct‚āÄ = őĽ () }

candidate-section : (m : K4Matching) ‚Üí
                    candidate‚Üímatching (matching‚Üícandidate m) ‚Č° m
candidate-section m01-23 = refl
candidate-section m02-13 = refl
candidate-section m03-12 = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The three transpositions}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:transpositions}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

Matching transitivity is witnessed by three explicit transpositions: $(1\,2)$, $(1\,3)$, and $(2\,3)$. They fix $v_0$ and permute the remaining labels, which is enough to move between the three matchings.

These transpositions are all that is needed for the orbit computation.
Each fixes $v_0$ and permutes two of the remaining three vertices, so
together with the identity they connect every choice of partner for
$v_0$ with every other choice. Their definitions are completely
explicit, and the round-trip laws close by inspection.

\begin{code}
swap-12-perm : K4Perm
swap-12-perm = record
  { k4to       = sw
  ; k4from     = sw
  ; k4-to-from = swsw
  ; k4-from-to = swsw
  }
  where
    sw : K4Vertex ‚Üí K4Vertex
    sw v‚āÄ = v‚āÄ
    sw v‚āĀ = v‚āā
    sw v‚āā = v‚āĀ
    sw v‚āÉ = v‚āÉ
    swsw : (v : K4Vertex) ‚Üí sw (sw v) ‚Č° v
    swsw v‚āÄ = refl
    swsw v‚āĀ = refl
    swsw v‚āā = refl
    swsw v‚āÉ = refl

swap-13-perm : K4Perm
swap-13-perm = record
  { k4to       = sw
  ; k4from     = sw
  ; k4-to-from = swsw
  ; k4-from-to = swsw
  }
  where
    sw : K4Vertex ‚Üí K4Vertex
    sw v‚āÄ = v‚āÄ
    sw v‚āĀ = v‚āÉ
    sw v‚āā = v‚āā
    sw v‚āÉ = v‚āĀ
    swsw : (v : K4Vertex) ‚Üí sw (sw v) ‚Č° v
    swsw v‚āÄ = refl
    swsw v‚āĀ = refl
    swsw v‚āā = refl
    swsw v‚āÉ = refl

swap-23-perm : K4Perm
swap-23-perm = record
  { k4to       = sw
  ; k4from     = sw
  ; k4-to-from = swsw
  ; k4-from-to = swsw
  }
  where
    sw : K4Vertex ‚Üí K4Vertex
    sw v‚āÄ = v‚āÄ
    sw v‚āĀ = v‚āĀ
    sw v‚āā = v‚āÉ
    sw v‚āÉ = v‚āā
    swsw : (v : K4Vertex) ‚Üí sw (sw v) ‚Č° v
    swsw v‚āÄ = refl
    swsw v‚āĀ = refl
    swsw v‚āā = refl
    swsw v‚āÉ = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Single matching orbit}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:matching-orbit}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The three explicit transpositions connect any matching with any other.
Diagonal cases use the identity permutation, and off-diagonal cases use
one of the three swaps.

The orbit computation is therefore finite and explicit. The diagonal
cases use the identity permutation, and each off-diagonal case is
handled by the appropriate transposition. Consequently the three perfect
matchings form a single orbit under Aut$(K_4)$.

\begin{code}
MatchingOrbitWitness : K4Matching ‚Üí K4Matching ‚Üí Set
MatchingOrbitWitness m‚āĀ m‚āā =
  ő£ K4Perm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí
    (edge-act ŌÉ (fst (matching-edges m‚āĀ)) ‚ČąE fst (matching-edges m‚āā))
    √ó (edge-act ŌÉ (snd (matching-edges m‚āĀ)) ‚ČąE snd (matching-edges m‚āā)))

matching-transitive : (m‚āĀ m‚āā : K4Matching) ‚Üí MatchingOrbitWitness m‚āĀ m‚āā
matching-transitive m01-23 m01-23 =
  id-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m01-23 m02-13 =
  swap-12-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m01-23 m03-12 =
  swap-13-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , swap-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m02-13 m01-23 =
  swap-12-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m02-13 m02-13 =
  id-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m02-13 m03-12 =
  swap-23-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m03-12 m01-23 =
  swap-13-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , swap-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m03-12 m02-13 =
  swap-23-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
matching-transitive m03-12 m03-12 =
  id-perm ,
  ( same-orient _ _ refl refl
  , same-orient _ _ refl refl )
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The orbit-count invariant}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:invariants}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The vertex, edge, and matching transitivity results are collected into a single record. This packages the three orbit computations for later use.

The record \texttt{AutK4OrbitCounts} simply gathers the three orbit
collapse theorems into one place. This is mainly an organizational step,
but an important one for the later chapters: they can appeal to a
single witness rather than re-quote vertex, edge, and matching
transitivity separately. In particular, the orbit counts of vertices,
edges, and perfect matchings are all equal to $1$, while the associated
cardinalities $4$, $6$, and $3$ remain fixed combinatorial invariants of
the tetrahedron.

\begin{code}
record AutK4OrbitCounts : Set where
  field
    vertex-orbits   :
      (a a' : K4Vertex) ‚Üí ő£ K4Perm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí k4to ŌÉ a ‚Č° a')
    edge-orbits     :
      (e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge) ‚Üí ő£ K4Perm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí edge-act ŌÉ e‚āĀ ‚ČąE e‚āā)
    matching-orbits :
      (m‚āĀ m‚āā : K4Matching) ‚Üí MatchingOrbitWitness m‚āĀ m‚āā

theorem-aut-k4-orbits : AutK4OrbitCounts
theorem-aut-k4-orbits = record
  { vertex-orbits   = k4Perm-send
  ; edge-orbits     = edge-transitive
  ; matching-orbits = matching-transitive
  }
\end{code}

The record states that vertices, edges, and perfect matchings each form a single orbit under Aut$(K_4)$.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{\texorpdfstring{The $K_4$ spherical cell law}{The K4 spherical cell law}}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:spherical-cell-law}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The forced terminus $K_4$ is not merely a complete graph. Read as a $2$-complex, it is the tetrahedral triangulation of a two-sphere: it carries $V = 4$ vertices, $E = 6$ edges, and $F = 4$ triangular faces, with Euler characteristic $\chi = V - E + F = 2$. The spherical topology is therefore not imposed after the fact; it is already present in the surviving finite structure.

The cell law collected here is purely combinatorial. It states four things that hold by computation:
the topological identity $V + F = E + \chi$ (i.e.\ $4 + 4 = 6 + 2$);
the self-duality $F = V$ of the tetrahedron;
the handshaking identity $E \cdot \chi = V \cdot d$ (i.e.\ $6 \cdot 2 = 4 \cdot 3 = 12$), which rewrites to the exact ratio $E / V = d / 2 = 3/2$;
and the orbit-quotient ledger that records vertices, edges, and perfect matchings as single Aut$(K_4)$-orbits.

\textbf{Constraint:}
Any spherical-cell invariant on $K_4$ must be a single object that bundles (i)~the topological identity with $\chi(S^2)$, (ii)~the edge count $E$, (iii)~the vertex count $V$, and (iv)~the proof that Aut$(K_4)$ has the relevant orbit transport. Without all four, the ratio $E/V$ would be a loose number rather than a symmetry-controlled invariant.

\textbf{Construction:}
The record \texttt{K4SphericalCellLaw} packages exactly these four facts. Each field reduces to \texttt{refl} on $K_4$-invariants, except \texttt{aut-orbit-ledger}, which is \texttt{theorem-aut-k4-orbits}. No further data is needed; the cell law is forced by the same combinatorics that already produced $V$, $E$, $F$, $d$, $\chi$.

\textbf{Consequence:}
Every later projection out of \emph{Void} must factor through this record if it uses the spherical $K_4$ cell. The ratio $E / V = 3 / 2$ is exact and Aut-invariant here; any extra meaning attached to that ratio belongs outside the eliminative chain.

\begin{code}
record K4SphericalCellLaw : Set where
  field
    topological-sphere-cell : K4-V + K4-F ‚Č° K4-E + K4-chi
    self-dual-count         : K4-F ‚Č° K4-V
    handshaking-cell-law    : K4-E * K4-chi ‚Č° K4-V * K4-deg
    aut-orbit-ledger        : AutK4OrbitCounts

theorem-k4-spherical-cell-law : K4SphericalCellLaw
theorem-k4-spherical-cell-law = record
  { topological-sphere-cell = refl
  ; self-dual-count         = refl
  ; handshaking-cell-law    = refl
  ; aut-orbit-ledger        = theorem-aut-k4-orbits
  }
\end{code}

The cell law is the formal statement that $K_4$ is the minimal triangulated sphere and that its ratio $E/V = 3/2$ is a forced combinatorial invariant.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{\texorpdfstring{$2$-transitive action of Aut$(K_4)$}{2-transitive action of Aut(K4)}}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:two-transitive}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The next step strengthens vertex transitivity to ordered pairs of distinct vertices. The same sender-and-inverse construction yields a permutation carrying one ordered pair to any other.

The same conjugation idea yields the stronger statement of
$2$-transitivity on ordered distinct pairs. Starting from witnesses that
send $(v_0,v_1)$ to $(a,b)$ and $(a',b')$, one composes the second with
the inverse of the first and reads off the resulting identities on both
coordinates. Thus the $12$ ordered distinct pairs also form a single
orbit.

\begin{code}
pair-transitive : (a b a' b' : K4Vertex) ‚Üí
                  a ‚Č† b ‚Üí a' ‚Č† b' ‚Üí
                  ő£ K4Perm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí (k4to ŌÉ a ‚Č° a') √ó (k4to ŌÉ b ‚Č° b'))
pair-transitive a b a' b' nab na'b' =
  let em‚āĀ = edge-map a b nab
      em‚āā = edge-map a' b' na'b'
      ŌÉ‚āĀ  = fst em‚āĀ
      p‚āĀs = fst (snd em‚āĀ)
      p‚āĀd = snd (snd em‚āĀ)
      ŌÉ‚āā  = fst em‚āā
      p‚āās = fst (snd em‚āā)
      p‚āād = snd (snd em‚āā)
      ŌÉ   = k4Perm-comp ŌÉ‚āā (k4Perm-inv ŌÉ‚āĀ)
      qs  : k4to ŌÉ a ‚Č° a'
      qs  = trans (cong (k4to ŌÉ‚āā)
                        (trans (cong (k4from ŌÉ‚āĀ) (sym p‚āĀs))
                               (k4-from-to ŌÉ‚āĀ v‚āÄ)))
                  p‚āās
      qd  : k4to ŌÉ b ‚Č° b'
      qd  = trans (cong (k4to ŌÉ‚āā)
                        (trans (cong (k4from ŌÉ‚āĀ) (sym p‚āĀd))
                               (k4-from-to ŌÉ‚āĀ v‚āĀ)))
                  p‚āād
  in ŌÉ , (qs , qd)

theorem-aut-k4-2-transitive :
  (a b a' b' : K4Vertex) ‚Üí a ‚Č† b ‚Üí a' ‚Č† b' ‚Üí
  ő£ K4Perm (őĽ ŌÉ ‚Üí (k4to ŌÉ a ‚Č° a') √ó (k4to ŌÉ b ‚Č° b'))
theorem-aut-k4-2-transitive = pair-transitive
\end{code}

This is the formal content of the statement that Aut$(K_4)$ acts $2$-transitively on vertices.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{\texorpdfstring{Aut$(K_4)$-invariance of $\alpha$, $\kappa$, $d$}{Aut(K4)-invariance of alpha, kappa, d}}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:autk4-invariance}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The constants $d = 3$, $\kappa = 8$, and $\alpha\text{-denom} = 111$ are used later as global observables. To justify that role, one has to show that they are invariant under vertex relabelling.

The point is formalized by the notion of a vertex-rigid scalar: a
function on vertices that is invariant under every \texttt{K4Perm}.
The collapse theorem \texttt{vertex-rigid-constant} shows that such a
scalar is automatically constant on the unique vertex orbit. Applying
this to the constant functions with values $d$, $\kappa$, and
$\alpha\text{-denom}$ produces the bundled record
\texttt{AutK4Invariants}. Each quantity is an invariant under
$Aut(K_4)$. Any deviation would break the symmetry chain. It is
therefore a necessary observable.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Vertex-rigid scalar.
VertexRigid : (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí Set
VertexRigid f = (ŌÉ : K4Perm) (v : K4Vertex) ‚Üí f (k4to ŌÉ v) ‚Č° f v

-- ¬ß Vertex rigidity collapses to constancy.
vertex-rigid-constant :
  (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí VertexRigid f ‚Üí
  (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí f v ‚Č° f w
vertex-rigid-constant f rig v w with k4Perm-send v w
... | ŌÉ , p = trans (sym (rig ŌÉ v)) (cong f p)

-- The three discrete observables, presented as constant functions on K4Vertex.
d-vertex : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
d-vertex _ = degree-K4

őļ-vertex : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
őļ-vertex _ = őļ-discrete

őĪ-denom-vertex : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
őĪ-denom-vertex _ = simplex-denom-K4

-- Each constant scalar is trivially K4Perm-invariant.
d-rigid : VertexRigid d-vertex
d-rigid _ _ = refl

őļ-rigid : VertexRigid őļ-vertex
őļ-rigid _ _ = refl

őĪ-denom-rigid : VertexRigid őĪ-denom-vertex
őĪ-denom-rigid _ _ = refl

-- ¬ß Aut(K‚āĄ)-invariance record for the three discrete observables.
record AutK4Invariants : Set where
  field
    d-rigidity         : VertexRigid d-vertex
    őļ-rigidity         : VertexRigid őļ-vertex
    őĪ-denom-rigidity   : VertexRigid őĪ-denom-vertex
    d-collapse         : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí d-vertex v ‚Č° d-vertex w
    őļ-collapse         : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí őļ-vertex v ‚Č° őļ-vertex w
    őĪ-denom-collapse   : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí őĪ-denom-vertex v ‚Č° őĪ-denom-vertex w
    d-value            : degree-K4       ‚Č° 3
    őļ-value            : őļ-discrete      ‚Č° 8
    őĪ-denom-value      : simplex-denom-K4 ‚Č° 111

theorem-autk4-invariants : AutK4Invariants
theorem-autk4-invariants = record
  { d-rigidity       = d-rigid
  ; őļ-rigidity       = őļ-rigid
  ; őĪ-denom-rigidity = őĪ-denom-rigid
  ; d-collapse       = vertex-rigid-constant d-vertex       d-rigid
  ; őļ-collapse       = vertex-rigid-constant őļ-vertex       őļ-rigid
  ; őĪ-denom-collapse = vertex-rigid-constant őĪ-denom-vertex őĪ-denom-rigid
  ; d-value          = law16B-2-degree
  ; őļ-value          = law16B-8-kappa
  ; őĪ-denom-value    = law16B-10-simplex-denom
  }
\end{code}

The record packages the rigidity witnesses, the orbit-collapse theorems,
and the numerical identities for $d$, $\kappa$, and
$\alpha\text{-denom}$.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Edge- and face-rigidity}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:edge-face-rigid}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The same rigidity argument extends to edge-indexed and face-indexed quantities. Edge transitivity handles the former, and the identification of faces with opposite vertices reduces the latter to the vertex case.

The same argument extends to the higher simplices. An edge-indexed
scalar must respect both the transported edge action and the
identification of opposite orientations; under these hypotheses,
edge-transitivity forces it to be constant on all six edges. For faces
there is even less to prove, because \texttt{K4Triangle} is identified
with \texttt{K4Vertex} and the face action is simply the vertex action
under that identification. Rigidity therefore propagates across all
three simplicial dimensions of the tetrahedron.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Edge rigidity: action-invariance plus orientation-invariance.
EdgeRigid : (K4Edge ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí Set
EdgeRigid f =
  ((ŌÉ : K4Perm) (e : K4Edge) ‚Üí f (edge-act ŌÉ e) ‚Č° f e)
  √ó
  ((e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge) ‚Üí e‚āĀ ‚ČąE e‚āā ‚Üí f e‚āĀ ‚Č° f e‚āā)

-- Edge collapse: every edge-rigid scalar is constant on K4Edge.
edge-rigid-constant :
  (f : K4Edge ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí EdgeRigid f ‚Üí
  (e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge) ‚Üí f e‚āĀ ‚Č° f e‚āā
edge-rigid-constant f (rig , swp) e‚āĀ e‚āā with edge-transitive e‚āĀ e‚āā
... | ŌÉ , p = trans (sym (rig ŌÉ e‚āĀ)) (swp _ _ p)

-- ¬ß Triangle rigidity under triangle-map.
TriangleRigid : (K4Triangle ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí Set
TriangleRigid f = (ŌÉ : K4Perm) (t : K4Triangle) ‚Üí f (triangle-map ŌÉ t) ‚Č° f t

-- ¬ß Face collapse reuses vertex-rigid constancy.
triangle-rigid-constant :
  (f : K4Triangle ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí TriangleRigid f ‚Üí
  (t‚āĀ t‚āā : K4Triangle) ‚Üí f t‚āĀ ‚Č° f t‚āā
triangle-rigid-constant f rig t‚āĀ t‚āā = vertex-rigid-constant f rig t‚āĀ t‚āā

-- The simplicial-dimension closure record.
record SimplicialRigidity : Set where
  field
    vertex-collapse :
      (f : K4Vertex   ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí VertexRigid   f ‚Üí (v w : K4Vertex)   ‚Üí f v ‚Č° f w
    edge-collapse   :
      (f : K4Edge     ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí EdgeRigid     f ‚Üí (e‚āĀ e‚āā : K4Edge)   ‚Üí f e‚āĀ ‚Č° f e‚āā
    face-collapse   :
      (f : K4Triangle ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí TriangleRigid f ‚Üí (t‚āĀ t‚āā : K4Triangle) ‚Üí f t‚āĀ ‚Č° f t‚āā

theorem-simplicial-rigidity : SimplicialRigidity
theorem-simplicial-rigidity = record
  { vertex-collapse = vertex-rigid-constant
  ; edge-collapse   = edge-rigid-constant
  ; face-collapse   = triangle-rigid-constant
  }
\end{code}

The record certifies that Aut$(K_4)$-rigidity is uniform across the three simplicial dimensions. No dimension carries an extra parameter; the symmetry collapse is total.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{\texorpdfstring{The full Aut$(K_4)$ invariant ledger}{The full Aut(K4) invariant ledger}}
\label{sec:forced-edge-matching:full-ledger}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The remaining discrete quantities $V$, $E$, $\chi$, $F_2$, $\mathcal{C}$, and the hierarchy exponent are handled in the same way: each is represented as a constant scalar and then bundled into a single invariance record.

Nothing essentially new is required here. Each of these quantities is
represented by a constant scalar on \texttt{K4Vertex}, and so each is
vertex-rigid by definition. The record \texttt{AutK4FullLedger}
packages those rigidity witnesses together with the corresponding
collapse theorems and the already established numerical values. In this
way the ledger is sealed: every discrete constant used later in the
spectral evaluation has already been certified as an Aut$(K_4)$-invariant.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß The remaining six discrete observables as constant vertex scalars.
V-vertex      : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
V-vertex      _ = vertexCountK4

E-vertex      : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
E-vertex      _ = edgeCountK4

Ōá-vertex      : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
Ōá-vertex      _ = eulerChar-computed

F‚āā-vertex     : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
F‚āā-vertex     _ = F‚āā

C-vertex      : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
C-vertex      _ = simplex-eval

hier-vertex   : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
hier-vertex   _ = hierarchy-exponent

V-rigid     : VertexRigid V-vertex
V-rigid     _ _ = refl
E-rigid     : VertexRigid E-vertex
E-rigid     _ _ = refl
Ōá-rigid     : VertexRigid Ōá-vertex
Ōá-rigid     _ _ = refl
F‚āā-rigid    : VertexRigid F‚āā-vertex
F‚āā-rigid    _ _ = refl
C-rigid     : VertexRigid C-vertex
C-rigid     _ _ = refl
hier-rigid  : VertexRigid hier-vertex
hier-rigid  _ _ = refl

record AutK4FullLedger : Set where
  field
    -- Already-certified observables (Step 3)
    d-rigidity         : VertexRigid d-vertex
    őļ-rigidity         : VertexRigid őļ-vertex
    őĪ-denom-rigidity   : VertexRigid őĪ-denom-vertex
    -- Full-ledger additions
    V-rigidity         : VertexRigid V-vertex
    E-rigidity         : VertexRigid E-vertex
    Ōá-rigidity         : VertexRigid Ōá-vertex
    F‚āā-rigidity        : VertexRigid F‚āā-vertex
    C-rigidity         : VertexRigid C-vertex
    hier-rigidity      : VertexRigid hier-vertex
    -- Single-value collapse witnesses
    V-collapse         : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí V-vertex     v ‚Č° V-vertex     w
    E-collapse         : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí E-vertex     v ‚Č° E-vertex     w
    Ōá-collapse         : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí Ōá-vertex     v ‚Č° Ōá-vertex     w
    F‚āā-collapse        : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí F‚āā-vertex    v ‚Č° F‚āā-vertex    w
    C-collapse         : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí C-vertex     v ‚Č° C-vertex     w
    hier-collapse      : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí hier-vertex  v ‚Č° hier-vertex  w
    -- Forced numerical values
    V-value            : vertexCountK4      ‚Č° 4
    E-value            : edgeCountK4        ‚Č° 6
    Ōá-value            : eulerChar-computed ‚Č° 2
    F‚āā-value           : F‚āā                 ‚Č° 17
    C-value            : simplex-eval       ‚Č° 137
    hier-value         : hierarchy-exponent ‚Č° 22

theorem-autk4-full-ledger : AutK4FullLedger
theorem-autk4-full-ledger = record
  { d-rigidity       = d-rigid
  ; őļ-rigidity       = őļ-rigid
  ; őĪ-denom-rigidity = őĪ-denom-rigid
  ; V-rigidity       = V-rigid
  ; E-rigidity       = E-rigid
  ; Ōá-rigidity       = Ōá-rigid
  ; F‚āā-rigidity      = F‚āā-rigid
  ; C-rigidity       = C-rigid
  ; hier-rigidity    = hier-rigid
  ; V-collapse       = vertex-rigid-constant V-vertex     V-rigid
  ; E-collapse       = vertex-rigid-constant E-vertex     E-rigid
  ; Ōá-collapse       = vertex-rigid-constant Ōá-vertex     Ōá-rigid
  ; F‚āā-collapse      = vertex-rigid-constant F‚āā-vertex    F‚āā-rigid
  ; C-collapse       = vertex-rigid-constant C-vertex     C-rigid
  ; hier-collapse    = vertex-rigid-constant hier-vertex  hier-rigid
  ; V-value          = refl
  ; E-value          = law16B-0-edges
  ; Ōá-value          = law16B-3-euler
  ; F‚āā-value         = law16B-12-F2-is-17
  ; C-value          = law15A-0-simplex-eval-137
  ; hier-value       = law16B-9-hierarchy
  }
\end{code}

The ledger packages the rigidity witnesses and numerical identities for
all remaining discrete constants used later in the evaluation.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\section{The Universal Observable Verdict}
\label{sec:observable-universal}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

The next question is whether there is a simple criterion for deciding
when a vertex observable is admissible. The answer is that rigidity is
equivalent to constancy on the unique vertex orbit.

The corresponding general statement is binary. A function on
\texttt{K4Vertex} is rigid exactly when it is constant on the unique
vertex orbit; if it separates two vertices, then it cannot be invariant
under Aut$(K_4)$. The record \texttt{RigidIffConstant} packages these
two implications, while \texttt{separates-non-rigid} gives the
contrapositive in the form needed for later eliminations. The sample
observable \texttt{ladder-vertex} shows that non-rigid observables
genuinely exist, so the dichotomy is not vacuous.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Reverse of vertex-rigid-constant: constants are vacuously rigid.
constant-vertex-rigid :
  (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí
  ((v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí f v ‚Č° f w) ‚Üí
  VertexRigid f
constant-vertex-rigid f const ŌÉ v = const (k4to ŌÉ v) v

-- ¬ß The full equivalence: rigidity iff orbit-constancy.
record RigidIffConstant (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) : Set where
  field
    rigid‚Üíconstant : VertexRigid f ‚Üí (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí f v ‚Č° f w
    constant‚Üírigid : ((v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí f v ‚Č° f w) ‚Üí VertexRigid f

theorem-rigid-iff-constant :
  (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí RigidIffConstant f
theorem-rigid-iff-constant f = record
  { rigid‚Üíconstant = vertex-rigid-constant f
  ; constant‚Üírigid = constant-vertex-rigid f
  }

-- ¬ß Elimination form: a single separation refutes rigidity globally.
separates-non-rigid :
  (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí
  f v ‚Č† f w ‚Üí
  ¬¨ VertexRigid f
separates-non-rigid f v w sep rig =
  sep (vertex-rigid-constant f rig v w)

-- ¬ß Concrete refutation witness: a four-valued observable on K‚āĄ.
ladder-vertex : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
ladder-vertex v‚āÄ = 0
ladder-vertex v‚āĀ = 1
ladder-vertex v‚āā = 2
ladder-vertex v‚āÉ = 3

ladder-separates : ladder-vertex v‚āÄ ‚Č† ladder-vertex v‚āĀ
ladder-separates ()

ladder-not-rigid : ¬¨ VertexRigid ladder-vertex
ladder-not-rigid =
  separates-non-rigid ladder-vertex v‚āÄ v‚āĀ ladder-separates

-- ¬ß The universal observable verdict packaged.
record ObservableUniversal : Set‚āĀ where
  field
    rigid-iff-constant :
      (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) ‚Üí RigidIffConstant f
    separation-refutes :
      (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí
      f v ‚Č† f w ‚Üí ¬¨ VertexRigid f
    non-rigid-witness-exists :
      ő£ (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē) (őĽ f ‚Üí ¬¨ VertexRigid f)

theorem-observable-universal : ObservableUniversal
theorem-observable-universal = record
  { rigid-iff-constant   = theorem-rigid-iff-constant
  ; separation-refutes   = separates-non-rigid
  ; non-rigid-witness-exists = (ladder-vertex , ladder-not-rigid)
  }
\end{code}

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\section{The Dirac Operator: Squaring to the Laplacian}
\label{sec:dirac-square-laplacian}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

The next construction asks whether the Laplacian on $K_4$ can be
realized as the square of a first-order operator. On vertex functions
alone it cannot, but on the direct sum of vertex and edge functions one
can define a Dirac operator whose square is the Laplacian on each
layer.

The way out is to enlarge the carrier from vertex functions to the
direct sum of vertex and edge functions. On this space the exterior
derivative and its adjoint exchange the two layers, and the Dirac
operator built from them becomes a genuine first-order object. Its
square is then forced to act diagonally, as the vertex Laplacian on the
vertex part and the edge Laplacian on the edge part. The decisive point
is computational rather than metaphorical: the squaring theorems close
by \texttt{refl}, so $D^2 = \Delta$ is literally the normal form of the
construction.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß Six oriented edges of K‚āĄ (one chosen orientation per unordered edge).
data OrientedEdge : Set where
  oe01 oe02 oe03 oe12 oe13 oe23 : OrientedEdge

-- ¬ß Source and target maps for the chosen orientations.
oe-src : OrientedEdge ‚Üí K4Vertex
oe-src oe01 = v‚āÄ
oe-src oe02 = v‚āÄ
oe-src oe03 = v‚āÄ
oe-src oe12 = v‚āĀ
oe-src oe13 = v‚āĀ
oe-src oe23 = v‚āā

oe-tgt : OrientedEdge ‚Üí K4Vertex
oe-tgt oe01 = v‚āĀ
oe-tgt oe02 = v‚āā
oe-tgt oe03 = v‚āÉ
oe-tgt oe12 = v‚āā
oe-tgt oe13 = v‚āÉ
oe-tgt oe23 = v‚āÉ

-- ¬ß Integer subtraction.
infixl 6 _-‚Ą§_
_-‚Ą§_ : ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§ ‚Üí ‚Ą§
x -‚Ą§ y = x +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ y

-- ¬ß Vertex differential (coboundary): signed difference along each edge.
d-ve : (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (OrientedEdge ‚Üí ‚Ą§)
d-ve f oe01 = f v‚āĀ -‚Ą§ f v‚āÄ
d-ve f oe02 = f v‚āā -‚Ą§ f v‚āÄ
d-ve f oe03 = f v‚āÉ -‚Ą§ f v‚āÄ
d-ve f oe12 = f v‚āā -‚Ą§ f v‚āĀ
d-ve f oe13 = f v‚āÉ -‚Ą§ f v‚āĀ
d-ve f oe23 = f v‚āÉ -‚Ą§ f v‚āā

-- ¬ß Edge codifferential (adjoint): incoming minus outgoing at each vertex.
d-star : (OrientedEdge ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§)
d-star g v‚āÄ = (neg‚Ą§ (g oe01) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (g oe02)) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (g oe03)
d-star g v‚āĀ = (g oe01 +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (g oe12)) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (g oe13)
d-star g v‚āā = (g oe02 +‚Ą§ g oe12) +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (g oe23)
d-star g v‚āÉ = (g oe03 +‚Ą§ g oe13) +‚Ą§ g oe23

-- ¬ß The Dirac state space: vertex functions ‚äē edge functions.
DiracState : Set
DiracState = (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§) √ó (OrientedEdge ‚Üí ‚Ą§)

-- ¬ß The Dirac operator: exchange layers via d-ve and d-star.
dirac : DiracState ‚Üí DiracState
dirac ŌÜ = (d-star (snd ŌÜ) , d-ve (fst ŌÜ))

-- ¬ß Square of the Dirac operator: Laplacian on each layer.
dirac¬≤ : DiracState ‚Üí DiracState
dirac¬≤ ŌÜ = dirac (dirac ŌÜ)

-- ¬ß Vertex Laplacian as d-star ‚ąė d-ve.
K4Lap-vert : (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§)
K4Lap-vert f = d-star (d-ve f)

-- ¬ß Edge Laplacian as d-ve ‚ąė d-star, dually.
K4Lap-edge : (OrientedEdge ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí (OrientedEdge ‚Üí ‚Ą§)
K4Lap-edge g = d-ve (d-star g)

-- ¬ß The squaring theorems: dirac¬≤ acts as the Laplacian on each layer.
theorem-dirac-squared-vertex :
  (ŌÜ : DiracState) (v : K4Vertex) ‚Üí
  fst (dirac¬≤ ŌÜ) v ‚Č° K4Lap-vert (fst ŌÜ) v
theorem-dirac-squared-vertex ŌÜ v‚āÄ = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-vertex ŌÜ v‚āĀ = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-vertex ŌÜ v‚āā = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-vertex ŌÜ v‚āÉ = refl

theorem-dirac-squared-edge :
  (ŌÜ : DiracState) (e : OrientedEdge) ‚Üí
  snd (dirac¬≤ ŌÜ) e ‚Č° K4Lap-edge (snd ŌÜ) e
theorem-dirac-squared-edge ŌÜ oe01 = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-edge ŌÜ oe02 = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-edge ŌÜ oe03 = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-edge ŌÜ oe12 = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-edge ŌÜ oe13 = refl
theorem-dirac-squared-edge ŌÜ oe23 = refl

-- ¬ß Algebraic identity: negation distributes over subtraction.
neg-sub : (a b : ‚Ą§) ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ (a -‚Ą§ b) ‚Č° b -‚Ą§ a
neg-sub a b =
  trans (neg-+‚Ą§ a (neg‚Ą§ b))
        (trans (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí neg‚Ą§ a +‚Ą§ t) (neg‚Ą§-involutive b))
               (+‚Ą§-comm (neg‚Ą§ a) b))

-- ¬ß Physical reading at v‚āÄ: K4Lap-vert f v‚āÄ = sum of (f v‚āÄ - f neighbor).
lap-physical-v‚āÄ :
  (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí
  K4Lap-vert f v‚āÄ ‚Č°
    ((f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āĀ) +‚Ą§ (f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āā)) +‚Ą§ (f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āÉ)
lap-physical-v‚āÄ f =
  trans
    (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí t +‚Ą§ neg‚Ą§ (f v‚āÉ -‚Ą§ f v‚āÄ))
      (cong‚āā _+‚Ą§_ (neg-sub (f v‚āĀ) (f v‚āÄ))
                  (neg-sub (f v‚āā) (f v‚āÄ))))
    (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí ((f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āĀ) +‚Ą§ (f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āā)) +‚Ą§ t)
          (neg-sub (f v‚āÉ) (f v‚āÄ)))

-- ¬ß The DiracOperator record bundles construction and squaring witness.
record DiracOperator : Set‚āĀ where
  field
    op                : DiracState ‚Üí DiracState
    op-squares-to-lap-v :
      (ŌÜ : DiracState) (v : K4Vertex) ‚Üí
      fst (op (op ŌÜ)) v ‚Č° K4Lap-vert (fst ŌÜ) v
    op-squares-to-lap-e :
      (ŌÜ : DiracState) (e : OrientedEdge) ‚Üí
      snd (op (op ŌÜ)) e ‚Č° K4Lap-edge (snd ŌÜ) e
    physical-vertex-form :
      (f : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ą§) ‚Üí
      K4Lap-vert f v‚āÄ ‚Č°
        ((f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āĀ) +‚Ą§ (f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āā)) +‚Ą§ (f v‚āÄ -‚Ą§ f v‚āÉ)

theorem-dirac-operator : DiracOperator
theorem-dirac-operator = record
  { op                  = dirac
  ; op-squares-to-lap-v = theorem-dirac-squared-vertex
  ; op-squares-to-lap-e = theorem-dirac-squared-edge
  ; physical-vertex-form = lap-physical-v‚āÄ
  }
\end{code}

The record packages the operator, the two squaring identities, and the explicit vertex formula. In this sense the relation $D^2 = \Delta$ is part of the proved structure itself.

%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź
\chapter{Scale Closure}
\label{ch:scale-closure}
%‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź

With $\mathit{edge\text{-}weight} = 1$, $\mathit{face\text{-}weight} = 1$, and $\mathit{vertex\text{-}weight} = 1$, no additional scale parameter remains. Any ratio built from these weights is therefore fixed, and the native FD scale reduces to the unit triple $(1,1,1)$.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Simplicial scale units}
\label{sec:scale-closure:natural-units}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The three simplicial scale units are:
\[
S_1 = \sqrt{\frac{w_2\, w_0}{w_1^3}}, \qquad
S_2 = \sqrt{\frac{w_2\, w_0}{w_1^5}}, \qquad
S_0 = \sqrt{\frac{w_2\, w_1}{w_0}},
\]
where $w_1$, $w_2$, $w_0$ denote the forced edge, face, and vertex weights respectively.
With all three weights equal to~$1$, every numerator and denominator is~$1$, so $S_1 = S_2 = S_0 = 1$ in FD units.

These expressions depend only on the previously forced weights. After
substituting $w_1 = w_2 = w_0 = 1$, each unit evaluates to $1$, so the
three simplicial dimensions share the same native unit.

\begin{code}
scale-dim1 : ‚Ąē
scale-dim1 = (face-weight * vertex-weight) * 1

scale-dim2 : ‚Ąē
scale-dim2 = (face-weight * vertex-weight) * 1

scale-dim0 : ‚Ąē
scale-dim0 = (face-weight * edge-weight) * 1

scale-dim1-is-1 : scale-dim1 ‚Č° 1
scale-dim1-is-1 = refl

scale-dim2-is-1 : scale-dim2 ‚Č° 1
scale-dim2-is-1 = refl

scale-dim0-is-1 : scale-dim0 ‚Č° 1
scale-dim0-is-1 = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{The closure record}
\label{sec:scale-closure:record}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The record \texttt{ScaleClosure} gathers the three weight identities
and the three unit identities into a single witness.

\begin{code}
record ScaleClosure : Set where
  field
    edge-forced    : edge-weight ‚Č° 1
    face-forced : face-weight ‚Č° 1
    vertex-forced    : vertex-weight ‚Č° 1
    dim1-1      : scale-dim1 ‚Č° 1
    dim2-1      : scale-dim2 ‚Č° 1
    dim0-1      : scale-dim0 ‚Č° 1

theorem-scale-closure : ScaleClosure
theorem-scale-closure = record
  { edge-forced    = refl
  ; face-forced = refl
  ; vertex-forced    = refl
  ; dim1-1      = refl
  ; dim2-1      = refl
  ; dim0-1      = refl
  }
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{No free scale}
\label{sec:scale-closure:no-free-scale}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The number of forced constants matches the number of simplicial
dimensions. In the arithmetic encoded below, this leaves no further
independent scale parameter.

\begin{code}
independent-constants : ‚Ąē
independent-constants = 3

independent-dimensions : ‚Ąē
independent-dimensions = 3

free-parameters : ‚Ąē
free-parameters = independent-dimensions ‚ął independent-constants

no-free-scale : free-parameters ‚Č° 0
no-free-scale = refl
\end{code}

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Normalized scale elimination}
\label{sec:scale-closure:normalized-scale-elimination}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

In normalized units one may ask whether any positive triple $(a,b,c)$
besides $(1,1,1)$ can satisfy the bridge equations. A scale choice is
then just a triple of natural-number assignments for the three
simplicial dimensions, and the bridge equations become
\[
b = a,
\qquad
b = c a^2,
\qquad
cb^2 = a^3.
\]
The proof is a case split on the first coordinate. Positivity excludes
$a=0$, the arithmetic lemmas exclude $a \ge 2$, and once $a=1$ the
bridge equations force $b=1$ and $c=1$ as well.

\begin{code}
IsScaleNonZero : ‚Ąē ‚Üí Set
IsScaleNonZero n = n ‚Č° 0 ‚Üí ‚ä•

record NormalizedScaleChoice : Set where
  field
    step-dim1      : ‚Ąē
    step-dim2      : ‚Ąē
    step-dim0      : ‚Ąē
    dim1-nonzero   : IsScaleNonZero step-dim1
    edge-lock      : step-dim2 ‚Č° step-dim1
    face-lock      : step-dim2 ‚Č° (step-dim0 *‚Ąē step-dim1) *‚Ąē step-dim1
    vertex-lock    : (step-dim0 *‚Ąē step-dim2) *‚Ąē step-dim2 ‚Č°
                     (step-dim1 *‚Ąē step-dim1) *‚Ąē step-dim1
\end{code}

Two small arithmetic lemmas do the work. First: if adding a tail leaves a natural unchanged, that tail must have been zero. Second: for any value $a \ge 2$, neither $a^2 = a$ nor $a^3 = a$ can hold.

\begin{code}
natPlusRightLe : (a : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí a ‚Č§ (a +‚Ąē b)
natPlusRightLe zero b = z‚Č§n
natPlusRightLe (suc a) b = s‚Č§s (natPlusRightLe a b)

natPlusCancelSelf : (a : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (b : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí a +‚Ąē b ‚Č° a ‚Üí b ‚Č° 0
natPlusCancelSelf a b eq with ‚Č§-+‚Ąē-cancelň° a b 0
  (‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ a) (sym eq) (‚Č§-refl a)) (sym (+‚Ąē-zero-right a)))
natPlusCancelSelf a zero eq | z‚Č§n = refl
natPlusCancelSelf a (suc b) eq | ()

squareNotSelf : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí ((suc (suc n) *‚Ąē suc (suc n)) ‚Č° suc (suc n)) ‚Üí ‚ä•
squareNotSelf n eq =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    expand : a *‚Ąē a ‚Č° a +‚Ąē (a *‚Ąē suc n)
    expand = *‚Ąē-suc-right-+‚Ąē a (suc n)

    tail0 : (a *‚Ąē suc n) ‚Č° 0
    tail0 = natPlusCancelSelf a (a *‚Ąē suc n) (trans (sym expand) eq)

    bad : suc n ‚Č° 0
    bad = *‚Ąē-pos-zero‚Üízero (suc n) (suc n) tail0
  in
  suc‚Č†0 bad
  where
    suc‚Č†0 : {k : ‚Ąē} ‚Üí suc k ‚Č° 0 ‚Üí ‚ä•
    suc‚Č†0 ()

squareDominates : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí suc (suc n) ‚Č§ (suc (suc n) *‚Ąē suc (suc n))
squareDominates n =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    base : suc 0 ‚Č§ a
    base = s‚Č§s z‚Č§n

    step : (suc 0 *‚Ąē a) ‚Č§ (a *‚Ąē a)
    step = ‚Č§-*‚Ąē-mono ≥ base a
  in
  subst (őĽ t ‚Üí t ‚Č§ (a *‚Ąē a)) (*‚Ąē-one-left a) step

cubeNotSelf : (n : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí (((suc (suc n) *‚Ąē suc (suc n)) *‚Ąē suc (suc n)) ‚Č° suc (suc n)) ‚Üí ‚ä•
cubeNotSelf n eq =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    sq : ‚Ąē
    sq = a *‚Ąē a

    expand : sq *‚Ąē a ‚Č° sq +‚Ąē (sq *‚Ąē suc n)
    expand = *‚Ąē-suc-right-+‚Ąē sq (suc n)

    sq‚Č§a : sq ‚Č§ a
    sq‚Č§a = ‚Č§-resp-‚Č° ≥ (natPlusRightLe sq (sq *‚Ąē suc n)) (trans (sym expand) eq)

    a‚Č§sq : a ‚Č§ sq
    a‚Č§sq = squareDominates n

    sq‚Č°a : sq ‚Č° a
    sq‚Č°a = ‚Č§-antisym sq‚Č§a a‚Č§sq
  in
  squareNotSelf n sq‚Č°a

dim0-unit-forced : (c : ‚Ąē) ‚Üí ((c *‚Ąē 1) *‚Ąē 1) ‚Č° 1 ‚Üí c ‚Č° 1
dim0-unit-forced zero ()
dim0-unit-forced (suc zero) refl = refl
dim0-unit-forced (suc (suc n)) ()
\end{code}

The elimination itself is direct. The three bridge equations collapse to $a^3 = a$, and that excludes every $a \ge 2$. Positivity excludes $a = 0$. So $a = 1$, hence also $b = 1$ and $c = 1$.

\begin{code}
normalized-dim1-is-1 : (S : NormalizedScaleChoice) ‚Üí NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim1 S ‚Č° 1
normalized-dim1-is-1 S
  with NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim1 S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim2 S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim0 S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.dim1-nonzero S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.edge-lock S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.face-lock S
     | NormalizedScaleChoice.vertex-lock S
... | zero          | _ | _ | nz | _  | _  | _  = ‚ä•-elim (nz refl)
... | suc zero      | _ | _ | _  | _  | _  | _  = refl
... | suc (suc n)   | b | c | _  | cl | hl | gl =
  let
    a : ‚Ąē
    a = suc (suc n)

    G-at-a : (c *‚Ąē a) *‚Ąē a ‚Č° (a *‚Ąē a) *‚Ąē a
    G-at-a = trans (sym (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí (c *‚Ąē t) *‚Ąē t) cl)) gl

    cubeEq : ((a *‚Ąē a) *‚Ąē a) ‚Č° a
    cubeEq = trans (sym G-at-a) (trans (sym hl) cl)
  in
  ‚ä•-elim (cubeNotSelf n cubeEq)

normalized-dim2-is-1 : (S : NormalizedScaleChoice) ‚Üí NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim2 S ‚Č° 1
normalized-dim2-is-1 S = trans (NormalizedScaleChoice.edge-lock S) (normalized-dim1-is-1 S)

normalized-dim0-is-1 : (S : NormalizedScaleChoice) ‚Üí NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim0 S ‚Č° 1
normalized-dim0-is-1 S =
  dim0-unit-forced
    (NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim0 S)
    (trans
      (cong (őĽ t ‚Üí (NormalizedScaleChoice.step-dim0 S *‚Ąē t) *‚Ąē t)
            (sym (normalized-dim1-is-1 S)))
      (trans
        (sym (NormalizedScaleChoice.face-lock S))
        (normalized-dim2-is-1 S)))

normalized-scale-witness : NormalizedScaleChoice
normalized-scale-witness = record
  { step-dim1      = 1
  ; step-dim2      = 1
  ; step-dim0      = 1
  ; dim1-nonzero   = őĽ ()
  ; edge-lock      = refl
  ; face-lock      = refl
  ; vertex-lock    = refl
  }
\end{code}

This is the normalized form of the scale-closure statement. In the
regime $w_1 = w_2 = w_0 = 1$, the only admissible positive scale triple
is $(1,1,1)$.

%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ
\section{Distinction as measurement kernel}
\label{sec:scale-closure:measurement-kernel}
%‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ

The next question concerns the minimal structure required for
measurement. At the level needed here, a measurement scheme must at
least distinguish two outcomes.

A minimal binary scheme therefore consists of an outcome type, two
distinct outcomes, and an exhaustive yes/no classification of that
type.

The record \texttt{BinaryMeasurement} isolates exactly these data. The
map \texttt{measurement-outcome-distinction} repackages them as a
\texttt{Distinction}, so the distinction kernel is recovered directly
from the measurement structure.

\begin{code}
record BinaryMeasurement : Set1 where
  field
    State   : Set
    Outcome : Set
    measure : State ‚Üí Outcome
    yes     : Outcome
    no      : Outcome
    yes‚Č†no  : yes ‚Č† no
    total   : (y : Outcome) ‚Üí (y ‚Č° yes) ‚äé (y ‚Č° no)

open BinaryMeasurement public

measurement-outcome-distinction : (M : BinaryMeasurement) ‚Üí Distinction
measurement-outcome-distinction M =
  fromDistinctCovered
    (yes M)
    (no M)
    (yes‚Č†no M)
    (total M)

measurement-outcome-nontrivial : (M : BinaryMeasurement) ‚Üí NonTrivial (Outcome M)
measurement-outcome-nontrivial M =
  law0-5-distinct-pair-forces-nontrivial
    (yes M , (no M , yes‚Č†no M))

measurement-has-distinct-outcomes : (M : BinaryMeasurement) ‚Üí HasDistinctPair (Outcome M)
measurement-has-distinct-outcomes M =
  yes M , (no M , yes‚Č†no M)
\end{code}

These lemmas establish only a threshold result: any structure encoded by
\texttt{BinaryMeasurement} has distinct outcomes and therefore supports
a distinction in its outcome space.

This provides a second route to the distinction package. One route
begins with the type-theoretic construction itself; the present one
begins with binary measurement data and extracts the same minimal
structure.

For the later continuum questions, only this consequence matters: once
measurement supplies a distinction kernel, the earlier discrete chain
may be reused without additional assumptions.

\phantomsection\label{sec:loop-classification}
\phantomsection\label{sec:handshaking}
\subsubsection*{The loop classification}

The invariant ledger permits a finite loop classification. Using
$E = 6$, $d = 3$, and $\chi = 2$, one obtains eight subset-sum
candidates for a loop numerator.

The relevant denominators are the bulk value $72 = VEd$ and its
extended version $576 = \kappa VEd$. A candidate survives only if it is
irreducible against these denominators and complete with respect to the
required prime strata.

The computation is finite. Six candidates fail irreducibility with
$72$, the remaining partial candidate $d+\chi = 5$ fails the
completeness test, and the only survivor is $E + d + \chi = 11$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß¬ß Loop Classification ‚ÄĒ the unique admissible loop numerator.

-- ¬ß The universal loop numerator: sum of all primitive loop parameters.
loop-numerator : ‚Ąē
loop-numerator = edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed

-- ¬ß Law: loop numerator is exactly 11.
law-loop-num-11 : loop-numerator ‚Č° 11
law-loop-num-11 = refl

-- ¬ß Decomposition: the three structural contributions.
law-loop-num-decomposition : loop-numerator ‚Č° 6 + 3 + 2
law-loop-num-decomposition = refl

-- ¬ß The handshaking identity: Ōá ¬∑ d = E.
law-chi-times-d-is-E : eulerChar-computed * degree-K4 ‚Č° edgeCountK4
law-chi-times-d-is-E = refl

-- ¬ß Denominator at the bulk scale: V ¬∑ E ¬∑ d = 72.
loop-denom-bulk : ‚Ąē
loop-denom-bulk = vertexCountK4 * edgeCountK4 * degree-K4

-- ¬ß Denominator at the extended scale: őļ ¬∑ 72 = 576.
loop-denom-extended : ‚Ąē
loop-denom-extended = loop-denom-bulk * őļ-discrete

-- ¬ß The eight candidates and their values:
loop-cand-000 : ‚Ąē           -- empty set
loop-cand-000 = 0
loop-cand-001 : ‚Ąē           -- Ōá only
loop-cand-001 = eulerChar-computed
loop-cand-010 : ‚Ąē           -- d only
loop-cand-010 = degree-K4
loop-cand-011 : ‚Ąē           -- d + Ōá
loop-cand-011 = degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed
loop-cand-100 : ‚Ąē           -- E only
loop-cand-100 = edgeCountK4
loop-cand-101 : ‚Ąē           -- E + Ōá
loop-cand-101 = edgeCountK4 + eulerChar-computed
loop-cand-110 : ‚Ąē           -- E + d
loop-cand-110 = edgeCountK4 + degree-K4
loop-cand-111 : ‚Ąē           -- E + d + Ōá
loop-cand-111 = edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed

-- ¬ß Values
law-cand-000 : loop-cand-000 ‚Č° 0
law-cand-000 = refl
law-cand-001 : loop-cand-001 ‚Č° 2
law-cand-001 = refl
law-cand-010 : loop-cand-010 ‚Č° 3
law-cand-010 = refl
law-cand-011 : loop-cand-011 ‚Č° 5
law-cand-011 = refl
law-cand-100 : loop-cand-100 ‚Č° 6
law-cand-100 = refl
law-cand-101 : loop-cand-101 ‚Č° 8
law-cand-101 = refl
law-cand-110 : loop-cand-110 ‚Č° 9
law-cand-110 = refl
law-cand-111 : loop-cand-111 ‚Č° 11
law-cand-111 = refl

-- ¬ß Filter 1: irreducibility with 72 eliminates 0, 2, 3, 6, 8, and 9.
law-elim-000 : gcd loop-cand-001 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 2   -- Ōá=2 shares factor 2
law-elim-000 = refl
law-elim-010 : gcd loop-cand-010 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 3   -- d=3 shares factor 3
law-elim-010 = refl
law-elim-100 : gcd loop-cand-100 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 6   -- E=6 shares factor 6
law-elim-100 = refl
law-elim-101 : gcd loop-cand-101 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 8   -- E+Ōá=8 shares factor 8
law-elim-101 = refl
law-elim-110 : gcd loop-cand-110 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 9   -- E+d=9 shares factor 9
law-elim-110 = refl

-- ¬ß Pass irreducibility: d+Ōá=5 and E+d+Ōá=11
law-pass-011 : gcd loop-cand-011 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1   -- d+Ōá=5 coprime ‚úď
law-pass-011 = refl
law-pass-111 : gcd loop-cand-111 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1   -- E+d+Ōá=11 coprime ‚úď
law-pass-111 = refl

-- ¬ß Filter 2: d+Ōá=5 omits the edge stratum.
law-partial-omits-E : loop-cand-011 + edgeCountK4 ‚Č° loop-cand-111
law-partial-omits-E = refl

law-full-is-partial-plus-E : loop-cand-111 ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + loop-cand-011
law-full-is-partial-plus-E = refl

-- ¬ß Structural independence: E is multiplicatively independent of d+Ōá.
law-E-structurally-independent :
  gcd edgeCountK4 (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) ‚Č° 1
law-E-structurally-independent = refl  -- gcd(6, 5) = 1

-- ¬ß E = 6 spans the full {2,3} prime base inside 72 = 2¬≥¬∑3¬≤.
law-E-spans-full-base : gcd edgeCountK4 (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 6
law-E-spans-full-base = refl  -- gcd(6, 6) = 6

-- ¬ß d = 3 contributes only the 3-stratum.
law-d-in-3-stratum : gcd degree-K4 (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 3
law-d-in-3-stratum = refl  -- gcd(3, 6) = 3

-- ¬ß Ōá = 2 contributes only the 2-stratum.
law-chi-in-2-stratum : gcd eulerChar-computed (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 2
law-chi-in-2-stratum = refl  -- gcd(2, 6) = 2

-- ¬ß d+Ōá = 5 escapes the {2,3} stratum completely.
law-dchi-escapes-stratum :
  gcd (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 1
law-dchi-escapes-stratum = refl  -- gcd(5, 6) = 1

-- ¬ß Completeness derivation from Ōá¬∑d = E and the volume structure.
record CompletenessDerivation : Set where
  field
    relation            : eulerChar-computed * degree-K4 ‚Č° edgeCountK4
    generators-coprime  : gcd eulerChar-computed degree-K4 ‚Č° 1
    volume-via-relation : (vertexCountK4 * eulerChar-computed) * (degree-K4 * degree-K4) ‚Č° loop-denom-bulk
    sum-product-coprime : gcd (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) (eulerChar-computed * degree-K4) ‚Č° 1
    partial-blind-to-E  : gcd loop-cand-011 edgeCountK4 ‚Č° 1
    full-witnesses-E    : loop-cand-111 ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + loop-cand-011
    full-irreducible    : gcd loop-cand-111 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1

theorem-completeness-derivation : CompletenessDerivation
theorem-completeness-derivation = record
  { relation            = refl
  ; generators-coprime  = refl
  ; volume-via-relation = refl
  ; sum-product-coprime = refl
  ; partial-blind-to-E  = refl
  ; full-witnesses-E    = refl
  ; full-irreducible    = refl
  }

-- ¬ß Filter 3: cross-scale consistency at extended scale.
law-partial-ext  : gcd loop-cand-011 loop-denom-extended ‚Č° 1   -- 5 coprime to 576 too
law-partial-ext  = refl
law-pass-111-ext : gcd loop-cand-111 loop-denom-extended ‚Č° 1   -- 11 coprime to 576 ‚úď
law-pass-111-ext = refl

-- ¬ß Loop classification record: unique admissible loop numerator.
record LoopClassification : Set where
  field
    forced-value        : loop-numerator ‚Č° 11
    forced-from-K4      : loop-numerator ‚Č° edgeCountK4 + degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed
    irreducible-bulk     : gcd loop-numerator loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1
    irreducible-ext      : gcd loop-numerator loop-denom-extended ‚Č° 1
    elim-chi            : gcd eulerChar-computed loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 2
    elim-d              : gcd degree-K4 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 3
    elim-E              : gcd edgeCountK4 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 6
    elim-E-chi          : gcd (edgeCountK4 + eulerChar-computed) loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 8
    elim-E-d            : gcd (edgeCountK4 + degree-K4) loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 9
    partial-coprime     : gcd (degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed) loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1
    partial-incomplete  : degree-K4 + eulerChar-computed ‚Č° 5
    partial-omits-E     : loop-cand-011 + edgeCountK4 ‚Č° loop-cand-111
    partial-also-ext    : gcd loop-cand-011 loop-denom-extended ‚Č° 1

theorem-loop-classification : LoopClassification
theorem-loop-classification = record
  { forced-value        = refl
  ; forced-from-K4      = refl
  ; irreducible-bulk     = refl
  ; irreducible-ext      = refl
  ; elim-chi            = refl
  ; elim-d              = refl
  ; elim-E              = refl
  ; elim-E-chi          = refl
  ; elim-E-d            = refl
  ; partial-coprime     = refl
  ; partial-incomplete  = refl
  ; partial-omits-E     = refl
  ; partial-also-ext     = refl
  }
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Loop basis forcing}

The previous classification uses the basis $\{E,d,\chi\}$. This
subsection explains why that basis is determined by the prime support
of $72 = 2^3 \cdot 3^2$.

The reason is arithmetic. $F_2 = 17$ lies outside the bulk prime
support, while $V$, $F$, and $\kappa$ remain in the $2$-stratum
generated by $\chi$. The non-redundant basis that still reaches both
prime strata is therefore $\{E,d,\chi\}$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß¬ß Loop Basis Forcing ‚ÄĒ the basis {E, d, Ōá} covers the bulk prime strata.

-- ¬ß F‚āā = 17 escapes the bulk stratum entirely (coprime to 72).
law-F‚āā-escapes-stratum : gcd F‚āā loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1
law-F‚āā-escapes-stratum = refl

-- ¬ß V, F, őļ are multiplicative powers of Ōá inside the 2-stratum.
law-V-is-chi-squared : vertexCountK4 ‚Č° eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed
law-V-is-chi-squared = refl

law-F-is-chi-squared : faceCountK4 ‚Č° eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed
law-F-is-chi-squared = refl

law-őļ-is-chi-cubed : őļ-discrete ‚Č° eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed
law-őļ-is-chi-cubed = refl

-- ¬ß Ōá is the minimal 2-stratum K‚āĄ invariant.
law-chi-‚Č§-V : eulerChar-computed ‚Č§ vertexCountK4
law-chi-‚Č§-V = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)

law-chi-‚Č§-F : eulerChar-computed ‚Č§ faceCountK4
law-chi-‚Č§-F = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)

law-chi-‚Č§-őļ : eulerChar-computed ‚Č§ őļ-discrete
law-chi-‚Č§-őļ = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)

-- ¬ß The bulk denominator equals 72 (refl through K‚āĄ definitions).
law-bulk-denom-72 : loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 72
law-bulk-denom-72 = refl

-- ¬ß Loop basis forcing record: {E, d, Ōá} is the unique minimal basis.
record LoopBasisForcing : Set where
  field
    -- Stratum escape: F‚āā has no role in a {2,3}-coverage argument.
    F‚āā-escapes-stratum   : gcd F‚āā loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 1
    -- Multiplicative redundancy: V, F, őļ are Ōá-powers in the 2-stratum.
    V-is-chi-squared     : vertexCountK4 ‚Č° eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed
    F-is-chi-squared     : faceCountK4 ‚Č° eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed
    őļ-is-chi-cubed       : őļ-discrete ‚Č° eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed * eulerChar-computed
    -- Ōá is the minimum of the 2-stratum cluster.
    chi-‚Č§-V              : eulerChar-computed ‚Č§ vertexCountK4
    chi-‚Č§-F              : eulerChar-computed ‚Č§ faceCountK4
    chi-‚Č§-őļ              : eulerChar-computed ‚Č§ őļ-discrete
    -- Stratum coverage by the surviving basis.
    chi-covers-2-stratum : gcd eulerChar-computed loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 2
    d-covers-3-stratum   : gcd degree-K4 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 3
    E-spans-both-strata  : gcd edgeCountK4 loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 6
    -- Handshake: E factors as Ōá¬∑d, locking the basis to three elements.
    handshake            : eulerChar-computed * degree-K4 ‚Č° edgeCountK4
    -- Bulk denominator value.
    bulk-denom-72        : loop-denom-bulk ‚Č° 72

theorem-loop-basis-forcing : LoopBasisForcing
theorem-loop-basis-forcing = record
  { F‚āā-escapes-stratum   = refl
  ; V-is-chi-squared     = refl
  ; F-is-chi-squared     = refl
  ; őļ-is-chi-cubed       = refl
  ; chi-‚Č§-V              = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
  ; chi-‚Č§-F              = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
  ; chi-‚Č§-őļ              = s‚Č§s (s‚Č§s z‚Č§n)
  ; chi-covers-2-stratum = refl
  ; d-covers-3-stratum   = refl
  ; E-spans-both-strata  = refl
  ; handshake            = refl
  ; bulk-denom-72        = refl
  }
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{Aut$(K_4)$-rigidity of the loop numerator}{Aut(K4)-rigidity of the loop numerator}}

The loop numerator $p = 11$ can be treated exactly like the earlier
ledger constants. As a constant scalar on vertices it is rigid under
every automorphism and hence constant on the unique vertex orbit.

The record \texttt{LoopNumeratorRigidity} packages this rigidity
statement together with the value $p = 11$.

\begin{code}
-- ¬ß¬ß Aut(K‚āĄ)-rigidity of the loop numerator p = 11.

p-vertex : K4Vertex ‚Üí ‚Ąē
p-vertex _ = loop-numerator

p-rigid : VertexRigid p-vertex
p-rigid _ _ = refl

record LoopNumeratorRigidity : Set where
  field
    p-rigidity   : VertexRigid p-vertex
    p-collapse   : (v w : K4Vertex) ‚Üí p-vertex v ‚Č° p-vertex w
    p-value      : loop-numerator ‚Č° 11

theorem-loop-numerator-rigidity : LoopNumeratorRigidity
theorem-loop-numerator-rigidity = record
  { p-rigidity = p-rigid
  ; p-collapse = vertex-rigid-constant p-vertex p-rigid
  ; p-value    = law-loop-num-11
  }
\end{code}

\subsubsection*{Exponent forcing}

The invariant ledger also fixes an exponent gap. If the bulk exponent
